questions.specification:
A ball of mass $m$ is released from rest at a height $H$ above the ground. It slides down a smooth curved surface and rises up to a height $h$ on the opposite side.

Assume there is no friction except at the bottom of the curve where some mechanical energy is lost.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/IwI8o3jPLu5dLcrf2Llg1.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.1 Conservation of energy

parts.content: State the principle of conservation of mechanical energy for a frictionless system.
parts.markscheme:
- State that in a frictionless system, the total mechanical energy (kinetic + potential) remains constant <marks>A1</marks>
- Provided no external non-conservative forces (e.g., friction) do work on the system <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define total mechanical energy
steps.content:
To answer this question using the sum of energies approach, we first need to define what mechanical energy is. In **Topic A.3 (Work, energy and power)**, we learn that the total mechanical energy of a system is the sum of its kinetic energy ($E_k$) and its potential energy ($E_p$).

From the data booklet, these are given by:
$$\begin{aligned} E_{k} &= \frac{1}{2}mv^{2} \\ \Delta E_{p} &= mg\Delta h \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the conservation principle
steps.content:
The principle of conservation of mechanical energy states that the **total mechanical energy** of an isolated system remains **constant** over time. 

Mathematically, this means the sum of kinetic and potential energy at any point in the motion is equal to the sum at any other point:
$$E_{total} = E_k + E_p = \text{constant}$$

steps.title: Identify the required conditions
steps.content:
For this principle to hold true, there must be **no work done by external non-conservative forces**. 

In the context of this problem, this means that forces like friction or air resistance must be absent (or their work must be zero). If friction were present, it would transform some of that mechanical energy into thermal energy, and the sum ($E_k + E_p$) would decrease.

highlights.text: State that in a frictionless system, the total mechanical energy (kinetic + potential) remains constant
Provided no external non-conservative forces (e.g., friction) do work on the system

steps.title: Finalize the statement
steps.content:
To earn both marks, your statement should combine these two parts clearly:

1. The total mechanical energy (the sum of kinetic and potential energy) remains constant.
2. This applies provided that no work is done by non-conservative forces, such as friction.

steps.title: Define mechanical energy
steps.content:
To understand conservation, we first need to define total mechanical energy. In the context of Topic A.3 (Work, energy and power), total mechanical energy is the sum of an object's kinetic energy ($E_k$) and its gravitational potential energy ($E_p$).

From the data booklet, we identify these as:

$$E_{k}=\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$$

$$\Delta E_{p}=mg\Delta h$$

steps.title: The Energy Transformation Principle
steps.content:
The **Energy Transformation Principle** states that in a closed system, energy is continuously converted from one form to another. 

For the ball sliding down the surface, any loss in gravitational potential energy (as it loses height) is exactly balanced by a gain in kinetic energy (as it speeds up), and vice versa as it climbs the other side. Mathematically, this means:

$$\Delta E_p + \Delta E_k = 0$$

steps.title: State the conservation principle
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must state that the total mechanical energy (the sum of $E_k$ and $E_p$) remains constant over time. 

However, this only holds true if there are no external non-conservative forces, such as friction or air resistance, doing work on the system to dissipate energy into other forms like heat.

highlights.text: State the principle of conservation of mechanical energy for a frictionless system.

steps.title: Final Answer Formulation
steps.content:
Based on the IB markscheme, your complete statement should be:

1. The total mechanical energy (sum of kinetic and potential energy) of a system remains constant.
2. This occurs provided that no external non-conservative forces (like friction) do work on the system.

parts.content: Outline why the ball does not reach the original height $H$ on the opposite side.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize mechanical energy is not fully conserved due to friction or other non-conservative forces at the bottom <marks>A1</marks>
- State that some mechanical energy is transformed into thermal energy or sound <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify initial energy
steps.content:
Initially, at height $H$, the ball is at rest and possesses gravitational potential energy. From the **Mechanics** section of the data booklet, this is given by:

$$\Delta E_{p} = mg\Delta h$$

At the starting point, the total mechanical energy is equal to $mgH$.

steps.title: Account for energy loss
steps.content:
The question states that there is friction at the bottom of the curve. Friction is a non-conservative force that does work against the motion of the ball.

Because of this friction, the **mechanical energy is not fully conserved** as the ball passes through the bottom section.

highlights.text: friction at the bottom of the curve where some mechanical energy is lost

steps.title: Describe energy transformation
steps.content:
When the ball encounters friction, some of its mechanical energy (specifically kinetic energy at that point) is **transformed into thermal energy (heat) or sound energy**.

This energy is dissipated into the surroundings and is no longer available to help the ball climb the opposite slope.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
Since the total mechanical energy has decreased, the ball has less energy to convert back into gravitational potential energy on the other side.

As a result, the final potential energy $mgh$ must be less than the initial potential energy $mgH$, leading to the conclusion that $h < H$.

steps.title: Analyze initial mechanical energy
steps.content:
To understand the motion, we first look at the total mechanical energy at the start. Since the ball is released from rest at height $H$, its initial energy is entirely gravitational potential energy:

$$\Delta E_{p}=mg\Delta h$$

In this case, the initial mechanical energy is $mgH$. This concept is covered in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

highlights.text: released from rest at a height H

steps.title: Identify work done by friction
steps.content:
The question specifies that friction is present at the bottom of the curve. Friction is a **non-conservative force**. Using the formula for work:

$$W=Fs\cos\theta$$

Because the friction force acts in the opposite direction to the displacement ($\theta = 180^{\circ}$), it does **negative work** on the ball as it passes the bottom section.

highlights.text: no friction except at the bottom of the curve

steps.title: Relate work to energy loss
steps.content:
According to the work-energy theorem, the work done by non-conservative forces equals the change in the system's total mechanical energy ($E_k + E_p$). 

Since friction does negative work, the total mechanical energy of the ball decreases. This energy is not 'gone' but is **transformed into thermal energy (heat) or sound energy** as the ball rubs against the surface. 

This earns the first mark ($A1$) by recognizing energy is not conserved and the second mark ($A1$) by stating the transformation to thermal energy.

steps.title: Determine the final height
steps.content:
When the ball reaches its maximum height $h$ on the opposite side, its kinetic energy is zero ($E_k = 0$). 

Because some mechanical energy was lost at the bottom, the final potential energy ($mgh$) must be less than the initial potential energy ($mgH$):

$$mgh < mgH \implies h < H$$

Therefore, the ball cannot reach the original height $H$ because it no longer has enough mechanical energy to do so.

parts.content: Assuming no friction except at the bottom, deduce an expression for the mechanical energy lost as the ball moves from height $H$ to height $h$.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct expression: $\text{Energy lost} = \text{initial potential energy} - \text{final potential energy} = mg(H-h)$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze the energy states
steps.content:
To find the energy lost, we look at the mechanical energy at the start and the end of the motion. 

At the starting point, the ball is released from rest at height $H$. At the highest point on the opposite side (height $h$), the ball momentarily comes to rest again. Since the velocity is zero at both points, the kinetic energy ($E_k$) is zero. Therefore, the total mechanical energy at both points consists only of gravitational potential energy ($E_p$).

This concept is part of **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

steps.title: Calculate initial potential energy
steps.content:
Using the formula for gravitational potential energy from the data booklet, we can find the energy the ball has at the start at height $H$:

$$\Delta E_{p}=mg\Delta h$$

Substituting $H$ for the height:
$$E_{p, \text{initial}} = mgH$$

steps.title: Calculate final potential energy
steps.content:
Similarly, we determine the potential energy at the final height $h$ on the opposite side:

$$E_{p, \text{final}} = mgh$$

steps.title: Determine the energy lost
steps.content:
The mechanical energy lost is the difference between the initial energy and the final energy. 

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Energy lost} &= E_{p, \text{initial}} - E_{p, \text{final}} \\ &= mgH - mgh \end{aligned}$$

We can factor out $mg$ to simplify the expression:

$$\text{Energy lost} = mg(H - h)$$ 

This expression represents the energy converted to thermal energy due to friction at the bottom of the curve.

steps.title: Define the Energy Balance
steps.content:
To solve this, we use the principle of conservation of energy. In a system with friction, the total initial mechanical energy is equal to the final mechanical energy plus any energy dissipated (lost) as heat due to friction.

$$\text{Initial Energy} = \text{Final Energy} + \text{Energy Lost}$$

highlights.text: no friction except at the bottom

steps.title: Determine Initial Mechanical Energy
steps.content:
At the starting point, the ball is released from rest at height $H$. Since it isn't moving yet, its kinetic energy ($E_k$) is zero. Using the formula for gravitational potential energy from the data booklet, the initial energy is:

$$\Delta E_{p}=mg\Delta h$$

$$E_{\text{initial}} = mgH$$

highlights.text: released from rest at a height H

steps.title: Determine Final Mechanical Energy
steps.content:
When the ball reaches its maximum height $h$ on the opposite side, it momentarily stops, meaning its final kinetic energy is also zero. Its final mechanical energy is entirely potential energy:

$$E_{\text{final}} = mgh$$

highlights.text: rises up to a height h

steps.title: Calculate Energy Lost
steps.content:
Now, we substitute our expressions into the energy balance equation and rearrange to find the energy lost:

$$\begin{aligned} mgH &= mgh + E_{\text{lost}} \\ E_{\text{lost}} &= mgH - mgh \\ E_{\text{lost}} &= mg(H - h) \end{aligned}$$

This expression represents the difference in potential energy, which is the mechanical energy lost to friction at the bottom of the curve.

parts.content: If $H = 2.5 \text{ m}$, $h = 2.0 \text{ m}$ and $m = 0.60 \text{ kg}$, calculate the energy lost.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into the expression: $\text{Energy lost} = (0.60)(9.81)(2.5 - 2.0)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final result: $2.943 \text{ J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the energy difference
steps.content:
To find the energy lost, we look at the difference between the mechanical energy the ball had at the start and what it had at the end. Since the ball starts and ends at rest at its maximum heights, we only need to compare its gravitational potential energy at $H$ and $h$.

This concept is part of **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**, where we learn that mechanical energy is conserved unless work is done by non-conservative forces like friction.

steps.title: Apply potential energy formula
steps.content:
From the data booklet, the formula for the change in gravitational potential energy is:

$$\Delta E_p = mg\Delta h$$

In this case, the energy lost is the initial potential energy minus the final potential energy:

$$\text{Energy lost} = mgH - mgh = mg(H - h)$$ 

This substitution of values into the energy expression is worth the first mark (**M1**).

steps.title: Calculate the energy loss
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the given values: 
- $m = 0.60 \text{ kg}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ (standard value used in the markscheme)
- $H = 2.5 \text{ m}$
- $h = 2.0 \text{ m}$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Energy lost} &= 0.60 \times 9.81 \times (2.5 - 2.0) \\ &= 0.60 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Perform the calculation in the Run-Matrix menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to get the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the total energy loss.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Performing the calculation gives us the final energy loss, which earns the accuracy mark (**A1**):

$$\text{Energy lost} = 2.943 \text{ J}$$

steps.title: Define the Kinetic Energy approach
steps.content:
To find the energy lost at the bottom of the track, we can compare two values:
1. The **initial kinetic energy** the ball would have at the bottom if it fell from height $H$ without loss.
2. The **required kinetic energy** at the bottom needed for the ball to successfully climb back up to height $h$.

The difference between these two values represents the mechanical energy lost to friction at the bottom.

steps.title: Calculate theoretical initial kinetic energy
steps.content:
The ball starts from rest at height $H$. Using the principle of conservation of energy (Topic A.3.1), the potential energy lost equals the kinetic energy gained at the bottom. 

We use the formula for gravitational potential energy from the data booklet:
$$\Delta E_p = mg\Delta h$$

Substituting $H = 2.5 \text{ m}$, $m = 0.60 \text{ kg}$, and $g = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$:
$$E_{k, \text{initial}} = 0.60 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 = 14.715 \text{ J}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply mass, gravity, and initial height: 0.60 × 9.81 × 2.5.

calculator.description: Calculate the theoretical kinetic energy at the bottom.

steps.title: Determine required kinetic energy
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the kinetic energy the ball must have after the friction zone to reach the final height $h = 2.0 \text{ m}$. This is equal to the potential energy at that height:

$$E_{k, \text{required}} = mgh$$
$$E_{k, \text{required}} = 0.60 \times 9.81 \times 2.0 = 11.772 \text{ J}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply mass, gravity, and final height: 0.60 × 9.81 × 2.0.

calculator.description: Calculate the required kinetic energy to reach height h.

steps.title: Calculate total energy lost
steps.content:
The energy lost is the difference between the energy the ball had before the friction zone and what it had after:

$$\text{Energy lost} = E_{k, \text{initial}} - E_{k, \text{required}}$$
$$\text{Energy lost} = 14.715 \text{ J} - 11.772 \text{ J} = 2.943 \text{ J}$$

This can also be written in one step as:
$$\text{Energy lost} = mg(H - h) = 0.60 \times 9.81 \times (2.5 - 2.0)$$ 

According to the markscheme, the final answer is $2.943 \text{ J}$.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the two values: 14.715 - 11.772 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the difference in energy.

parts.content: Calculate the percentage of mechanical energy lost during the motion.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate initial energy: $E_{\text{initial}} = mgH = (0.60)(9.81)(2.5) = 14.715 \text{ J}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate percentage lost: $\frac{2.943}{14.715} \times 100 = 20.0\%$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Calculate initial mechanical energy
steps.content:
Since the ball is released from rest, its initial mechanical energy ($E_{\text{initial}}$) is entirely gravitational potential energy. We use the formula for potential energy from the data booklet:

$$\Delta E_{p}=mg\Delta h$$

Given $m = 0.60\text{ kg}$, $H = 2.5\text{ m}$, and using $g = 9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$:

$$E_{\text{initial}} = mgH = 0.60 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 = 14.715\text{ J}$$

highlights.text: released from rest at a height $H$

steps.title: Calculate final mechanical energy
steps.content:
When the ball reaches its maximum height $h$ on the opposite side, it is again momentarily at rest. Its final mechanical energy ($E_{\text{final}}$) is its potential energy at that height ($h = 2.0\text{ m}$):

$$E_{\text{final}} = mgh = 0.60 \times 9.81 \times 2.0 = 11.772\text{ J}$$

steps.title: Determine the energy lost
steps.content:
The mechanical energy lost during the motion is the difference between the initial and final energies:

$$\text{Energy lost} = E_{\text{initial}} - E_{\text{final}}$$
$$\text{Energy lost} = 14.715 - 11.772 = 2.943\text{ J}$$

steps.title: Calculate the percentage loss
steps.content:
To find the percentage of mechanical energy lost, we divide the energy lost by the initial energy and multiply by 100:

$$\% \text{ lost} = \frac{\text{Energy lost}}{E_{\text{initial}}} \times 100$$
$$\% \text{ lost} = \frac{2.943}{14.715} \times 100 = 20.0\%$$

desmos.instructions: Type (2.943/14.715)*100 into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the percentage loss by dividing the loss by the initial value.

steps.title: Establish the proportionality
steps.content:
In this problem, we are looking at the mechanical energy at the highest points of the motion. At these points (the release height $H$ and the final height $h$), the ball is momentarily at rest, meaning all its mechanical energy is Gravitational Potential Energy ($E_p$). 

From the data booklet, the formula for potential energy is:
$$\Delta E_p = mg\Delta h$$

Since the mass ($m$) and the acceleration due to gravity ($g$) remain constant throughout the motion, we can see that the mechanical energy is directly proportional to the height ($E \propto h$).

steps.title: Define the percentage loss
steps.content:
Because energy is directly proportional to height, the percentage of energy lost is identical to the percentage of height lost. We can write the formula for percentage loss as:

$$\text{Percentage Loss} = \frac{E_{\text{initial}} - E_{\text{final}}}{E_{\text{initial}}} \times 100\%$$

By substituting the proportional heights, this simplifies to:

$$\text{Percentage Loss} = \frac{H - h}{H} \times 100\%$$

steps.title: Calculate the result
steps.content:
Using the values provided in the problem ($H = 2.5\text{ m}$ and $h = 2.0\text{ m}$), we can calculate the percentage loss:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Percentage Loss} &= \frac{2.5 - 2.0}{2.5} \times 100\% \\ &= \frac{0.5}{2.5} \times 100\% \\ &= 20.0\% \end{aligned}$$

This approach is much faster than calculating the joules of energy first, as the constants $m$ and $g$ cancel out in the ratio.

gdc.instructions: Perform the calculation on the main computation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the ratio calculation into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the percentage loss using the height ratio.

questions.specification: A shipping crate of mass $$24\text{ kg}$$ is being pulled along a horizontal warehouse floor by a nylon strap. The strap is held at an angle of $$32^\circ$$ above the horizontal. The tension in the strap is maintained at $$110\text{ N}$$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses force components and vertical equilibrium to find the normal force, involving Newton’s laws and free-body analysis with tension at an angle and resistive force.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Final speed after pulling through a distance against a constant resistive force can be solved via work done by net force (work–energy theorem) using the horizontal component of tension.

parts.content: Determine the magnitude of the normal contact force exerted by the floor on the crate.
parts.markscheme:
weight $= 24 \times 9.81 (= 235.4\text{ N})$ OR vertical component of tension $= 110 \times \sin(32^\circ) (= 58.3\text{ N})$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$R = 235.4 - 58.3 = 177(\text{N})$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Allow use of $$g = 10\text{ m s}^{-2}$$. Weight $= 240\text{ N}$, Answer $= 181.7(\text{N})$.

parts.content: Calculate the component of the tension in the strap that acts parallel to the warehouse floor.
parts.markscheme:
horizontal component $= 110 \times \cos(32^\circ) = 93.3(\text{N})$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Allow a bald correct answer.

parts.content: A constant resistive force of $$65\text{ N}$$ acts on the crate. The crate starts from rest and is pulled through a distance of $$5.0\text{ m}$$. Calculate the final velocity of the crate at the end of this distance.
parts.markscheme:
net force $= 93.3 - 65 (= 28.3\text{ N})$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

acceleration $= \frac{28.3}{24} (= 1.18\text{ m s}^{-2})$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ OR $v = \sqrt{2 \times 1.18 \times 5.0}$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$3.44(\text{m s}^{-1})$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

***OR***

net force $= 93.3 - 65 (= 28.3\text{ N})$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Work done $= F_{net} \times d = 28.3 \times 5.0 (= 141.5\text{ J})$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$141.5 = \frac{1}{2} \times 24 \times v^2$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$3.44(\text{m s}^{-1})$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Allow ECF from part (b).

parts.content: Calculate the output power required to lift the passengers and cabin.

parts.content: Calculate the electrical power input required to maintain this speed.

parts.content: Calculate the useful power output of the motor.
parts.markscheme:
- State formula: $P_{\text{useful}}=Fv$, where $F=mg$
- Substitute values: $P_{\text{useful}}=(100.0)(9.81)(0.800)$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate useful power: $P_{\text{useful}}=785 \mathrm{~W}$ (accept $784.8 \mathrm{~W}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Determine the efficiency of the motor.
parts.markscheme:
- State formula: $\eta=\frac{P_{\text{useful}}}{P_{\text{input}}} \times 100$
- Substitute values: $\eta=\frac{784.8}{900.0} \times 100$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate efficiency: $\eta=87.2 \%$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
This question is about investigating the relationship between the pressure and volume of a gas at a constant temperature. 

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-14db12a3-cd51-4ad9-b43e-5bd3e91cfc7e-1771929956947/87395d99-e67e-4808-9292-fd7f3de66eaa.jpeg)

A student uses a gas syringe connected to a digital pressure sensor to measure the pressure $P$ of a trapped mass of air as its volume $V$ is varied. The experiment is performed slowly to ensure the temperature of the gas remains constant.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic B - The particulate nature of matter > B.3 Gas laws > B.3.1 Kinetic theory and gas behavior
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.3.1 Kinetic theory and gas behavior
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses ideal gas law at constant temperature to show P ∝ 1/V (Boyle’s law relationship) and interprets pressure–volume behavior for a trapped gas.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic B - The particulate nature of matter > B.3 Gas laws > B.3.2 Microscopic basis for macroscopic behavior
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.3.2 Microscopic basis for macroscopic behavior
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Includes experimental investigation/graphing of macroscopic gas behavior (P vs 1/V) and using the graph to infer pressure at a given volume, which can be linked to macroscopic behavior arising from particle model.

parts.content: Show that for an ideal gas at constant temperature, the pressure $P$ is directly proportional to the reciprocal of the volume, $\frac{1}{V}$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the ideal gas equation $PV = nRT$ or Boyle's Law $PV = \text{constant}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Explain that if $n$, $R$, and $T$ are constant, then $P = \frac{k}{V}$, where $k$ is a constant, which implies $P \propto \frac{1}{V}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content:
The table below shows the measured values of pressure $P$ for different volumes $V$ of the trapped air. (Uncertainties in measurement are not shown in the table.)

| $V / \mathrm{cm^3}$ | $P / \mathrm{kPa}$ | $\frac{1}{V} / \mathrm{cm^{-3}}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\infty$ | 0 | 0 |
| 30.0 | 80 | .......... |
| 20.0 | 120 | .......... |
| 15.0 | 160 | .......... |
| 10.0 | 240 | .......... |

Complete the last column of the table.
Plot a graph of $P$ against $\frac{1}{V}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Correctly labelled axes with units ($P$ on y-axis, $1/V$ on x-axis) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Appropriate linear scales using more than half the grid <marks>1 mark</marks>

- All data points from the table plotted accurately <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Line of best fit drawn as a straight line passing through the origin <marks>1 mark</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Consider the situation where the volume of the gas is adjusted to $12.5 \, \mathrm{cm^3}$. Use information from your graph to determine the pressure of the gas at this volume.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the reciprocal volume: $\frac{1}{12.5} = 0.080 \, \mathrm{cm^{-3}}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Read the corresponding pressure from the graph: $P = 192 \, \mathrm{kPa}$ (Accept range $188 - 196 \, \mathrm{kPa}$ depending on the line of best fit) <marks>1 mark</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: The digital pressure sensor has an absolute uncertainty of $\pm 6 \, \mathrm{kPa}$. Calculate the percentage uncertainty in the pressure value determined in part (d).
parts.markscheme:
- State the formula for percentage uncertainty: $\frac{\Delta P}{P} \times 100$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Substitute values: $\frac{6}{192} \times 100 = 3.1 \%$ (Accept values between $3.0 \%$ and $3.2 \%$ based on part d) <marks>1 mark</marks>

2 marks total

questions.specification: An object moving on a horizontal surface has kinetic energy $K$ when its speed is $v$. A resistive force acts on the object until its speed is reduced to $\frac{v}{3}$. How much work is done by the resistive force on the object?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses the work–energy principle: work done by a resistive force equals the change in kinetic energy as speed reduces from v to v/3.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.1 Conservation of energy
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.1 Conservation of energy
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Implicitly relies on energy considerations (kinetic energy change accounting for energy transferred/dissipated by resistive forces).

options.markscheme: Since $K \propto v^2$, when $v$ becomes $\frac{v}{3}$, $K_f = K\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{K}{9}$. Work done by the resistive force equals the change in kinetic energy: $W = K_f - K_i = \frac{K}{9} - K = -\frac{8K}{9}$. (Negative since the resistive force removes energy.)
Incorrect. This corresponds to a linear (not squared) dependence on speed, or an incorrect subtraction.
Incorrect. This treats kinetic energy as proportional to $v$ rather than $v^2$.
Incorrect. $\frac{K}{9}$ is the final kinetic energy, not the work done (change in kinetic energy).

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.1 Conservation of energy

options.markscheme:
- Calculate initial potential energy: $E_p = mgh$
- Account for energy loss: $E_k = 0.8 E_p = 0.8mgh$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Set up energy equation: $\frac{1}{2}mv^2 = 0.8mgh$
- Solve for $v$: $v = \sqrt{1.6gh}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute values: $v = \sqrt{1.6 \times 9.81 \times 10}$
- Calculate final answer: $v = 12.5 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

questions.specification:
A student investigates the relationship between the angle of an inclined plane and the acceleration of a trolley rolling down the plane. The trolley is released from rest, and its acceleration is measured using a motion sensor placed at the top of the ramp.

The results of the experiment are recorded in the table below:
$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|}
\hline \text { Angle of Incline }(\theta) & \text { Acceleration }(a)\left(\mathrm{ms}^{-2}\right) \\
\hline 10^{\circ} & 1.70 \\
\hline 20^{\circ} & 3.34 \\
\hline 30^{\circ} & 4.90 \\
\hline 40^{\circ} & 6.20 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

The theoretical relationship between acceleration and the angle of incline is given by:

$$
a=g \sin \theta
$$

where $g=9.81 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses the inclined-plane model a = g sinθ, which comes from resolving weight component along the slope via Newton’s second law; also asks for reasons experimental a is lower (e.g., friction/air resistance), i.e., forces affecting acceleration.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Involves measuring and plotting acceleration data, comparing to a theoretical linear relationship, and interpreting a graph to judge consistency with a model (kinematics representation/analysis of motion).

parts.content: Using the equation $a=g \sin \theta$, calculate the theoretical acceleration for each angle in the table and add it as a third column.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate $\sin \theta$ for each angle: $\sin(10^{\circ})=0.1736$, $\sin(20^{\circ})=0.3420$, $\sin(30^{\circ})=0.5000$, $\sin(40^{\circ})=0.6428$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate theoretical $a$ ($a=g \sin \theta$):
  For $\theta=10^{\circ}$, $a=(9.81)(0.1736)=1.70 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$
  For $\theta=20^{\circ}$, $a=(9.81)(0.3420)=3.36 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$
  For $\theta=30^{\circ}$, $a=(9.81)(0.5000)=4.91 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$
  For $\theta=40^{\circ}$, $a=(9.81)(0.6428)=6.31 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Add results to table: <marks>A1</marks>

| Angle of Incline $(\theta)$ | Acceleration $(a)(\mathrm{ms}^{-2})$ | Theoretical Acceleration $(\mathrm{ms}^{-2})$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $10^{\circ}$ | 1.70 | 1.70 |
| $20^{\circ}$ | 3.34 | 3.36 |
| $30^{\circ}$ | 4.90 | 4.91 |
| $40^{\circ}$ | 6.20 | 6.31 |

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To find the theoretical acceleration, we use the specific relationship provided in the problem description. We are given the acceleration due to gravity, $g$, and we need to substitute each angle $\theta$ into the formula.

$$a = g \sin \theta$$

This formula is derived from Newton's Second Law ($$F=ma$$) applied to a mass on an incline, which is a core concept in **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**.

steps.title: Calculate for first two angles
steps.content:
We calculate the theoretical acceleration for $10^{\circ}$ and $20^{\circ}$ by substituting the values into the formula. 

**For $\theta = 10^{\circ}$:**
$$\begin{aligned} a &= 9.81 \times \sin(10^{\circ}) \\ a &\approx 9.81 \times 0.1736 \\ a &= 1.70 \text{ m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

**For $\theta = 20^{\circ}$:**
$$\begin{aligned} a &= 9.81 \times \sin(20^{\circ}) \\ a &\approx 9.81 \times 0.3420 \\ a &= 3.36 \text{ m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Set angle mode to Degrees.
2. Enter 9.81 * sin(10).
3. Enter 9.81 * sin(20).

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MODE], ensure 'DEGREE' is highlighted.
2. Press [2ND] [QUIT].
3. Type 9.81 * sin(10) and press [ENTER].
4. Type 9.81 * sin(20) and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions:
1. Click the wrench icon (Settings) and select 'Degrees'.
2. Type 9.81 * sin(10).
3. Type 9.81 * sin(20).

calculator.description: Calculate theoretical acceleration for 10 and 20 degrees. Ensure your calculator is in DEGREE mode.

steps.title: Calculate for remaining angles
steps.content:
Repeat the substitution for $30^{\circ}$ and $40^{\circ}$.

**For $\theta = 30^{\circ}$:**
$$\begin{aligned} a &= 9.81 \times \sin(30^{\circ}) \\ a &= 9.81 \times 0.5000 \\ a &= 4.91 \text{ m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

**For $\theta = 40^{\circ}$:**
$$\begin{aligned} a &= 9.81 \times \sin(40^{\circ}) \\ a &\approx 9.81 \times 0.6428 \\ a &= 6.31 \text{ m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions:
1. Type 9.81 * sin(30) and press [ENTER].
2. Type 9.81 * sin(40) and press [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate theoretical acceleration for 30 and 40 degrees.

steps.title: Update the data table
steps.content:
Finally, we compile our calculated values into a third column in the table. 

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|}
\hline \text { Angle } (\theta) & \text { Accel. } (a) (\text{ms}^{-2}) & \text { Theoretical } a (\text{ms}^{-2}) \\
\hline 10^{\circ} & 1.70 & 1.70 \\
\hline 20^{\circ} & 3.34 & 3.36 \\
\hline 30^{\circ} & 4.90 & 4.91 \\
\hline 40^{\circ} & 6.20 & 6.31 \\
\hline
\end{array}$$

Notice that the theoretical values are slightly higher than the measured values, likely due to factors like friction or air resistance which are ignored in the simple model.

steps.title: Identify the Theoretical Relationship
steps.content:
The question asks us to use the theoretical formula provided to calculate the acceleration for different angles. This relationship is part of the study of forces on inclined planes, which you can review in **Topic A.2 Forces and momentum**.

We are given the formula:
$$a = g \sin \theta$$

Where:
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ (the acceleration due to gravity)
- $\theta$ is the angle of incline in degrees.

steps.title: Input Data into GDC
steps.content:
To calculate all values efficiently, we will use the List function on your Graphing Display Calculator (GDC). First, enter the experimental angles into the first list ($L_1$).

**Important:** Ensure your calculator is set to **Degree mode** since the angles are given in degrees ($10^\circ, 20^\circ, \dots$).

gdc.instructions: Go to the 'Statistics' or 'List' menu. In List 1, enter 10, 20, 30, 40. Check system settings to ensure Angle is set to 'Degree'.

ti84.instructions: Press [STAT] [1:Edit]. In L1, enter 10, 20, 30, 40. Press [MODE] and ensure 'DEGREE' is highlighted.

desmos.instructions: Create a list: L_1 = [10, 20, 30, 40]. Ensure the settings toggle (wrench icon) is set to 'Degrees'.

calculator.description: Entering angles into a list for bulk calculation.

steps.title: Calculate Using List Formula
steps.content:
Now, we define the second list ($L_2$) using the theoretical formula. By applying the formula to the entire list, we get all four values at once:

$$L_2 = 9.81 \times \sin(L_1)$$

Rounding to two decimal places (to match the experimental data's precision), we get:
- For $10^\circ$: $9.81 \times \sin(10^\circ) \approx 1.70 \text{ m s}^{-2}$
- For $20^\circ$: $9.81 \times \sin(20^\circ) \approx 3.36 \text{ m s}^{-2}$
- For $30^\circ$: $9.81 \times \sin(30^\circ) \approx 4.91 \text{ m s}^{-2}$
- For $40^\circ$: $9.81 \times \sin(40^\circ) \approx 6.31 \text{ m s}^{-2}$

gdc.instructions: Highlight the header of List 2. Enter the formula '9.81 * sin(List 1)' and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: In the [STAT] [Edit] screen, scroll up to highlight the header 'L2'. Type '9.81 * sin(L1)' and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: In a new line, type the calculation using the list name.

calculator.description: Calculate theoretical acceleration for all angles simultaneously.

steps.title: Construct the Final Table
steps.content:
Finally, we present the data in the requested table format. In an IB exam, showing the $\sin \theta$ intermediate values can earn you a method mark (**M1**), while the final calculated accelerations earn an accuracy mark (**A1**).

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline \text{Angle } (\theta) & \text{Acc. } (a) \text{ (exp.)} & \text{Theoretical Acc. } (\text{m s}^{-2}) \\
\hline 10^\circ & 1.70 & 1.70 \\
\hline 20^\circ & 3.34 & 3.36 \\
\hline 30^\circ & 4.90 & 4.91 \\
\hline 40^\circ & 6.20 & 6.31 \\
\hline
\end{array}$$

Notice that the experimental and theoretical values are very close, but they diverge slightly as the angle increases, which is typical in real-world physics experiments.

parts.content: Plot a graph of experimental acceleration $a$ ($y$-axis) against $\sin \theta$ ($x$-axis). Use the graph to determine whether the experimental results are consistent with the theoretical model.
parts.markscheme:
- Plot the graph of experimental acceleration $a$ ($y$-axis) against $\sin \theta$ ($x$-axis) <marks>M1</marks>

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/32779a69-42e4-4136-b9e2-01ae104ce101.png)

- State that a straight line through the origin indicates that acceleration is proportional to $\sin \theta$ <marks>R1</marks>

- Conclude that the experimental results support the theoretical model within limits of experimental error, noting that experimental values are consistently slightly lower than theoretical values <marks>R1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify plot axes
steps.content:
To check the relationship $$a = g \sin \theta$$, we must linearize the equation. The question asks us to plot experimental acceleration $$a$$ on the $$y$$-axis against $$\sin \theta$$ on the $$x$$-axis. 

This maps to the linear form $$y = mx + c$$:
- $$y = a$$
- $$x = \sin \theta$$
- Gradient $$m = g$$
- $$y$$-intercept $$c = 0$$

highlights.text: Plot a graph of experimental acceleration $a$ ($y$-axis) against $\sin \theta$ ($x$-axis)

steps.title: Calculate x-axis values
steps.content:
We need to calculate the value of $$\sin \theta$$ for each angle given in the table. 

$$\begin{aligned} \sin(10^\circ) &\approx 0.17 \\ \sin(20^\circ) &\approx 0.34 \\ \sin(30^\circ) &= 0.50 \\ \sin(40^\circ) &\approx 0.64 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Ensure angle setting is Degrees. Enter the list of angles and apply the sine function.

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is set to DEGREE. Calculate sin(angle) for each value.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to get the decimal values.

calculator.description: Calculate sine values for the angles

steps.title: Plot the data points
steps.content:
Plot the points ($$\sin \theta$$, $$a$$) on the graph grid:

- $$(0.17, 1.70)$$
- $$(0.34, 3.34)$$
- $$(0.50, 4.90)$$
- $$(0.64, 6.20)$$

Draw a straight line of best fit through these points. The line should pass through or very close to the origin $$(0,0)$$.

steps.title: Check for linearity
steps.content:
Observe the shape of the graph:
1. The plotted points form a straight line pattern.
2. The line of best fit passes through the origin $$(0,0)$$.

In physics, a straight line through the origin indicates that the variable on the $$y$$-axis is directly proportional to the variable on the $$x$$-axis ($$a \propto \sin \theta$$).

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
Since the graph is a straight line passing through the origin, the experimental results are consistent with the theoretical model $$a = g \sin \theta$$. 

However, note that the experimental points may lie slightly below the theoretical line (since $$g \approx 9.8$$ and $$6.20 < 9.8 \times 0.64 \approx 6.27$$), which could be due to friction or air resistance.

steps.title: Linearize the equation
steps.content:
To perform the **Gradient Comparison**, we first need to express the theoretical relationship as a linear equation of the form $y = mx + c$.

The given equation is:
$$a = g \sin \theta$$

Comparing this to $y = mx + c$:
- $y = a$ (acceleration)
- $x = \sin \theta$
- $m = g$ (the gradient represents the acceleration due to gravity)
- $c = 0$ (the line should pass through the origin)

Therefore, if we plot $a$ against $\sin \theta$, we expect a straight line with a gradient equal to $g$.

steps.title: Calculate x-axis data
steps.content:
We need to calculate the values for the x-axis ($x = \sin \theta$) corresponding to each angle in the table.

$$\begin{aligned} \sin(10^{\circ}) &\approx 0.174 \\ \sin(20^{\circ}) &\approx 0.342 \\ \sin(30^{\circ}) &= 0.500 \\ \sin(40^{\circ}) &\approx 0.643 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In Run-Matrix mode, set Angle unit to Degrees. Enter sin(10), etc.

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is set to DEGREES. Calculate sin(10), sin(20), sin(30), sin(40).

desmos.instructions: Type sin(10 deg) to calculate values.

calculator.description: Calculate sin(theta) for each angle

steps.title: Plot the graph
steps.content:
Now, plot the data points ($x, y$) on the graph:
- $(0.174, 1.70)$
- $(0.342, 3.34)$
- $(0.500, 4.90)$
- $(0.643, 6.20)$

Draw a **line of best fit** through these points. Since the theoretical equation has no y-intercept ($c=0$), the line should pass through or very close to the origin $(0,0)$.

highlights.text: Plot a graph of experimental acceleration a (y-axis) against sin θ (x-axis)

steps.title: Calculate the gradient
steps.content:
Select two points on your line of best fit to calculate the gradient ($m$). For accuracy, choose points far apart, for example, the origin $(0,0)$ and the point for $30^{\circ}$ which lies almost perfectly on the trend $(0.50, 4.90)$.

$$m = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{4.90 - 0}{0.500 - 0} = 9.80 \text{ m s}^{-2}$$

calculator.description: Verify gradient calculation

steps.title: Compare with theory
steps.content:
The theoretical value for the gradient is $g = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$.

Our experimental gradient is approximately $9.80 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ (or slightly lower depending on your exact line of best fit, e.g., using all points might yield $\approx 9.73$).

The graph is a straight line through the origin, confirming that $a \propto \sin \theta$. The experimental gradient is very close to the theoretical value of $g$, supporting the model. The slight difference (experimental values being slightly lower) is likely due to friction, which opposes motion and reduces acceleration.

parts.content: The experimental accelerations are found to be slightly lower than the theoretical values. Suggest two reasons for this discrepancy and explain how the experimental setup could be improved to reduce these errors.
parts.markscheme:
- State that friction between the trolley and the inclined plane reduces the observed acceleration <marks>A1</marks>
- Suggest using a smoother ramp or applying lubrication to minimize friction <marks>A1</marks>

- State that air resistance acting on the trolley may also reduce its acceleration slightly <marks>A1</marks>
- Suggest using a heavier trolley to reduce the relative impact of air resistance <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award 1 mark for each relevant reason and 1 mark for the explanation on the improvement.</note>

steps.title: Verify the discrepancy
steps.content:
First, let's verify the statement that experimental values are lower than theoretical ones. Using the given formula for $\theta = 40^{\circ}$:

$$
a_{\text{theoretical}} = 9.81 \times \sin(40^{\circ}) \approx 6.31 \text{ m s}^{-2}
$$

The experimental value in the table is $6.20 \text{ m s}^{-2}$. Since $6.20 < 6.31$, there is indeed a resistive force opposing the motion that the theoretical model ignores. We need to identify two such forces.

ti84.instructions: Enter 9.81 * sin(40). Ensure mode is set to DEGREES.

calculator.description: Calculate theoretical acceleration for 40 degrees

highlights.text: The experimental accelerations are found to be slightly lower than the theoretical values.

steps.title: Reason 1: Friction
steps.content:
The theoretical model assumes the contact surfaces are perfectly smooth. In reality, **friction** acts between the wheels of the trolley and the ramp (and within the wheel bearings).

Friction is a non-conservative force that acts in the direction opposite to the motion. This reduces the net force acting down the slope:

$$
F_{\text{net}} = mg \sin \theta - F_{\text{friction}}
$$

Consequently, the measured acceleration is less than $g \sin \theta$.

steps.title: Improvement 1: Reducing friction
steps.content:
To reduce the error caused by friction, we need to minimize the contact resistance.

**Improvement:** Use a **smoother ramp** surface or apply **lubrication** (oil/grease) to the wheel axles and the ramp surface. This reduces the coefficient of friction, bringing the experimental conditions closer to the frictionless theoretical model.

steps.title: Reason 2: Air resistance
steps.content:
The second non-conservative force is **air resistance** (or drag). As the trolley accelerates down the ramp, it moves through the air, creating a resistive force opposite to its velocity.

The theoretical equation $a=g \sin \theta$ ignores this fluid resistance. Even though the trolley is not moving very fast, this force still contributes to a slight reduction in the net force and acceleration.

steps.title: Improvement 2: Minimizing air resistance impact
steps.content:
We cannot remove the air, but we can reduce the *effect* of air resistance on the acceleration.

**Improvement:** Use a **heavier (more massive) trolley**.

Consider Newton's Second Law with drag included:

$$
a = \frac{F_{\text{net}}}{m} = \frac{mg \sin \theta - F_{\text{drag}}}{m} = g \sin \theta - \frac{F_{\text{drag}}}{m}
$$

By increasing the mass $m$, the term $\frac{F_{\text{drag}}}{m}$ becomes smaller, making the observed acceleration $a$ closer to the theoretical value $g \sin \theta$.

questions.specification: Two particles, $X$ and $Y$, are in uniform circular motion. Particle $X$ moves in a circle of radius $r$ with a period $T$. Particle $Y$ moves in a circle of radius $2r$ with a period $3T$. What is the ratio $\frac{\text{centripetal acceleration of } X}{\text{centripetal acceleration of } Y}$?

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b) and (c) explicitly use constant-acceleration kinematic equations in the vertical direction (e.g., h = 1/2 g t^2) to derive and compute time of flight.

parts.content: State the initial value of the vertical component of the ball's velocity.
parts.markscheme: Zero / $0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Derive an expression for the time $t$ the ball takes to reach the ground in terms of $h$ and $g$.
parts.markscheme:
Consider vertical motion with $u_y = 0$:

$$h = u_y t + \frac{1}{2}gt^2 \implies h = \frac{1}{2}gt^2$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

Solving for $t$:

$$t = \sqrt{\frac{2h}{g}}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the time of flight for a ball projected from a cliff with a height $h = 32 \text{ m}$. Take $g = 9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$.
parts.markscheme:
$$t = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 32}{9.8}}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$$t = 2.55 \text{ s}$$ (accept $2.6 \text{ s}$ or $2.56 \text{ s}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Draw a vector diagram showing the velocity of the ball and its horizontal and vertical components at the instant it is halfway (in terms of time) through its flight. Label the resultant velocity $v$, the horizontal component $v_x$, and the vertical component $v_y$.
parts.markscheme:
Diagram showing:
- Horizontal vector $v_x$ pointing right <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Vertical vector $v_y$ pointing downward <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Resultant vector $v$ acting at an angle between $v_x$ and $v_y$ forming a vector triangle or rectangle <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Length of $v_x$ should be constant; $v_y$ should be approximately half its final impact length.</note>

parts.content: Determine an expression for the initial horizontal speed $v_x$ in terms of $D$, $h$, and $g$.
parts.markscheme:
Horizontal distance $D = v_x t$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

Substitute expression for $t$ from part (b):

$$D = v_x \sqrt{\frac{2h}{g}}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

Rearrange for $v_x$:

$$v_x = D \sqrt{\frac{g}{2h}}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

options.markscheme: Student calculates the area of the triangular section only ($20 \text{ kJ}$) and divides by the time ($20 \text{ s}$).
Student calculates the area of the rectangular section only ($80 \text{ kJ}$) and divides by the time ($20 \text{ s}$).

- The work done is equal to the area under the force-distance graph.
- Area of triangle (0 to 10 m) = $\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \text{ m} \times 4.0 \text{ kN} = 20 \text{ kJ}$.
- Area of rectangle (10 to 30 m) = $(30 - 10) \text{ m} \times 4.0 \text{ kN} = 80 \text{ kJ}$.
- Total work done $W = 20 + 80 = 100 \text{ kJ}$.
- Average power $P = \frac{W}{t} = \frac{100 \text{ kJ}}{20 \text{ s}} = 5.0 \text{ kW}$.
Student incorrectly assumes the force is a constant $4.0 \text{ kN}$ for the entire distance: $\frac{4.0 \text{ kN} \times 30 \text{ m}}{20 \text{ s}} = 6.0 \text{ kW}$.

questions.specification: A machine lifts a $500\ \text{kg}$ load vertically upward at a constant speed of $2.00\ \text{m s}^{-1}$. The gravitational field strength is $9.81\ \text{N kg}^{-1}$.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) asks for work done over a time interval and relates work to force and displacement (W=Fd, W=Pt). Part (c) discusses where the work goes (gravitational potential energy rather than kinetic), which is an energy transfer/work interpretation.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.1 Conservation of energy
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.1 Conservation of energy
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) relies on conservation of energy: with constant speed there is no change in kinetic energy, so the machine’s work increases gravitational potential energy (and possibly losses), consistent with energy conservation reasoning.

parts.content: Calculate the power output required to lift the load at this constant speed.
parts.markscheme:
$P = Fv = (500 \cdot 9.81) \cdot 2.00 = 9810 \, \text{W}$
- Correct substitution for weight and power <marks>1 mark</marks> 
- Correct final answer with unit <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the motion
steps.content: The problem states the load is lifted at a **constant speed**. This is a key concept from **Topic A.2 (Forces and momentum)**. Since there is no acceleration, the net force is zero. This means the upward force $F$ applied by the machine must exactly equal the downward weight of the load ($mg$).

steps.title: Calculate the weight
steps.content:
First, we calculate the force required to lift the load. Using the mass and the gravitational field strength provided:

$$F = mg$$

Substituting the values:

$$F = 500 \times 9.81 = 4905\text{ N}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the mass by the gravitational field strength.

calculator.description: Calculate the weight (force) in Newtons.

steps.title: Apply the power formula
steps.content:
In **Topic A.3 (Work, energy and power)**, we learn that when a force moves an object at a constant velocity, the power $P$ is the product of force $F$ and velocity $v$:

$$P = Fv$$

Now, substitute the force we found and the given velocity:

$$P = 4905 \times 2.00 = 9810\text{ W}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the weight by the constant speed.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the force (4905) by the velocity (2.00).

desmos.instructions: Type 4905 * 2 to get the power output.

calculator.description: Calculate the power output in Watts.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The power output required is $9810\text{ W}$. 

In an IB exam, ensure you include the correct unit (Watts, or $\text{J s}^{-1}$). Based on the markscheme, you earn one mark for the correct substitution of values and one mark for the final numerical answer with its unit.

methods.label: Using Rate of Change of Energy

steps.title: Define the energy method
steps.content:
To find the power output, we can use the definition of power as the rate of energy transfer. In this case, the machine is doing work to increase the gravitational potential energy ($E_p$) of the load. We can calculate this by looking at how much energy is gained over a specific time interval, such as 1 second.

This concept is part of **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

steps.title: Find height change per second
steps.content:
Let's choose a time interval of $\Delta t = 1.00\text{ s}$. Since the load moves at a constant speed of $2.00\text{ m s}^{-1}$, we calculate the vertical distance (height) moved in that time:

$$\Delta h = v \times \Delta t$$
$$\Delta h = 2.00\text{ m s}^{-1} \times 1.00\text{ s} = 2.00\text{ m}$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply the velocity by the time interval.

calculator.description: Calculate the displacement for 1 second of travel.

steps.title: Calculate potential energy change
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the increase in gravitational potential energy ($\Delta E_p$) for this 1-second interval using the formula from the data booklet:

$$\Delta E_{p}=mg\Delta h$$

Substituting the values:
$$\Delta E_p = 500\text{ kg} \times 9.81\text{ N kg}^{-1} \times 2.00\text{ m}$$
$$\Delta E_p = 9810\text{ J}$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply mass, gravity, and height change.

calculator.description: Calculate the change in gravitational potential energy.

steps.title: Determine the power output
steps.content:
Power is defined as the rate of change of energy over time:

$$P = \frac{\Delta E_p}{\Delta t}$$

Since we used a time interval of $1.00\text{ s}$:
$$P = \frac{9810\text{ J}}{1.00\text{ s}} = 9810\text{ W}$$

The power output required is $9810\text{ W}$ (or $9.81\text{ kW}$).

calculator.description: Divide the energy change by the time interval to find power.

parts.content: Determine the work done by the machine in $10.0\ \text{s}$.
parts.markscheme:
$W = P \cdot t = 9810 \cdot 10.0 = 98100 \, \text{J}$ 
- Correct formula and substitution <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct final answer with unit <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the strategy
steps.content:
To find the work done using the **Force and Displacement** approach, we need to determine the constant force exerted by the machine and the total vertical distance the load moves. This uses concepts from **Topic A.3: Work and energy transfer**.

Work is defined as:

$$W = Fs \cos\theta$$

Since the force is in the same direction as the displacement, $\cos\theta = 1$, so we focus on $W = Fs$.

steps.title: Calculate the upward force
steps.content:
The load is lifted at a **constant speed**, which means the net force is zero (Newton's First Law). Therefore, the upward force $F$ applied by the machine must exactly equal the weight ($mg$) of the load.

Using the mass and the gravitational field strength provided:

$$\begin{aligned} F &= m \times g \\ F &= 500 \times 9.81 \\ F &= 4905\ \text{N} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the weight (force).

steps.title: Calculate the vertical displacement
steps.content:
Next, we find how far the load travels in $10.0\ \text{s}$. Since the speed is constant, we use the simple kinematic relationship $s = vt$:

$$\begin{aligned} s &= v \times t \\ s &= 2.00 \times 10.0 \\ s &= 20.0\ \text{m} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate vertical displacement.

steps.title: Determine the work done
steps.content:
Finally, we multiply the force and the displacement to find the total work done:

$$\begin{aligned} W &= F \times s \\ W &= 4905 \times 20.0 \\ W &= 98100\ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

In an IB exam, you earn **1 mark** for showing the correct substitution into the formula and **1 mark** for the correct final answer with the unit (Joules).

calculator.description: Calculate the final work done.

steps.title: Calculate the vertical displacement
steps.content:
To find the work done through the gravitational potential energy approach, we first need to determine the change in height ($Δh$). Since the load is moving vertically at a constant speed, we use the basic kinematics formula from **Topic A.1**:

$$\Delta h = v \times t$$

Substituting the given values:
$$\Delta h = 2.00\ \text{m s}^{-1} \times 10.0\ \text{s} = 20.0\ \text{m}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the product of velocity and time.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication on the home screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the change in height.

steps.title: Determine change in GPE
steps.content:
Since the machine is lifting the load at a constant speed, the work done by the machine is equal to the increase in the gravitational potential energy of the load. We use the formula from the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$\Delta E_{p} = mg\Delta h$$

Substitute the known values into the equation:
$$\Delta E_{p} = 500\ \text{kg} \times 9.81\ \text{N kg}^{-1} \times 20.0\ \text{m}$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply mass, gravity, and height change on the home screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the change in gravitational potential energy.

steps.title: State final work done
steps.content:
The work done ($W$) is equivalent to the change in potential energy calculated:

$$W = 98100\ \text{J}$$

In IB exams, remember to include the correct units (Joules) and ensure your answer reflects the significant figures provided in the question (3 sig figs). Here, $9.81 \times 10^4\ \text{J}$ is also an acceptable format.

**Marking Note:** You earn 1 mark for the correct substitution into the energy formula and 1 mark for the final numerical answer with the correct unit.

steps.title: Find the lifting force
steps.content:
To find the work done using the Power and Time approach, we first need the force applied by the machine. The load is moving at a **constant speed**, which means the upward lifting force ($F$) must exactly balance the downward weight of the load ($mg$). This relates to **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**.

Using the formula for weight:
$$F = mg$$
$$F = 500 \times 9.81$$
$$F = 4905\text{ N}$$

steps.title: Calculate the power output
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the power ($P$) generated by the machine. Power is the rate at which work is done. For an object moving at a constant velocity ($v$), power can be calculated using the formula from **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**:

$$P = Fv$$
$$P = 4905 \times 2.00$$
$$P = 9810\text{ W}$$

steps.title: Determine total work done
steps.content:
Now that we have the power, we can find the total work done ($W$) over the given time interval. Since Power is work divided by time ($P = \frac{W}{t}$), we can rearrange this to solve for work:

$$W = P \times t$$
$$W = 9810 \times 10.0$$
$$W = 98100\text{ J}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final work done by multiplying power and time.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The work done by the machine in $10.0\text{ s}$ is **$98100\text{ J}$** (or $9.81 \times 10^4\text{ J}$).

In an IB exam, you earn one mark for the correct substitution into the power and work formulas and one mark for the correct final value with units.

parts.content: Explain why the load does not gain kinetic energy even though the machine is doing work.
parts.markscheme:
- The machine does work to overcome the gravitational force, not to increase speed. <marks>1 mark</marks>
-  Since speed is constant, kinetic energy remains unchanged. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
The graph shows resultant force $F$ against distance $s$ for a $4.0\text{ kg}$ object. The object starts from rest and moves in a straight line.

![Graph](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-31718c8c-0796-42cb-9881-8fd342a2ec89-1771929956947/00a46942-6e17-4df9-a3e4-461cda22898c.jpeg)

What is the speed of the object after it has travelled a distance of $5.0\text{ m}$?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Speed after traveling 5.0 m from a force–distance graph is found via work done (area under F–s curve) and the work–energy theorem to get kinetic energy and hence speed.

options.markscheme: Using work–energy: area under $F$–$s$ graph from $0$ to $5.0\text{ m}$ is $W=8\times3 + \tfrac12\times2\times8=32\text{ J}$. Then $\tfrac12 m v^2=W\Rightarrow v=\sqrt{\tfrac{2W}{m}}=\sqrt{\tfrac{64}{4.0}}=4.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$, so $3.2\text{ m s}^{-1}$ is incorrect.
Area under $F$–$s$ graph gives $W=32\text{ J}$, so $v=\sqrt{\tfrac{2W}{m}}=4.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$. Hence $3.5\text{ m s}^{-1}$ is incorrect.

Work done equals area under the $F$–$s$ graph: rectangle $8\text{ N}\times3\text{ m}=24\text{ J}$ plus triangle $\tfrac12\times2\text{ m}\times8\text{ N}=8\text{ J}$, so $W=32\text{ J}$. Starting from rest, $\tfrac12 m v^2=W\Rightarrow v=\sqrt{\tfrac{2W}{m}}=\sqrt{\tfrac{64}{4.0}}=4.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$.
Correct work is $W=32\text{ J}$ from the area under the graph, giving $v=\sqrt{\tfrac{2W}{m}}=4.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$. Therefore $4.5\text{ m s}^{-1}$ is incorrect.
