parts.content: Calculate the gravitational potential energy gained by the water.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into gravitational potential energy formula: $E_p = mgh = 200.0 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 15.0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate final energy: $29430 \text{ J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content: To find the energy gained by the water, we first identify the relevant values provided in the question. We are given the mass of the water and the vertical distance it is moved.

steps.title: Select the GPE formula
steps.content:
Since the water is being lifted vertically, it gains gravitational potential energy ($E_p$). According to **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**, we use the following formula from your data booklet:

$$\Delta E_{p}=mg\Delta h$$

In this equation, $m$ is the mass, $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity ($9.81\ \text{m s}^{-2}$), and $\Delta h$ is the change in vertical height.

steps.title: Calculate the energy gained
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the known values into the formula to find the total energy gained:

$$\begin{aligned} E_p &= 200.0 \times 9.81 \times 15.0 \\ E_p &= 29430\ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 200.0 * 9.81 * 15.0 on the main calculation screen.

calculator.description: Calculate gravitational potential energy by multiplying mass, gravity, and height.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The gravitational potential energy gained by the water is **$29430\ \text{J}$**.

In an IB exam, you earn one mark for the correct substitution of values and one mark for the correct final answer with units.

parts.content: Determine the useful power output of the pump.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into power formula: $P = \frac{W}{t} = \frac{29430}{50.0}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate useful power: $588.6 \text{ W}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify known variables
steps.content:
To determine the useful power, we first identify the given information from the question. We are working with mass ($m$), vertical height ($h$), and time ($t$).

- Mass of water ($m$) = $200.0\ \text{kg}$
- Height ($h$) = $15.0\ \text{m}$
- Time taken ($t$) = $50.0\ \text{s}$

This problem falls under **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

steps.title: Calculate work done
steps.content:
The work done ($W$) by the pump is equal to the change in gravitational potential energy ($\Delta E_p$) of the water as it is lifted. We use the formula from the data booklet:

$$\Delta E_p = mg\Delta h$$

Using $g = 9.81\ \text{m s}^{-2}$ (the standard value for gravitational acceleration used in the markscheme):

$$\begin{aligned} W &= 200.0 \times 9.81 \times 15.0 \\ W &= 29430\ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the work done by multiplying mass, gravity, and height.

steps.title: Calculate useful power output
steps.content:
Power is defined as the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred. 

$$P = \frac{W}{t}$$

Substitute the values for work and time:

$$\begin{aligned} P &= \frac{29430}{50.0} \\ P &= 588.6\ \text{W} \end{aligned}$$

This corresponds to the first mark (M1) for correct substitution and the final mark (A1) for the correct result.

calculator.description: Divide the total work done by the time taken.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The useful power output of the pump is:

$$P = 588.6\ \text{W}$$

steps.title: Identify the approach
steps.content:
To find the useful power output using the **Force and Velocity approach**, we need to determine the constant force required to lift the water and the constant vertical velocity at which it is moved. 

This method relates to **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**, specifically the relationship between power, force, and velocity: 

$$P = Fv$$

steps.title: Determine the force
steps.content:
The force required to lift the water at a constant speed is equal to its weight. We use the formula for weight from the **Mechanics** section of the data booklet:

$$F = mg$$

Using the mass $m = 200.0\text{ kg}$ and taking $g = 9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$ (to match the markscheme's precision):

$$F = 200.0 \times 9.81 = 1962\text{ N}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the force (weight) of the water.

steps.title: Calculate the vertical velocity
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the average vertical velocity of the water. Velocity is the displacement (height) divided by the time taken:

$$v = \frac{s}{t}$$

Given the height $h = 15.0\text{ m}$ and time $t = 50.0\text{ s}$:

$$v = \frac{15.0}{50.0} = 0.300\text{ m s}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the vertical velocity.

steps.title: Calculate useful power output
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the force and velocity into the power formula:

$$\begin{aligned} P &= F \times v \\ P &= 1962 \times 0.300 \\ P &= 588.6\text{ W} \end{aligned}$$

This confirms the result from the markscheme, where $P = \frac{W}{t} = \frac{29430}{50.0} = 588.6\text{ W}$.

calculator.description: Multiply force by velocity to find power.

parts.content: The actual power consumed by the pump is $1000\ \text{W}$. Calculate the efficiency of the pump.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into efficiency formula: $\eta = \frac{P_{\text{useful}}}{P_{\text{input}}} = \frac{588.6}{1000}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate final efficiency: $0.589 = 58.9\%$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content:
To find the efficiency, we first need to identify the energy transfers occurring. We are given:
- Mass of water ($m$) = $200.0\text{ kg}$
- Height ($h$) = $15.0\text{ m}$
- Time taken ($t$) = $50.0\text{ s}$
- Power input ($P_{\text{input}}$) = $1000\text{ W}$

This falls under **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

highlights.text: actual power consumed by the pump is 1000 W

steps.title: Calculate potential energy gain
steps.content:
The useful work done by the pump is equal to the gain in gravitational potential energy of the water. We use the formula from the data booklet:

$$\Delta E_p = mg\Delta h$$

Using $g = 9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$ (the standard value for acceleration due to gravity required to match the markscheme):

$$\Delta E_p = 200.0 \times 9.81 \times 15.0 = 29430\text{ J}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the product of mass, gravity, and height.

desmos.instructions: Enter the expression into a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the total change in potential energy.

steps.title: Determine useful power output
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the useful power output ($P_{\text{useful}}$), which is the rate at which this energy is transferred:

$$P_{\text{useful}} = \frac{\text{Energy}}{\text{time}} = \frac{\Delta E_p}{t}$$

$$P_{\text{useful}} = \frac{29430}{50.0} = 588.6\text{ W}$$

desmos.instructions: Type the division into a cell.

calculator.description: Divide the energy by the time taken to find power.

steps.title: Calculate the pump's efficiency
steps.content:
Efficiency ($\eta$) is defined as the ratio of useful power output to the total power input:

$$\text{Efficiency} = \frac{P_{\text{useful}}}{P_{\text{input}}}$$

$$\eta = \frac{588.6}{1000} = 0.5886$$

To express this as a percentage:

$$\eta = 0.5886 \times 100\% = 58.86\% \approx 58.9\%$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final efficiency ratio and percentage.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The efficiency of the pump is **$0.589$** (or **$58.9\%$**).

In an IB exam, you earn one mark (**M1**) for correctly substituting your values into the efficiency formula and one mark (**A1**) for the final correct answer.

steps.title: Identify the Energy Ratio Method
steps.content:
To calculate the efficiency using the **Energy Ratio Method**, we first need to determine the total useful work done (energy output) and the total energy consumed by the pump (energy input) over the same time period. Efficiency is the ratio of these two values.

This concept is part of **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Calculate total useful work
steps.content:
The useful work done by the pump is the gravitational potential energy gained by the water. Using the formula for potential energy:

$$\Delta E_{p} = mg\Delta h$$

Substituting the given values:
- Mass ($m$) = $200.0\ \text{kg}$
- Gravity ($g$) = $9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$
- Height ($h$) = $15.0\ \text{m}$

$$\Delta E_{p} = 200.0 \times 9.8 \times 15.0 = 29400\ \text{J}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply 200 by 9.8 and then by 15 on the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication 200 * 9.8 * 15 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 200 * 9.8 * 15 in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the total useful work (Potential Energy).

steps.title: Calculate total input energy
steps.content:
Next, we find the total energy input by multiplying the actual power consumed by the time. The pump consumes $1000\ \text{W}$ over a period of $50.0\ \text{s}$:

$$E_{\text{input}} = P_{\text{input}} \times t$$

$$E_{\text{input}} = 1000 \times 50.0 = 50000\ \text{J}$$

desmos.instructions: Type 1000 * 50 to find the total input energy.

calculator.description: Calculate the total energy input.

highlights.text: actual power consumed by the pump is 1000 W

steps.title: Calculate the final efficiency
steps.content:
Efficiency ($\eta$) is the ratio of the useful energy output to the total energy input. 

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Useful Energy Output}}{\text{Total Energy Input}}$$

$$\eta = \frac{29400}{50000} = 0.588$$

To express this as a percentage, multiply by 100:

$$\eta = 58.8\%$$

desmos.instructions: Calculate the fraction 29400 / 50000 to get the decimal efficiency.

calculator.description: Divide the useful work by the total input energy to find the efficiency.

parts.content: Explain what happens to the missing energy not used to raise the water.
parts.markscheme:
- State primary energy loss: The missing energy is lost to the surroundings, mostly as thermal energy due to internal friction in the motor or resistance in electrical wiring. <marks>A1</marks>

- State secondary energy loss: Some energy may also be lost to sound or vibration. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Energy conservation and dissipation
steps.content: In any real-world machine like a water pump, the total energy input is always greater than the useful work done. According to the principle of conservation of energy (covered in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**), energy cannot be destroyed; instead, the "missing" energy is transformed into non-useful forms and dissipated (spread out) into the surroundings.

highlights.text: missing energy not used to raise the water

steps.title: Primary loss: Thermal energy
steps.content:
The largest portion of the dissipated energy is usually converted into **thermal energy**. This happens due to two main physical mechanisms:
1. **Friction:** Moving parts within the motor and the pump rub against each other, generating heat.
2. **Electrical Resistance:** As current flows through the copper wiring of the motor, the resistance of the wires causes them to heat up (often called Joule heating).

This thermal energy is then transferred to the air and the water itself, which is not the intended purpose of the pump.

steps.title: Secondary losses: Sound and vibration
steps.content:
In addition to heat, some energy is converted into **sound energy** and **vibrations**. 
- **Sound:** The mechanical movement and air displacement caused by the motor create pressure waves we hear as noise.
- **Vibrations:** Some energy is used to cause the pump housing and its mountings to oscillate.

While these forms represent a smaller fraction of the total energy, they contribute to the overall energy "loss" from the system.

questions.specification:
A student uses an electronic timer in an attempt to estimate the acceleration of free-fall $g$.
She measures the time $t$ taken for a small metal ball to fall through a height $h$ of 0.50 m.
The percentage uncertainty in the measurement of time is 0.3% and the percentage uncertainty in height is 0.6%.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses the constant-acceleration equation h = (1/2) g t^2 for free-fall and requires rearranging for g; also part (b) concerns improving an experimental determination of g based on this kinematics relation.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Involves measurement uncertainties/percentage uncertainty propagation in an experimental estimate of g from measured h and t, which is commonly assessed alongside quantitative description of motion.

parts.content: Using $h=\frac{1}{2} g t^2$ calculate the expected percentage uncertainty in the value of $g$.
parts.markscheme: the estimated percentage uncertainty in $g$ is $2\times 0.3 + 0.6 = 1.2\%$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Rearrange the equation
steps.content:
To find the uncertainty in $g$, we first rearrange the given formula to make $g$ the subject.

Starting with:
$$h = \frac{1}{2}gt^2$$

Multiply both sides by 2:
$$2h = gt^2$$

Divide by $t^2$:
$$g = \frac{2h}{t^2}$$

steps.title: Identify percentage uncertainties
steps.content:
Next, we identify the percentage uncertainty values provided in the question for height ($h$) and time ($t$).

*   Percentage uncertainty in height ($h$): $0.6\%$
*   Percentage uncertainty in time ($t$): $0.3\%$

highlights.text: percentage uncertainty in the measurement of time is 0.3%

steps.title: Apply propagation rules
steps.content:
We apply the standard uncertainty propagation rules for multiplication, division, and powers:

1.  **Constants:** The number $2$ is an exact constant and has no uncertainty.
2.  **Powers:** For $t^2$, we multiply the percentage uncertainty of $t$ by the power $2$.
3.  **Summation:** We add the percentage uncertainties of the variables $h$ and $t^2$.

This gives us the equation:
$$\% \Delta g = \% \Delta h + 2(\% \Delta t)$$

steps.title: Calculate the final result
steps.content:
Substitute the values into our propagation equation:

$$\begin{aligned} \% \Delta g &= 0.6\% + 2(0.3\%) \\ \% \Delta g &= 0.6\% + 0.6\% \\ \% \Delta g &= 1.2\% \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the sum of the uncertainties:

ti84.instructions: Calculate the sum of the uncertainties:

desmos.instructions: Calculate the sum of the uncertainties:

calculator.description: Calculate the total percentage uncertainty

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: The expected percentage uncertainty in the value of $g$ is **$1.2\%$**.

parts.content: State and explain how the student could obtain a more reliable value for $g$.
parts.markscheme:
- use more than one height <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- obtain $g$ from a suitable graph (of height $h$ versus $t^2$) <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- $g$ is twice the gradient <marks> 1 mark </marks>

***OR***

- use a denser ball (to reduce air resistance) <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- use a (much) larger height <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- repeat many times (to get an average of time) <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Vary the height
steps.content: To improve the reliability of the result, the student should measure the time of fall $t$ for **several different heights** $h$, rather than relying on a single measurement at 0.50 m. This produces a set of data points that can be analyzed together.

steps.title: Identify the linear relationship
steps.content:
Using the kinematic equation for displacement from the data booklet, where initial velocity $u=0$, acceleration $a=g$, and displacement $s=h$:

$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2 \implies h = \frac{1}{2}gt^2$$

This equation suggests a linear relationship between height $h$ and time squared $t^2$.

steps.title: Plot a graph
steps.content: The student should plot a graph of **height $h$ on the vertical axis** versus **time squared $t^2$ on the horizontal axis**. Drawing a line of best fit through these points helps average out random errors in the individual timing measurements.

steps.title: Calculate g from the gradient
steps.content:
By comparing the experimental equation $h = \frac{1}{2}gt^2$ with the straight line equation $y = mx$, we can see that the gradient (slope) $m$ represents half of $g$.

$$\text{gradient} = \frac{g}{2}$$

Therefore, the student can calculate a more reliable value for $g$ by doubling the gradient of the line of best fit:

$$g = 2 \times \text{gradient}$$

steps.title: Analyze the experimental setup
steps.content: To obtain a more reliable value for $g$, we need to consider how to reduce uncertainties and errors in the experiment. The question states the student currently uses a single height of $0.50\text{ m}$ and measures the time once. We can improve this procedure using **Experimental Refinements** targeting random errors, percentage uncertainties, and systematic errors.

steps.title: Reduce random error by repeating
steps.content:
The first refinement is to **repeat the measurement** of time $t$ multiple times for the same height. 

By calculating the mean (average) value of $t$, you minimize the impact of random errors in the timing process, leading to a more reliable result.

steps.title: Reduce percentage uncertainty with height
steps.content:
The second refinement is to use a **much larger height** than $0.50\text{ m}$.

Using the kinematic equation from the data booklet:
$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

Since $u=0$ and $a=g$, we have $h = \frac{1}{2}gt^2$. A larger height $h$ results in a longer fall time $t$. Since the absolute uncertainty in the timer is fixed (e.g., $\pm 0.01\text{ s}$), measuring a longer time decreases the **percentage uncertainty** in $t$.

steps.title: Minimize air resistance
steps.content:
The third refinement is to use a **denser ball** (like a steel ball bearing) or an object with a smaller surface area-to-mass ratio.

This reduces the effect of **air resistance** relative to the weight of the ball, ensuring the acceleration is closer to the true free-fall value $g$ rather than being reduced by drag forces.

questions.specification: A car is travelling at a constant speed of $20 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. Its engine takes $200 \text{ kW}$ of power and the average resistive force acting on the car is $7500 \text{ N}$. What is the efficiency of the engine?

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3 Power and efficiency

questions.specification: A power station generates $2.50 \, \text{MW}$ of electrical power by burning fuel with an energy density of $45.0 \, \text{MJ kg}^{-1}$. The power station operates with an efficiency of $35.0\%$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.3 Power and efficiency
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3 Power and efficiency
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a)-(c) use power (energy per second) and efficiency to relate useful electrical output to total fuel energy input, then use energy density to find mass flow rate; part (d) asks about improving efficiency.

parts.content: Calculate the useful energy output per second.
parts.markscheme:
Correct value stated or inferred: $E_{\text{useful}} = 2.50 \, \text{MJ} = 2.50 \times 10^{6} \, \text{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Define power and energy
steps.content:
To solve this, we first need to recall the relationship between power and energy. Power ($P$) is defined as the rate at which energy ($E$) is transferred or transformed over time ($t$). 

This is covered in **Topic A.3.3 Power and efficiency** of your syllabus. The relationship is expressed as:

$$P = \frac{E}{t}$$

steps.title: Identify useful power output
steps.content:
The question states that the power station generates $2.50 \text{ MW}$ of electrical power. In the context of a power station, the electrical power produced is the "useful" part of the energy conversion process.

Therefore, the useful power output is:

$$P_{\text{useful}} = 2.50 \text{ MW}$$

steps.title: Calculate energy per second
steps.content:
Since we want to find the energy output **per second**, we set the time interval to $t = 1 \text{ s}$. 

Using the rearranged formula $E = P \times t$:

$$\begin{aligned} E_{\text{useful}} &= P_{\text{useful}} \times t \\ E_{\text{useful}} &= 2.50 \times 10^6 \text{ W} \times 1 \text{ s} \\ E_{\text{useful}} &= 2.50 \times 10^6 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

Note that $1 \text{ MW} = 10^6 \text{ W}$, and $1 \text{ W} = 1 \text{ J s}^{-1}$.

gdc.instructions: Multiply the power (in Watts) by the time (1 second).

ti84.instructions: Multiply the power (in Watts) by the time (1 second).

desmos.instructions: Multiply the power (in Watts) by the time (1 second).

calculator.description: Verify the energy calculation for a 1-second interval.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The useful energy output per second is $2.50 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$, which can also be written as $2.50 \text{ MJ}$.

According to the markscheme, stating either form is correct for the 1 mark available.

$$E_{\text{useful}} = 2.50 \text{ MJ}$$

parts.content: Determine the total energy input per second from the fuel.
parts.markscheme:
Correct rearrangement and substitution: $\eta = \frac{E_{\text{useful}}}{E_{\text{input}}} \Rightarrow E_{\text{input}} = \frac{2.50 \times 10^{6}}{0.35}$ <marks>M1</marks>

Correct final answer with unit: $E_{\text{input}} = 7.14 \times 10^6 \, \text{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Calculate the mass of fuel burned per second.
parts.markscheme:
Correct formula and substitution: $\text{mass} = \frac{E_{\text{input}}}{\text{energy density}} = \frac{7.14 \times 10^{6}}{45.0 \times 10^6}$ <marks>M1</marks>

Correct final answer with unit: $\text{mass} = 0.159 \, \text{kg}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Convert units to SI
steps.content:
Before we begin, we should convert the given values into standard SI units (Watts and Joules) to ensure our calculations are consistent.

- Useful output power ($P_{\text{out}}$): $2.50 \text{ MW} = 2.50 \times 10^6 \text{ W}$
- Energy density: $45.0 \text{ MJ kg}^{-1} = 45.0 \times 10^6 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$
- Efficiency ($\eta$): $35.0\% = 0.350$

This relates to **Topic A.3 Work, energy and power**, specifically the definitions of power and efficiency.

steps.title: Calculate total input power
steps.content:
Using the sequential approach, we first find the total power input ($P_{\text{in}}$) required from the fuel. Efficiency is the ratio of useful output to total input.

$$\text{efficiency} = \frac{\text{useful power output}}{\text{total power input}}$$

Rearranging to solve for $P_{\text{in}}$:

$$P_{\text{in}} = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{\eta} = \frac{2.50 \times 10^6 \text{ W}}{0.350}$$

$$P_{\text{in}} = 7.14 \times 10^6 \text{ W}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the total power input by dividing the output power by the efficiency.

steps.title: Calculate mass burned per second
steps.content:
The input power is the energy released per second ($P_{\text{in}} = \frac{\text{Energy}}{\text{time}}$). We can find the mass of fuel burned per second ($m$) by dividing this power by the energy density of the fuel.

$$\text{mass per second} = \frac{\text{Power input}}{\text{Energy density}}$$

$$\text{mass} = \frac{7.14 \times 10^6 \text{ J s}^{-1}}{45.0 \times 10^6 \text{ J kg}^{-1}}$$

$$\text{mass} = 0.159 \text{ kg}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of fuel by dividing the input power by the energy density.

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
The mass of fuel burned per second is **$0.159 \text{ kg}$**.

In an IB exam, you earn marks as follows:
- **M1**: Correct substitution into the mass formula using the calculated input power and energy density.
- **A1**: The correct final answer with appropriate units ($0.159 \text{ kg}$).

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
First, let's list the information given in the question and convert everything to standard SI units. We are dealing with concepts from **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

- Useful power output ($P_{\text{out}}$): $2.50 \text{ MW} = 2.50 \times 10^6 \text{ J s}^{-1}$
- Energy density ($\rho_E$): $45.0 \text{ MJ kg}^{-1} = 45.0 \times 10^6 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$
- Efficiency ($\eta$): $35.0\% = 0.35$

steps.title: Create single algebraic expression
steps.content:
To find the mass of fuel per second ($\dot{m}$), we can combine the efficiency and energy density formulas into one step.

Efficiency is defined as:
$$\eta = \frac{\text{Useful power output}}{\text{Total power input}} = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}$$

The total power input ($P_{\text{in}}$) is the energy released by the fuel per second, which depends on the mass burned per second and its energy density:
$$P_{\text{in}} = \dot{m} \times \rho_E$$

Substituting $P_{\text{in}}$ into the efficiency formula and solving for mass per second ($\dot{m}$), we get:
$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{\eta \times \rho_E}$$

steps.title: Calculate the mass flow
steps.content:
Now, we substitute our values into the combined expression. Notice that the $10^6$ factors in the power and the energy density will cancel each other out, simplifying the calculation.

$$\dot{m} = \frac{2.50 \times 10^6}{0.35 \times 45.0 \times 10^6} = \frac{2.50}{0.35 \times 45.0}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression into the calculation window: 2.50 / (0.35 * 45.0).

ti84.instructions: Enter the values into the main screen. Use parentheses to ensure the denominator is calculated first: 2.50 / (0.35 * 45.0).

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar. Desmos will calculate the fraction automatically.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of fuel burned per second using the derived formula.

steps.title: State the final result
steps.content:
Using our calculation, we find the mass flow rate. Following the IB convention for significant figures, since the given values have three significant figures, we should provide our answer to three significant figures.

$$\dot{m} \approx 0.15873... \text{ kg s}^{-1}$$

Rounding to three significant figures gives:
$$\text{mass} = 0.159 \text{ kg}$$

steps.title: Convert units to standard form
steps.content:
First, let's identify our known values and convert them to standard SI units (Joules and Watts) to ensure our calculation is accurate.

- Electrical power output: $2.50 \, \text{MW} = 2.50 \times 10^6 \, \text{J s}^{-1}$
- Energy density: $45.0 \, \text{MJ kg}^{-1} = 45.0 \times 10^6 \, \text{J kg}^{-1}$
- Efficiency: $35.0\% = 0.35$

This problem involves concepts from **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**, specifically efficiency and energy transformations.

steps.title: Calculate useful energy per kilogram
steps.content:
Using the **Useful Energy per Unit Mass** approach, we first determine how much of the energy in each kilogram of fuel actually turns into electricity. We do this by multiplying the total energy density by the efficiency:

$$\text{Useful energy per kg} = \text{energy density} \times \text{efficiency}$$
$$\text{Useful energy per kg} = 45.0 \times 10^6 \, \text{J kg}^{-1} \times 0.35$$
$$\text{Useful energy per kg} = 15.75 \times 10^6 \, \text{J kg}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the useful energy available per kilogram of fuel.

steps.title: Determine mass burned per second
steps.content:
Now, we know the power station needs to output $2.50 \times 10^6 \, \text{Joules}$ every second. To find the mass required to produce this, we divide the total required power by the useful energy produced by one kilogram:

$$\text{mass per second} = \frac{\text{Power output}}{\text{Useful energy per kg}}$$
$$\text{mass per second} = \frac{2.50 \times 10^6 \, \text{J s}^{-1}}{15.75 \times 10^6 \, \text{J kg}^{-1}}$$
$$\text{mass per second} \approx 0.1587 \, \text{kg s}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Divide the power output by the useful energy per kilogram.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Rounding our result to three significant figures (as per the given data), we get:

$$\text{mass} = 0.159 \, \text{kg}$$

In an exam, you earn one mark for the correct substitution of values and one mark for the final answer with the correct unit.

questions.specification:
A block of mass $4.0 \, \mathrm{kg}$ is pushed from rest up a ramp of length $5.0 \, \mathrm{m}$ to a vertical height of $3.0 \, \mathrm{m}$ by a constant force of $40 \, \mathrm{N}$ acting parallel to the ramp surface. The block reaches the top of the ramp with a speed of $4.0 \, \mathrm{m\,s}^{-1}$. 

Assume $g=10 \, \mathrm{m\,s}^{-2}$. What is the work done against friction as the block moves up the ramp?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Determining work done against friction using applied force over distance, gain in gravitational potential energy (mgh), and change in kinetic energy is an application of work-energy transfer.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.1 Conservation of energy
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.1 Conservation of energy
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses energy conservation/energy accounting: input work equals increase in mechanical energy plus energy dissipated by friction.

options.markscheme:
Work done by the applied force:
$$W_{\text{applied}} = Fs = 40 \times 5.0 = 200 \, \mathrm{J}$$

Change in mechanical energy:
$$\begin{aligned} \Delta E &= \Delta E_p + \Delta E_k \\ &= mgh + \frac{1}{2}mv^2 \\ &= (4.0)(10)(3.0) + \frac{1}{2}(4.0)(4.0^2) \\ &= 120 + 32 = 152 \, \mathrm{J} \end{aligned}$$

Conservation of energy for the system:
$$\begin{aligned} W_{\text{applied}} &= \Delta E + W_{\text{friction}} \\ 200 &= 152 + W_{\text{friction}} \\ W_{\text{friction}} &= 48 \, \mathrm{J} \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: State the formula linking power, force, and velocity.
parts.markscheme:
- State the formula: $P = Fv$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the relevant variables
steps.content:
The question asks for a relationship between three specific physical quantities mentioned in the scenario: 
- **Power** ($P$)
- **Force** ($F$)
- **Velocity** ($v$)

These concepts are studied in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

steps.title: Recall the power formula
steps.content:
In the IB Physics curriculum, when an object moves at a constant velocity $v$ against a constant resistive force $F$, the power $P$ delivered is given by the product of force and velocity.

The formula is:

$$P = Fv$$

ti84.instructions: Divide the power (in Watts) by the velocity to find the force.

calculator.description: Verify the relationship using the values provided in the question ($P = 20.0\text{ kW}$, $v = 25.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$) to find the force.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The markscheme requires the direct statement of this formula to earn the mark.

$$P = Fv$$

steps.title: Define Power
steps.content:
Power is defined as the rate at which work is done over time. This concept is covered in **Topic A.3.3: Power and efficiency**. We can express this using the formula:

$$P = \frac{W}{t}$$

where $P$ is power, $W$ is work done, and $t$ is time.

steps.title: Express Work done
steps.content:
Next, we look at the definition of work. From the Mechanics section of your data booklet, work done is the product of force and displacement in the direction of the force:

$$W = Fs \cos\theta$$

Assuming the force and the motion of the car are in the same direction ($\\theta = 0^{\circ}$), this simplifies to:

$$W = Fs$$

Substituting this into our power equation gives:

$$P = \frac{Fs}{t}$$

steps.title: Substitute for Velocity
steps.content:
Recall from **Topic A.1.1** that average velocity ($v$) is defined as displacement ($s$) divided by time ($t$):

$$v = \frac{s}{t}$$

We can see the term $\frac{s}{t}$ appearing in our power expression from the previous step.

steps.title: State final formula
steps.content:
By replacing $\frac{s}{t}$ with $v$ in our equation, we derive the relationship required by the question:

$$P = Fv$$

This formula is extremely useful when calculating power for objects moving at a constant speed against a constant resistive force.

parts.content: Calculate the resistive force acting on the car.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to substitute values into the formula: $F = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{20000}{25.0}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Correct answer with unit: $800\ \text{N}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Explain why the car is not accelerating despite the engine doing work.
parts.markscheme:
- The engine's force balances the resistive forces, hence the net force is zero and speed is constant <marks>R1</marks>

1 mark total

methods.label: Newton's First Law and Force Equilibrium

steps.title: Identify the state of motion
steps.content: The question specifies that the car is moving at a **constant speed**. In physics, moving at a constant speed in a straight line means the object has a constant velocity, which implies that its acceleration is zero.

steps.title: Apply Newton's First Law
steps.content:
According to **Newton's First Law**, an object will continue to move at a constant velocity if the net (resultant) force acting on it is zero. While the engine does work to provide a forward driving force, the car also experiences resistive forces, such as air resistance and friction, acting in the opposite direction.

For the speed to remain constant, these forces must be in **equilibrium**:

$$\text{Driving Force} = \text{Resistive Forces}$$

steps.title: Relate to Newton's Second Law
steps.content:
Using the formula from the data booklet for Newton's Second Law:

$$F = ma$$

Since the driving force and the resistive forces are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction, the net force ($F_{net}$) is zero. If the net force is zero, the acceleration ($a$) must also be zero, regardless of how much work the engine is doing to overcome those resistances.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content: The engine does work to maintain the car's speed against friction and air resistance. Because the engine's force perfectly balances these resistive forces, the resultant force is zero, which is why the car does not accelerate.

steps.title: Apply the Work-Energy Theorem
steps.content:
The Work-Energy Theorem states that the net (total) work done on an object is equal to its change in kinetic energy ($E_k$):

$$W_{\text{net}} = \Delta E_k$$

For a car to accelerate, its kinetic energy must increase, which requires the net work done on it to be greater than zero. This concept is found in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

steps.title: Balance work and dissipation
steps.content:
Although the engine is doing positive work to move the car forward, resistive forces like air resistance and friction are doing an equal amount of negative work. 

In this case, the work done by the engine is entirely dissipated by the work done against these resistive forces. Therefore, the net work ($W_{\text{net}}$) acting on the car is zero.

steps.title: Connect to zero acceleration
steps.content:
Because the net work is zero, there is no change in the car's kinetic energy ($ \Delta E_k = 0 $). Since kinetic energy is constant, the car moves at a constant speed and its acceleration is zero.

In the IB markscheme, this is often expressed as the engine's force balancing the resistive forces, leading to a net force of zero.

questions.specification:
A student investigates the principle of conservation of energy using a small ball rolling down a ramp. The ball starts at rest from different heights and rolls down the ramp onto a flat surface. The student measures the speed of the ball at the bottom of the ramp using a motion sensor.

The height $h$, the mass $m$ of the ball, and the velocity $v$ at the bottom are recorded. The following data are provided:

| Height $h$ / m | Velocity $v$ / $\mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ | Kinetic Energy $\text{KE}$ / J | Gravitational Potential Energy $\text{GPE}$ / J |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0.20 | 1.50 |  |  |
| 0.40 | 2.10 |  |  |
| 0.60 | 2.50 |  |  |
| 0.80 | 2.80 |  |  |

The mass of the ball is $0.50 \mathrm{~kg}$, and $g=9.81 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.1 Conservation of energy
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.1 Conservation of energy
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The experiment and all parts focus on testing mechanical energy conservation by comparing GPE (mgh) at different heights with KE (½mv²) at the bottom, and discussing reasons for non-conservation (losses).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) asks for a source of energy loss (e.g., friction/air resistance/sound/thermal) and how to reduce it, which involves energy transfer out of the mechanical system via non-conservative work.

parts.content: Using expressions of kinetic and potential energy, explain how the principle of conservation of energy can be tested in this experiment.
parts.markscheme:
- State the expressions for potential and kinetic energy: $\text{GPE}=m g h, \quad \text{KE}=\frac{1}{2} m v^2$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Explanation of conservation principle: The $\text{GPE}$ of the ball at the top should be fully converted into $\text{KE}$ at the bottom of the ramp (assuming no energy losses). By comparing the calculated $\text{GPE}$ and $\text{KE}$, we can test if energy is conserved in the system. <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: State energy formulas
steps.content:
First, we identify the relevant energy formulas from the **Mechanics** section of the data booklet. 

The gravitational potential energy (GPE) is determined by the height $h$, and the kinetic energy (KE) is determined by the velocity $v$.

steps.title: Calculate energies separately
steps.content:
To test the principle using the **Comparing Calculated Energy Values** approach, we must calculate the energy at the top and the energy at the bottom independently for each data point.

*   **At the top:** The ball is at rest, so we calculate the initial Potential Energy using $$ \text{GPE} = mgh $$ where $m$ is mass, $g$ is gravity, and $h$ is the starting height.
*   **At the bottom:** The height is zero, so we calculate the final Kinetic Energy using $$ \text{KE} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 $$ where $v$ is the measured velocity.

steps.title: Compare the calculated values
steps.content:
Finally, we compare these two calculated values.

The principle of conservation of energy states that energy is neither created nor destroyed, only transferred. In this experiment, the GPE lost should be transformed into KE gained (assuming negligible friction).

Therefore, if the calculated values are approximately equal ($$ mgh \approx \frac{1}{2}mv^2 $$), the principle of conservation of energy is verified.

steps.title: State energy expressions
steps.content:
First, we identify the relevant energy formulas from the **Mechanics** section of the data booklet. The gravitational potential energy (GPE) lost by the ball and the kinetic energy (KE) gained are given by:

$$E_{k}=\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$$ and $$\Delta E_{p}=mg\Delta h$$

where $m$ is mass, $v$ is velocity, $g$ is acceleration due to gravity, and $h$ is height.

steps.title: Apply conservation principle
steps.content:
According to the principle of conservation of energy, if we assume no energy is lost to friction or air resistance, the gravitational potential energy at the top of the ramp should be fully converted into kinetic energy at the bottom.

We can write this relationship as:

$$\text{GPE lost} = \text{KE gained}$$
$$mgh = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

steps.title: Derive linear relationship
steps.content:
To verify this experimentally using a graph, we rearrange the equation to find a linear relationship between the measured variables $v$ and $h$. Notice that the mass $m$ cancels out:

$$\begin{aligned} gh &= \frac{1}{2}v^2 \\ v^2 &= 2gh \end{aligned}$$

This equation is in the form of a straight line $$y = mx + c$$, where $y = v^2$ and $x = h$.

steps.title: Describe graphical test
steps.content:
To test the conservation of energy:

1. Plot a graph with **$v^2$ on the y-axis** and **$h$ on the x-axis**.
2. If the principle of conservation holds, the data points should form a **straight line passing through the origin**.
3. The gradient of this line should theoretically be equal to $$2g$$.

parts.content: Complete the table by calculating the $\text{KE}$ and $\text{GPE}$ values for each height. State whether mechanical energy is conserved in this experiment.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate $\text{GPE}$ and $\text{KE}$ for $h=0.20$: $\text{GPE}=(0.50)(9.81)(0.20)=0.981 \mathrm{~J}$ and $\text{KE}=\frac{1}{2}(0.50)(1.50)^2=0.563 \mathrm{~J}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Correct values for all other heights:
  $h=0.40$: $\text{GPE}=1.962 \mathrm{~J}, \text{KE}=1.103 \mathrm{~J}$
  $h=0.60$: $\text{GPE}=2.943 \mathrm{~J}, \text{KE}=1.563 \mathrm{~J}$
  $h=0.80$: $\text{GPE}=3.924 \mathrm{~J}, \text{KE}=1.960 \mathrm{~J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- State the conclusion: The $\text{KE}$ values are consistently lower than the $\text{GPE}$ values, indicating energy is not conserved (energy is lost). <marks>R1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify relevant energy formulas
steps.content:
To investigate the conservation of energy, we need to calculate the Gravitational Potential Energy (GPE) lost and the Kinetic Energy (KE) gained for each height. We use the formulas from the **Mechanics** section of the data booklet:

$$ \Delta E_p = mg\Delta h $$
$$ E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 $$

Where:
- Mass $m = 0.50 \text{ kg}$
- Acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81 \text{ ms}^{-2}$

steps.title: Calculate for height 0.20 m
steps.content:
For the first row, $h = 0.20 \text{ m}$ and $v = 1.50 \text{ ms}^{-1}$.

Calculate GPE:
$$ \text{GPE} = (0.50)(9.81)(0.20) = 0.981 \text{ J} $$

Calculate KE:
$$ \text{KE} = \frac{1}{2}(0.50)(1.50)^2 = 0.563 \text{ J} $$

ti84.instructions: Enter the expressions for GPE and KE:

calculator.description: Calculate GPE and KE for h=0.20

steps.title: Calculate for height 0.40 m
steps.content:
For the second row, $h = 0.40 \text{ m}$ and $v = 2.10 \text{ ms}^{-1}$.

Calculate GPE:
$$ \text{GPE} = (0.50)(9.81)(0.40) = 1.962 \text{ J} $$

Calculate KE:
$$ \text{KE} = \frac{1}{2}(0.50)(2.10)^2 \approx 1.103 \text{ J} $$

calculator.description: Calculate GPE and KE for h=0.40

steps.title: Calculate for height 0.60 m
steps.content:
For the third row, $h = 0.60 \text{ m}$ and $v = 2.50 \text{ ms}^{-1}$.

Calculate GPE:
$$ \text{GPE} = (0.50)(9.81)(0.60) = 2.943 \text{ J} $$

Calculate KE:
$$ \text{KE} = \frac{1}{2}(0.50)(2.50)^2 \approx 1.563 \text{ J} $$

calculator.description: Calculate GPE and KE for h=0.60

steps.title: Calculate for height 0.80 m
steps.content:
For the fourth row, $h = 0.80 \text{ m}$ and $v = 2.80 \text{ ms}^{-1}$.

Calculate GPE:
$$ \text{GPE} = (0.50)(9.81)(0.80) = 3.924 \text{ J} $$

Calculate KE:
$$ \text{KE} = \frac{1}{2}(0.50)(2.80)^2 = 1.960 \text{ J} $$

calculator.description: Calculate GPE and KE for h=0.80

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
Comparing the values, we can see that the Kinetic Energy at the bottom is consistently lower than the Gravitational Potential Energy at the start for all heights.

- GPE: 0.981 J, 1.962 J, 2.943 J, 3.924 J
- KE: 0.563 J, 1.103 J, 1.563 J, 1.960 J

**Conclusion:** Mechanical energy is **not conserved** in this experiment because significant energy is lost (likely due to friction and air resistance).

parts.content: The student notices that the calculated $\text{KE}$ values are slightly less than the corresponding $\text{GPE}$ values. Suggest one source of energy loss in the experiment and describe how the setup could be modified to reduce this energy loss.
parts.markscheme:
- State one source of energy loss: Friction between the ball and the ramp OR air resistance. <marks>A1</marks>

- Describe a modification: Use a smoother ramp surface (to reduce friction) OR use a denser ball/vacuum chamber (to reduce air resistance effects). <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze energy discrepancy
steps.content: The question states that the Kinetic Energy (KE) at the bottom is less than the initial Gravitational Potential Energy (GPE). According to the principle of conservation of energy (Topic A.3), energy cannot be destroyed, only transformed. This missing energy implies that work was done against resistive forces.

highlights.text: calculated $\text{KE}$ values are slightly less than the corresponding $\text{GPE}$ values

steps.title: Identify friction as source
steps.content: Using the **Friction and Surface Modification** approach, we identify the primary resistive force as **friction** between the ball and the ramp. As the ball rolls, non-conservative work is done against friction, which converts mechanical energy into thermal energy (heat). This explains why the final kinetic energy is lower than expected.

steps.title: Suggest surface modification
steps.content: To minimize the energy loss caused by friction, we need to reduce the roughness of the contact surfaces. A practical modification is to use a **smoother ramp surface**. For example, replacing a rough wooden ramp with a polished wood, glass, or plastic surface would significantly decrease the coefficient of friction.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Based on the analysis above, the required answer is:

**Source of energy loss:** Friction between the ball and the ramp.

**Modification:** Use a smoother ramp surface (or apply a lubricant) to reduce the frictional force.

steps.title: Analyze the energy difference
steps.content: The student found that the final kinetic energy (KE) is less than the initial gravitational potential energy (GPE). This indicates that the system is not perfectly efficient and some mechanical energy has been "lost" (transferred) to the surroundings during the descent.

highlights.text: calculated KE values are slightly less than the corresponding GPE values

steps.title: Identify source of loss
steps.content:
Using the **Air Resistance and Aerodynamics** perspective, we identify the interaction between the moving ball and the air.

As the ball moves, it collides with air molecules, creating a resistive force known as **air resistance** (or drag). This force acts in the opposite direction to the velocity, doing negative work on the ball and dissipating some energy as heat.

steps.title: Suggest a modification
steps.content:
To minimize the energy lost to air resistance, you can modify the experiment in one of the following ways:

1.  **Use a denser ball:** For a ball of the same size, a denser material (like steel) increases the mass and gravitational force without increasing the air drag. This makes the energy lost to air resistance a much smaller percentage of the total energy.
2.  **Use a vacuum chamber:** Removing the air entirely eliminates air resistance.

(Note: This relates to **Topic A.1.4 Fluid resistance** and **A.3 Work, energy and power**.)

parts.content: Calculate the work done by the student on the crate.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into work formula: $W = 100 \times 5.0$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate final answer with units: $500 \text{ J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
First, let's identify the values provided in the question. We are given the force applied by the student and the displacement of the crate.

*   Force ($F$) = $100\ \text{N}$
*   Displacement ($s$) = $5.0\ \text{m}$

steps.title: Select the work formula
steps.content:
To calculate the work done, we use the formula for work from the **Mechanics** section of your IB data booklet:

$$W = Fs \cos\theta$$

In this scenario, the student is pushing the crate across the floor. Since the force is applied in the same direction as the motion, the angle $\theta$ is $0^\circ$. Recall that $\cos(0^\circ) = 1$, so the formula simplifies to:

$$W = Fs$$

steps.title: Substitute and calculate
steps.content:
Now, we substitute our known values into the equation to find the total work done. This step usually earns the first mark (M1) in an IB exam.

$$\begin{aligned} W &= 100 \times 5.0 \\ W &= 500 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the work done by multiplying force and displacement.

steps.title: Final answer with units
steps.content: The work done by the student is **$500\ \text{J}$** (Joules). Always remember to include the correct units to secure your final mark (A1)!

steps.title: Relate work to area
steps.content: In physics, the work done on an object can be determined by finding the **area under the curve** of a Force–displacement ($F$ vs. $s$) graph. This is a key concept from **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

steps.title: Visualize the graph
steps.content:
Since the student applies a constant force of $100\text{ N}$ over a distance of $5.0\text{ m}$, the graph of Force versus displacement is a horizontal line. 

On this graph, we have:
- A height of $100\text{ N}$ (the force)
- A width of $5.0\text{ m}$ (the displacement)

The area under this line forms a **rectangle**.

steps.title: Calculate the area
steps.content:
To find the work done, we calculate the area of this rectangle using the formula:

$$\text{Area} = \text{height} \times \text{base}$$
$$\text{Work} = 100\text{ N} \times 5.0\text{ m}$$

Substituting these values is the first step toward earning marks in an IB exam.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the product of force and displacement to find the work done.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The area, and thus the work done, is $500$. Since work is a form of energy transfer, the unit is Joules ($\text{J}$).

$$\text{Work} = 500\text{ J}$$

In the markscheme, you receive **1 mark** for the correct substitution and **1 mark** for the final value with the correct units.

parts.content: State what happens to the energy transferred by the student.
parts.markscheme:
- State the energy transfer: The energy is transferred as thermal energy (heat) due to friction <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze energy changes
steps.content:
To understand where the energy goes, we first look at the mechanical energy of the crate. 

1. Since the crate moves at a **constant speed**, its kinetic energy ($E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$) does not change.
2. Since it moves along a **horizontal floor**, its gravitational potential energy ($E_p = mgh$) does not change.

Because there is no increase in mechanical energy, all the work done by the student must be transferred into another form.

steps.title: Consider work and friction
steps.content:
The student performs work on the crate using a force of $100\text{ N}$. You can find the formula for work in section **A.3 Work, energy and power** of your data booklet:

$$W = Fs \cos \theta$$

However, the floor is **rough**, meaning friction is acting against the motion. Since the velocity is constant, the net force is zero, which means the student's force is exactly balanced by the frictional force.

steps.title: Identify the energy transfer
steps.content:
According to the energy balance approach, the work done by the student against the resistive force of friction is converted into internal energy. 

As the surfaces rub together, the molecules in the crate and the floor vibrate more vigorously, which we observe as a rise in temperature. Therefore, the energy is transferred as **thermal energy (heat)**.

steps.title: Analyze the motion
steps.content:
The question states that the crate is moving at a **constant speed**. This is a key detail from **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**. 

According to Newton's First Law, if the speed is constant, the net force acting on the crate must be zero. This means the force applied by the student ($100\text{ N}$) is exactly balanced by a resistive force of equal magnitude.

steps.title: Identify the resistive force
steps.content:
Since the crate is moving across a **rough horizontal floor**, the resistive force opposing the motion is **friction**. 

As the student performs work to push the crate, they are not increasing the crate's kinetic energy (because the speed isn't changing). Instead, all the work done by the student is used to overcome the friction between the crate and the floor.

steps.title: Determine energy dissipation
steps.content:
In physics, when work is done against friction, the energy doesn't just disappear—it changes form. 

Following the **Friction Dissipation Approach**, the mechanical energy transferred by the student is dissipated as **thermal energy** (heat). This thermal energy causes a small increase in the temperature of both the crate and the floor surface.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you must state that the energy is transferred into the thermal energy of the surroundings due to the work done against friction.

**Final Answer:**
The energy is transferred as thermal energy (heat) to the crate and the floor due to friction.

parts.content: Identify the type of energy transformation taking place during the motion.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the transformation: Mechanical energy is transformed into internal (thermal) energy <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze kinetic and potential energy
steps.content:
To understand the energy transformation, we first look at the state of the crate. The question states that the crate is moving at a **constant speed**. Referring to the kinetic energy formula in the data booklet:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

Since the speed ($v$) is constant, there is no change in kinetic energy ($\Delta E_k = 0$). Furthermore, the crate moves across a **horizontal floor**, meaning the height does not change. From the potential energy formula:

$$\Delta E_p = mg\Delta h$$

Since $\Delta h = 0$, there is no change in gravitational potential energy ($\Delta E_p = 0$).

steps.title: Apply Work-Energy Balance
steps.content:
The student performs work ($W$) on the crate by applying a force over a distance. The work-energy balance tells us that this work input must result in a change in the system's energy. 

$$\text{Work Done} = \Delta E_k + \Delta E_p + \Delta \text{Internal Energy}$$

Since we established that $\Delta E_k = 0$ and $\Delta E_p = 0$, all the mechanical work done by the student is being used to overcome the friction of the **rough** floor.

steps.title: Identify the energy transformation
steps.content:
When work is done against friction, the energy does not disappear; instead, it is transferred into the random microscopic motion of the atoms in the crate and the floor. This is known as **internal energy** (or thermal energy).

Therefore, the transformation is: **Mechanical energy is transformed into internal (thermal) energy.**

steps.title: Analyze the motion
steps.content:
The question states the crate moves at a **constant speed**. This is a key piece of information from **Topic A.1 (Kinematics)**. 

If the speed is constant, the kinetic energy ($E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$) of the crate does not change. Since the floor is horizontal, the gravitational potential energy ($E_p = mgh$) also remains constant. This means the work done by the student is not being stored as mechanical energy in the crate.

steps.title: Identify the role of friction
steps.content:
We are told the crate is pushed across a **rough horizontal floor**. In physics, a "rough" surface implies that there is a significant frictional force opposing the motion. 

Because the crate moves at a constant speed, Newton's First Law tells us the net force is zero. Therefore, the $100\text{ N}$ force applied by the student is exactly balanced by the frictional force. The student is doing work specifically to overcome this friction.

steps.title: Determine the energy transformation
steps.content:
Using the formula for work from the data booklet:

$$W = Fs \cos \theta$$

The student performs mechanical work on the system. Based on the **Friction-Based Reasoning** approach, when work is done against friction, the energy is not "lost" but is converted into different forms. 

Friction causes the molecules in the crate and the floor to vibrate more vigorously, which increases the **internal energy** (or thermal energy) of the system. This is often noticed as a rise in temperature.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The mechanical work done by the student is transformed into internal energy of the crate and the floor.

**Energy Transformation:** Mechanical energy $\rightarrow$ Internal (thermal) energy.

parts.content: Calculate the gravitational potential energy gained by the box.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct substitution: $E_p = 10.0 \times 9.81 \times 2.00$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct answer: $196\ \text{J}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the given values
steps.content:
First, let's list the information provided in the question. We are given the mass of the box, the height it is lifted, and the gravitational field strength.

- Mass ($m$) = $10.0\ \text{kg}$
- Height change ($\Delta h$) = $2.00\ \text{m}$
- Acceleration due to gravity ($g$) = $9.81\ \text{m s}^{-2}$

highlights.text: acceleration due to gravity is 9.81 m s^{-2}

steps.title: Select the correct formula
steps.content:
To find the gravitational potential energy gained, we use the formula for change in potential energy from the **Mechanics** section of your IB Data Booklet:

$$\Delta E_{p}=mg\Delta h$$

steps.title: Calculate the energy gain
steps.content:
Substitute the known values into the equation to calculate the energy. 

$$\Delta E_{p} = 10.0 \times 9.81 \times 2.00$$

According to the markscheme, you earn 1 mark for this correct substitution.

gdc.instructions: Multiply the three values in the calculation app.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the product of mass, gravity, and height.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Performing the calculation gives us $196.2\ \text{J}$. 

Since our input values ($10.0$, $9.81$, and $2.00$) all have three significant figures, we should round our final answer to three significant figures.

$$\Delta E_{p} = 196\ \text{J}$$

steps.title: Link work and energy
steps.content: To find the gravitational potential energy gained, we can calculate the work done by the student. Since the box is lifted at a constant speed, the work done by the lifting force is exactly equal to the change in gravitational potential energy ($ΔE_p$). This concept is covered in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

steps.title: Calculate the lifting force
steps.content:
Because the box moves at a constant speed, it is in translational equilibrium. This means the upward lifting force ($F$) applied by the student must be equal in magnitude to the downward weight of the box ($mg$).

Using the formula for weight from the **Mechanics** section:

$$F = mg$$

Substituting the given values:

$$F = 10.0 \times 9.81 = 98.1\ \text{N}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the lifting force (weight).

steps.title: Calculate work done
steps.content:
Now we use the definition of work done from the data booklet. Since the force and displacement are in the same direction, the angle $\theta$ is $0^\circ$ and $\cos(0^\circ) = 1$.

$$W = Fs\cos\theta$$

Substituting our force and the height ($s = 2.00\ \text{m}$):

$$W = 98.1 \times 2.00 \times 1 = 196.2\ \text{J}$$

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The work done by the student is the energy transferred to the box's potential energy store. 

Rounding to three significant figures (as per the input values $10.0$, $9.81$, and $2.00$):

$$\Delta E_p = 196\ \text{J}$$

steps.title: Identify the physics concepts
steps.content:
To solve this using the Work-Force relationship, we need to consider the force the student applies to the box and the distance it moves. This problem falls under **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

From the question, we have:
- Mass ($m$) = $10.0\text{ kg}$
- Height/Displacement ($s$) = $2.00\text{ m}$
- Acceleration due to gravity ($g$) = $9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$

steps.title: Calculate the upward force
steps.content:
The question states the box is lifted at a **constant speed**. This means the acceleration is zero, so the upward force ($F$) applied by the student must exactly equal the downward weight of the box ($mg$). 

Using the force formula from the data booklet:
$$\begin{aligned} F &= mg \\ F &= 10.0 \times 9.81 \\ F &= 98.1\text{ N} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply the mass by the acceleration due to gravity.

calculator.description: Calculate the weight (force) of the box.

steps.title: Calculate the work done
steps.content:
Work is defined as the force multiplied by the displacement in the direction of the force. Since the student lifts the box upward with a force of $98.1\text{ N}$ over a distance of $2.00\text{ m}$, we use the work formula:

$$\begin{aligned} W &= Fs \\ W &= 98.1 \times 2.00 \\ W &= 196.2\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply the force result by the distance.

calculator.description: Calculate the work done by multiplying force and displacement.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
To maintain the correct precision (3 significant figures based on the input values), we round the result to $196\text{ J}$.

$$W = 196\text{ J}$$

steps.title: Relate work to potential energy
steps.content:
When an object is lifted at a **constant speed**, its kinetic energy remains unchanged. According to the principle of conservation of energy, the work done by the student ($W$) is equal to the change in the gravitational potential energy ($\Delta E_p$) of the box.

$$W = \Delta E_p$$

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To calculate the change in gravitational potential energy, we use the formula found in the **Mechanics** section of your IB Physics Data Booklet:

$$\Delta E_p = mg\Delta h$$

steps.title: Calculate the work done
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the values provided in the question:
- Mass ($m$) = $10.0\ \text{kg}$
- Acceleration due to gravity ($g$) = $9.81\ \text{m s}^{-2}$
- Change in height ($\Delta h$) = $2.00\ \text{m}$

$$\begin{aligned} W &= (10.0) \times (9.81) \times (2.00) \\ W &= 196.2\ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures (as given in the question), the work done is $196\ \text{J}$.

parts.content: Explain why the work done by the student is equal to the gain in gravitational potential energy.
parts.markscheme:
- The force applied is equal and opposite to weight. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Therefore, all the work goes into increasing gravitational potential energy. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the motion
steps.content:
To understand why the work done is equal to the potential energy gain, we first look at the condition of the lift. The box is being moved at a **constant speed**.

In physics, if an object moves at a constant speed in a straight line, its acceleration ($a$) is zero. According to Newton's Second Law:

$$F_{net} = ma = 10.0 \times 0 = 0 \text{ N}$$

steps.title: Balance the forces
steps.content:
Since the net force is zero, the upward force applied by the student ($F$) must exactly balance the downward force of gravity (the weight, $mg$).

$$\begin{aligned} F_{net} &= F - mg = 0 \\ F &= mg \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate work done
steps.content:
Now we look at the work done ($W$) by the student. Using the formula for work where the force and displacement ($h$) are in the same direction:

$$W = Fs \cos\theta$$

Since we found that $F = mg$, and the displacement $s$ is the height $h$, we substitute these in:

$$W = (mg) \times h = mgh$$

steps.title: Relate to potential energy
steps.content:
Finally, we compare this to the formula for the gain in gravitational potential energy ($\Delta E_p$). From the data booklet:

$$\Delta E_p = mg\Delta h$$

Because the student's applied force is equal to the weight, the work done ($mgh$) is numerically identical to the gain in potential energy ($mg\Delta h$). No energy is wasted on accelerating the box (increasing kinetic energy), so all work is transferred into potential energy.

steps.title: Analyze the speed condition
steps.content:
The question specifies that the box is lifted at a **constant speed**. This is a crucial detail for energy analysis! 

In **Topic A.3.2 Work and energy transfer**, we learn that the kinetic energy ($E_k$) of an object depends on its speed. Because the speed doesn't change, the change in kinetic energy is zero:

$$\Delta E_k = 0$$

steps.title: Apply the Work-Energy Theorem
steps.content:
The **Work-Energy Theorem** states that the net work ($W_{net}$) done on an object is equal to its change in kinetic energy ($W_{net} = \Delta E_k$).

Since we established that $\Delta E_k = 0$, the total work done by all forces must be zero:

$$\begin{aligned} W_{net} &= 0 \\ W_{student} + W_{gravity} &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

This implies that the upward work done by the student must be exactly equal and opposite to the downward work done by gravity ($W_{student} = -W_{gravity}$). For this to happen, the student must apply a force that is equal and opposite to the weight of the box.

steps.title: Relate work to potential energy
steps.content:
By definition, the work done against gravity is stored as gravitational potential energy ($E_p$). Mathematically, the work done by gravity is the negative change in potential energy:

$$W_{gravity} = -\Delta E_p$$

Substituting this into our equation from Step 1:

$$\begin{aligned} W_{student} &= -(-\Delta E_p) \\ W_{student} &= \Delta E_p \end{aligned}$$

Because the speed is constant, no energy is used to accelerate the box (increasing $E_k$). Therefore, **all** the work done by the student is converted directly into the **gain in gravitational potential energy**.

questions.specification: A block of mass $5.0 \text{ kg}$ slides down a frictionless incline of height $3.0 \text{ m}$ and length $5.0 \text{ m}$.

parts.content: Calculate the gravitational potential energy of the block at the top of the incline relative to the bottom.
parts.markscheme:
- State the formula for potential energy: $E_p = m g h$
- Substitute values: $E_p = (5.0)(9.8)(3.0)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final value: $E_p = 147 \text{ J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Determine the speed of the block when it reaches the bottom of the incline.
parts.markscheme:
- Equate potential and kinetic energy: $m g h = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Rearrange for velocity: $v = \sqrt{2 g h}$
- Substitute values: $v = \sqrt{2(9.8)(3.0)}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the speed: $v = 7.67 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Apply Conservation of Energy
steps.content: Since the incline is frictionless, the total mechanical energy of the block is conserved. This means the gravitational potential energy ($E_p$) at the top is completely converted into kinetic energy ($E_k$) at the bottom. This concept is found in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

steps.title: State the energy equations
steps.content:
From the IB Data Booklet, we use the formulas for kinetic energy and change in gravitational potential energy:

$$\Delta E_{p}=mg\Delta h$$
$$E_{k}=\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$$

Equating these (setting the top as initial and bottom as final) gives us the conservation equation for this scenario:

$$mgh = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

steps.title: Rearrange for velocity
steps.content:
Notice that the mass ($m$) appears on both sides of the equation, so it cancels out. This shows that the final speed is independent of the mass. We then rearrange to solve for $v$:

$$\begin{aligned} gh &= \frac{1}{2}v^2 \\ 2gh &= v^2 \\ v &= \sqrt{2gh} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final speed
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values provided: $g = 9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ (from the constants section) and $h = 3.0 \text{ m}$.

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.8 \times 3.0}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the square root function and multiply the constants: 2, 9.8, and 3.0.

desmos.instructions: Type 'sqrt(2 * 9.8 * 3.0)' in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the square root of the energy product.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Using the calculated value:

$$v \approx 7.67 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

Based on the markscheme, the final answer is rounded to three significant figures. Marks are awarded for equating the energy types (M1), substituting correctly (M1), and providing the final value with units (A1).

steps.title: Identify given parameters
steps.content:
To solve this using dynamics and kinematics, we first identify the values from the problem:
- Height of incline ($h$): $3.0 \text{ m}$
- Length of incline (distance $s$): $5.0 \text{ m}$
- Initial velocity ($u$): $0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ (the block starts from rest)
- Acceleration due to gravity ($g$): $9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$

steps.title: Calculate acceleration down slope
steps.content:
For a frictionless incline, the acceleration $a$ is the component of gravity acting parallel to the slope. Using Newton's Second Law ($F=ma$):

$$a = g \sin\theta$$

From the geometry of the triangle, $\sin\theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{h}{L}$. 

$$\begin{aligned} a &= 9.8 \times \frac{3.0}{5.0} \\ a &= 9.8 \times 0.6 \\ a &= 5.88 \text{ m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply kinematic equation
steps.content:
Now we use the constant acceleration kinematic equation to find the final speed $v$. We know the total distance traveled along the incline is $s = 5.0 \text{ m}$.

$$v^{2}=u^{2}+2as$$

Substituting our known values:

$$\begin{aligned} v^{2} &= 0^{2} + 2(5.88)(5.0) \\ v^{2} &= 58.8 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate final speed
steps.content:
Finally, take the square root to find $v$:

$$v = \sqrt{58.8} \approx 7.67 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

This method aligns with **Topic A.1 (Kinematics)** and **Topic A.2 (Forces)**. Even though the markscheme uses an energy approach, both methods are valid in IB Physics and lead to the same result.

gdc.instructions: Enter the square root function and input 58.8 to get the result.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [x²] (the square root symbol), type 58.8, and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt(58.8) into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the final velocity by taking the square root of 58.8.

questions.specification: A constant force $F$ acts on an object of mass $m$, which is initially at rest. After a time interval $t$, the object reaches a speed $v$. A second object of mass $2m$, also initially at rest, is acted upon by a constant force $3F$ for a time interval $\frac{t}{2}$. What is the final speed of the second object in terms of $v$?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.2 Linear momentum and impulse
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.2 Linear momentum and impulse
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is a direct application of the impulse-momentum theorem (FΔt = mΔv), relating force, time, mass, and the resulting change in velocity.

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question uses the kinematic relationship between constant acceleration, time, and final velocity (v = u + at) for objects starting from rest.

options.markscheme:
The final speed $v$ is given by the impulse-momentum theorem: $F \cdot t = m \cdot v$, so $v = \frac{Ft}{m}$.
For the second object, the final speed $V$ is: $V = \frac{\text{Force} \times \text{time}}{\text{mass}} = \frac{3F \times \frac{t}{2}}{2m} = \frac{\frac{3}{2}Ft}{2m} = \frac{3}{4} \left( \frac{Ft}{m} \right) = \frac{3}{4}v$.

steps.title: Relate work to potential energy

In this problem, we are looking for the work done by the pulling force. Because the box is moved at a **constant speed**, its kinetic energy does not change. Additionally, the surface is **smooth**, meaning there is no friction to worry about. Therefore, all the work done by the pulling force goes directly into increasing the box's gravitational potential energy ($E_p$).

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To find the gravitational potential energy gained, we use the formula from the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$\Delta E_{p} = mg\Delta h$$

Here, $m$ is the mass, $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity, and $\Delta h$ is the vertical height gained. This topic is covered in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

steps.title: Calculate the vertical height
steps.content:
The box moves along the incline, but gravitational potential energy only depends on the **vertical** displacement. 

We can find the vertical height ($h$) by treating the incline as a right-angled triangle where the distance along the incline is the hypotenuse:

$$\begin{aligned} h &= s \cdot \sin(\theta) \\ h &= 5.0 \cdot \sin(30^{\circ}) \end{aligned}$$

Using a calculator, we find that $\sin(30^{\circ}) = 0.5$.
