parts.content: Calculate the initial gravitational potential energy of the car.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct formula and substitution: $E_p = mgh = 500.0 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 40.0$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Correct final answer with unit: $196\,200 \text{ J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify given variables
steps.content: To calculate the initial gravitational potential energy, we first identify the mass ($m$) and the starting height ($h$) from the problem description. This topic is covered in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

steps.title: Select the correct formula
steps.content:
In the **Mechanics** section of your IB Physics data booklet, you will find the formula for gravitational potential energy. Since we are calculating the energy at a specific height relative to the ground, we use:

$$\Delta E_{p}=mg\Delta h$$

steps.title: Perform the direct calculation
steps.content:
Substitute the mass ($m = 500.0\text{ kg}$), the acceleration due to gravity ($g \approx 9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$), and the initial height ($h = 40.0\text{ m}$) into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} E_p &= 500.0 \times 9.81 \times 40.0 \\ E_p &= 196\,200 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

Note: If you use $g = 9.8\text{ m s}^{-2}$ as listed in some versions of the data booklet constants, the answer is $196\,000\text{ J}$. Both are typically accepted, but $9.81$ is often used for higher precision.

gdc.instructions: Use the calculation mode to multiply the three values.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the total energy by multiplying mass, gravity, and height.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The initial gravitational potential energy of the roller coaster car is **$196\,200 \text{ J}$**.

In an exam, you earn:
- **1 mark** for the correct substitution of values into the formula.
- **1 mark** for the correct final answer with the unit (Joules).

parts.content: Determine the mechanical energy available at the bottom of the track.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct application of efficiency: $E_{\text{available}} = 0.80 \cdot 196\,200$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Correct final answer with unit: $156\,960 \text{ J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify initial mechanical energy
steps.content:
To find the energy at the bottom, we first need to determine the total mechanical energy at the start. Since the roller coaster car "starts from rest", its initial kinetic energy is zero. Therefore, the total mechanical energy at the peak is equal to its gravitational potential energy ($E_p$).

We are given:
- Mass ($m$) = $500.0 \text{ kg}$
- Initial height ($h$) = $40.0 \text{ m}$

steps.title: Calculate initial potential energy
steps.content:
Using the formula for gravitational potential energy from the data booklet:

$$\Delta E_{p}=mg\Delta h$$

Substituting our values (using $g = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ to align with the provided markscheme):

$$\begin{aligned} E_{p, \text{initial}} &= 500.0 \times 9.81 \times 40.0 \\ E_{p, \text{initial}} &= 196,200 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the initial gravitational potential energy.

steps.title: Apply energy conservation efficiency
steps.content:
The question states that due to friction, only $80.0\%$ of the mechanical energy is conserved. To find the mechanical energy available at the bottom, we multiply the initial total energy by the efficiency factor ($0.80$).

$$\begin{aligned} E_{\text{available}} &= 0.80 \times 196,200 \\ E_{\text{available}} &= 156,960 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final available energy by applying the 80% factor.

highlights.text: 80.0% of the mechanical energy is conserved

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The mechanical energy available at the bottom of the track is $156,960 \text{ J}$.

In an IB exam, you earn **1 mark** for correctly applying the efficiency factor to the initial energy and **1 mark** for the correct final numerical answer with units.

steps.title: Calculate initial potential energy
steps.content:
First, we determine the total mechanical energy at the start. Since the car "starts from rest", its initial kinetic energy is zero ($E_k = 0$). Therefore, the total initial mechanical energy is equal to its gravitational potential energy ($E_p$) at the peak height of $40.0\text{ m}$.

Using the formula from the data booklet:
$$\Delta E_p = mg\Delta h$$

Taking $g = 9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$ (to match the provided markscheme calculation):
$$\begin{aligned} E_{total, initial} &= 500.0\text{ kg} \times 9.81\text{ m s}^{-2} \times 40.0\text{ m} \\ E_{total, initial} &= 196,200\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 500 * 9.81 * 40 on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 500 * 9.81 * 40 into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate initial gravitational potential energy.

steps.title: Determine energy loss
steps.content:
The question states that "80.0% of the mechanical energy is conserved". This implies that $20.0\%$ of the energy is lost to resistive forces (friction and air resistance).

We calculate the energy lost by finding $20.0\%$ of the initial mechanical energy:
$$\begin{aligned} E_{loss} &= 0.20 \times 196,200\text{ J} \\ E_{loss} &= 39,240\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

desmos.instructions: Type 0.20 * 196200 into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate 20% energy loss.

highlights.text: 80.0% of the mechanical energy is conserved

steps.title: Calculate available mechanical energy
steps.content:
Finally, to find the mechanical energy available at the bottom, we subtract the energy lost from the initial total energy.

$$\begin{aligned} E_{available} &= E_{total, initial} - E_{loss} \\ E_{available} &= 196,200\text{ J} - 39,240\text{ J} \\ E_{available} &= 156,960\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation correctly applies the efficiency factor to the initial energy to find the final mechanical energy at the bottom.

desmos.instructions: Type 196200 - 39240 into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Subtract loss from total energy.

parts.content: Calculate the speed of the car at the bottom of the track.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct calculation of kinetic energy: $E_k = 156\,960 - mgh_{\text{bottom}} = 156\,960 - (500.0 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 5.0) = 132\,435 \text{ J}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Correct calculation of speed: $v = \sqrt{\frac{2E_k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 132\,435}{500.0}} = 23.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Calculate initial potential energy
steps.content:
First, we calculate the total energy the car has at the start. Since it starts from rest, all its initial mechanical energy is in the form of gravitational potential energy ($E_p$). Using the formula from the data booklet:

$$\Delta E_p = mgh$$

Substituting the values:
$$E_{p, \text{initial}} = 500.0 \times 9.81 \times 40.0 = 196,200 \text{ J}$$

desmos.instructions: Type 500 * 9.81 * 40 in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the initial gravitational potential energy.

steps.title: Account for energy conservation
steps.content:
The question states that only $80.0\%$ of the mechanical energy is conserved. This means the total mechanical energy available at any point later in the ride is $80.0\%$ of the initial energy.

$$E_{\text{mech, final}} = 0.80 \times 196,200 = 156,960 \text{ J}$$

calculator.description: Calculate 80% of the initial energy.

steps.title: Determine final kinetic energy
steps.content:
At the bottom of the track ($5.0 \text{ m}$), the total mechanical energy is split between kinetic energy ($E_k$) and the remaining potential energy ($E_p$). To find $E_k$, we subtract the potential energy at the bottom from the total energy available:

$$E_k = E_{\text{mech, final}} - mgh_{\text{bottom}}$$
$$E_k = 156,960 - (500.0 \times 9.81 \times 5.0) = 132,435 \text{ J}$$

This calculation earns the first mark (**M1**).

calculator.description: Subtract the potential energy at the bottom from the conserved energy.

steps.title: Calculate the final speed
steps.content:
Finally, we use the kinetic energy formula to solve for the speed $v$:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 \implies v = \sqrt{\frac{2E_k}{m}}$$

Substituting our values:
$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 132,435}{500.0}} \approx 23.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

This final result earns the second mark (**A1**). This concept of energy transformation is covered in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

calculator.description: Solve for speed v from kinetic energy.

steps.title: Define the energy conservation
steps.content:
In this problem, we look at the transfer of energy from the top of the track to the bottom. Since the car starts from rest, its initial energy is purely gravitational potential energy ($E_p$). At the bottom, its mechanical energy consists of both kinetic energy ($E_k$) and potential energy (because it is still $5.0\text{ m}$ above the ground).

According to the question, only $80.0\%$ of the initial mechanical energy is conserved. This means:

$$0.80 \times E_{initial} = E_{final}$$

This falls under **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**, where we learn that mechanical energy is the sum of kinetic and potential energies.

highlights.text: only 80.0% of the mechanical energy is conserved

steps.title: Formulate the algebraic equation
steps.content:
Using the formulas from the data booklet, we can express the energy terms as follows:

- Initial energy: $E_{initial} = mgh_{i}$
- Final energy: $E_{final} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + mgh_{f}$

Placing these into our conservation equation:

$$0.80(mgh_{i}) = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + mgh_{f}$$

Where:
- $m$ is the mass ($500.0\text{ kg}$)
- $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity ($9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$)
- $h_i$ is the initial height ($40.0\text{ m}$)
- $h_f$ is the final height ($5.0\text{ m}$)

steps.title: Simplify and solve for speed
steps.content:
One advantage of the algebraic approach is that we can see the mass $m$ appears in every term. We can cancel $m$ from both sides of the equation:

$$0.80gh_{i} = \frac{1}{2}v^2 + gh_{f}$$

Now, rearrange to solve for the final speed $v$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{2}v^2 &= 0.80gh_{i} - gh_{f} \\ v^2 &= 2(0.80gh_{i} - gh_{f}) \\ v &= \sqrt{2g(0.80h_{i} - h_{f})} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final result
steps.content:
Now we substitute the known values into our simplified expression. Note that while the data booklet lists $g = 9.8\text{ m s}^{-2}$, the markscheme uses the more precise $9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$ for this specific calculation.

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times (0.80 \times 40.0 - 5.0)}$$

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times (32.0 - 5.0)}$$

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 27.0} = \sqrt{529.74}$$

$$v \approx 23.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the square root function and input the expression 2 * 9.81 * (0.8 * 40 - 5).

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [x²] (the square root key), then type 2 * 9.81 * (0.8 * 40 - 5) and press [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate the speed $v$ using the simplified algebraic expression.

- Explain that energy losses are accounted for by increasing the starting height or reducing friction through better materials and design <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

questions.specification: A lift in a high-rise building starts from rest and moves upwards to the top floor. The motion consists of three distinct stages: first, it accelerates uniformly at $1.20 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$ for a duration of $3.00 \, \mathrm{s}$; second, it travels at a constant velocity for $10.0 \, \mathrm{s}$; third, it decelerates uniformly to rest in a final period of $4.00 \, \mathrm{s}$. Air resistance and friction are negligible.

parts.content: By considering the kinematics of each stage of the motion, determine the total vertical distance traveled by the lift from the start until it reaches the top floor.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the distance covered during the acceleration phase: $s_{1} = \frac{1}{2}at^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.20 \times 3.00^{2} = 5.40 \, \mathrm{m}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Calculate the constant velocity reached: $v = u + at = 0 + 1.20 \times 3.00 = 3.60 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Calculate the distance covered during the constant velocity and deceleration phases:
  - Constant velocity phase: $s_{2} = v \times t = 3.60 \times 10.0 = 36.0 \, \mathrm{m}$
  - Deceleration phase: $s_{3} = \frac{(u+v)}{2}t = \frac{(3.60 + 0)}{2} \times 4.00 = 7.20 \, \mathrm{m}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Determine the total distance: $s_{total} = 5.40 + 36.0 + 7.20 = 48.6 \, \mathrm{m}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The lift starts its motion at $t = 0$. On the axes provided, draw a graph to show the variation with time $t$ of the acceleration $a$ of the lift from $t = 0$ until it reaches the top floor. Mark the calculated numerical values of the acceleration on the vertical axis.

![acceleration-time graph axis](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-9047c5a0-77ec-4cb8-9a67-2bbae2ee8a67-1771929956947/05787654-8417-44e6-9943-7cf56f4ca6d9.jpeg)
parts.markscheme:
- Horizontal line at $a = +1.20 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$ from $t = 0$ to $t = 3.0 \, \mathrm{s}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Horizontal line at $a = 0$ from $t = 3.0 \, \mathrm{s}$ to $t = 13.0 \, \mathrm{s}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Correct calculation of deceleration for the final stage: $a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \frac{0 - 3.60}{4.00} = -0.90 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Horizontal line at $a = -0.90 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$ from $t = 13.0 \, \mathrm{s}$ to $t = 17.0 \, \mathrm{s}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Vertical lines connecting the segments are acceptable but not required. Numerical values must be clearly indicated on the y-axis.</note>

questions.specification:
A high-performance electric vehicle is undergoing a speed test on a straight, level track. The velocity $v$ of the vehicle is recorded at specific time intervals $t$ during the test. The data collected is presented in the table below.

| Time $t$ / $\mathrm{s}$ | Velocity $v$ / $\mathrm{m\ s^{-1}}$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0.0 | 0.0 |
| 5.0 | 15.0 |
| 10.0 | 30.0 |
| 15.0 | 30.0 |
| 25.0 | 0.0 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts use velocity–time data/graph features: identifying zero acceleration (constant v), finding acceleration from slope, and displacement/distance from area under a v–t graph, plus calculating distances over intervals.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) involves calculating acceleration from change in velocity over time, which overlaps with constant-acceleration kinematics relationships (though primarily via v–t gradient).

parts.content: State the time interval during which the vehicle travels with zero acceleration.
parts.markscheme: - Between $10.0\ \mathrm{s}$ and $15.0\ \mathrm{s}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the acceleration and the displacement of the vehicle can be determined from the features of a velocity–time graph.
parts.markscheme:
- The acceleration is determined by the gradient (slope) of the graph <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The displacement is determined by the area under the graph <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the acceleration of the vehicle during the first $10.0\ \mathrm{s}$ of the test.
parts.markscheme:
- $a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \frac{30.0 - 0.0}{10.0 - 0.0}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $a = 3.0\ \mathrm{m\ s^{-2}}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Determine the distance traveled by the vehicle while it was moving at its maximum velocity.
parts.markscheme:
- $s = v \times t = 30.0 \times (15.0 - 10.0)$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $s = 150\ \mathrm{m}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the total distance traveled by the vehicle from $t = 0.0\ \mathrm{s}$ to $t = 25.0\ \mathrm{s}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Distance from $0.0$ to $10.0\ \mathrm{s}$ (area of triangle) $= \frac{1}{2} \times 10.0 \times 30.0 = 150\ \mathrm{m}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Distance from $15.0$ to $25.0\ \mathrm{s}$ (area of triangle) $= \frac{1}{2} \times 10.0 \times 30.0 = 150\ \mathrm{m}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Total distance $= 150 + 150 + 150 = 450\ \mathrm{m}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 3 marks for correctly identifying the total area of the trapezoid: $\frac{1}{2} \times (5 + 25) \times 30 = 450\ \mathrm{m}$.</note>

questions.specification:
A student investigates the motion of a small car rolling down a frictionless ramp to determine the acceleration $a$ of the car. The ramp is inclined at an angle, and the car is released from rest. The student measures the time $t$ taken for the car to travel a distance $s=1.20 \, \mathrm{m}$ down the ramp. The acceleration $a$ is calculated using the equation:

$$
s=\frac{1}{2} a t^2
$$

The percentage uncertainty in the measurement of $t$ is $0.4 \%$, and the percentage uncertainty in the measurement of $s$ is $0.3 \%$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses the constant-acceleration equation s = (1/2)at^2 to calculate acceleration from distance and time, directly testing SUVAT/kinematics equations of motion.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Includes processing repeated measurements (mean, half-range) and discussing reducing uncertainty, which fits quantitative description/analysis of motion data in a kinematics context.

parts.content:
Using the equation $s=\frac{1}{2} a t^2$, show that the percentage uncertainty in $a$ is given by:

Percentage uncertainty in $a = 2 \times$ percentage uncertainty in $t$ + percentage uncertainty in $s$
parts.markscheme:
- Correctly derive the relationship: Percentage uncertainty in $a = 2 \times$ percentage uncertainty in $t$ + percentage uncertainty in $s$ <marks>1</marks>

- Clearly explain that this follows because $a \propto \frac{s}{t^2}$ (powers add for multiplication/division) <marks>1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Rearrange for acceleration
steps.content:
To find how the uncertainty in $a$ depends on $s$ and $t$, we first rearrange the given kinematic equation to make $a$ the subject.

Starting with:
$$s = \frac{1}{2} a t^2$$

Multiply both sides by 2 and divide by $t^2$:
$$\begin{aligned} 2s &= at^2 \\ a &= \frac{2s}{t^2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply propagation rules
steps.content:
In IB Physics, when quantities are multiplied or divided, we add their **percentage uncertainties**. 

Our equation is $a = \frac{2s}{t^2}$. Note that the constant '2' has no uncertainty, so it does not contribute to the calculation. The relationship can be expressed as:
$$a \propto \frac{s}{t^2}$$

According to the rules for propagation of uncertainties:
1. For the quotient $\frac{s}{...}$, we add the percentage uncertainty of $s$.
2. For the power $t^2$, we multiply the percentage uncertainty of $t$ by the power (which is 2).

steps.title: State the final relationship
steps.content:
Combining these rules, the total percentage uncertainty in $a$ is the sum of the individual contributions:

$$\text{Percentage uncertainty in } a = (2 \times \text{percentage uncertainty in } t) + (\text{percentage uncertainty in } s)$$

This matches the expression we were asked to show. In an exam, you earn marks for clearly showing the rearranged formula and explaining that powers are multiplied and uncertainties are added for division.

highlights.text: Percentage uncertainty in $a = 2 \times$ percentage uncertainty in $t$ + percentage uncertainty in $s$

steps.title: Rearrange for acceleration $a$
steps.content:
To determine the uncertainty in the acceleration $a$, we first need to express it in terms of the measured variables $s$ and $t$. We start with the kinematic equation provided in the question:

$$s = \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

By multiplying both sides by 2 and dividing by $t^2$, we isolate $a$:

$$a = \frac{2s}{t^2}$$

This equation is a specific case of the displacement formula found in **Topic A.1 Kinematics** when the initial velocity $u = 0$.

steps.title: Identify constants and proportionality
steps.content:
In our rearranged equation $a = \frac{2s}{t^2}$, the number $2$ is a mathematical constant. Pure numbers like this have no uncertainty associated with them. 

Because the constant doesn't affect the spread of the measurement, we focus on the proportionality relationship between the variables:

$$a \propto \frac{s}{t^2}$$

This shows that $a$ depends on the first power of $s$ and the inverse square of $t$.

steps.title: Apply uncertainty power rules
steps.content:
In IB Physics, we use specific rules for propagating uncertainties:
1. When variables are multiplied or divided, their **percentage uncertainties** are added.
2. When a variable is raised to a power $n$, its percentage uncertainty is **multiplied by $n$**.

Applying these to our relationship $a \propto \frac{s}{t^2}$:
- For $s$: We add the percentage uncertainty in $s$ (power of 1).
- For $t^2$: We take the percentage uncertainty in $t$ and multiply it by $2$ because of the exponent.

steps.title: State the final expression
steps.content:
Combining the rules from the previous step, we arrive at the final relationship for the total percentage uncertainty in $a$:

$$\text{Percentage uncertainty in } a = 2 \times (\text{percentage uncertainty in } t) + (\text{percentage uncertainty in } s)$$

This logical derivation earns 2 marks: one for correctly rearranging and identifying the relationship, and one for explaining the addition of powers for multiplication and division.

highlights.text: Percentage uncertainty in $a = 2 \times$ percentage uncertainty in $t$ + percentage uncertainty in $s$

parts.content: The student records $t=1.50 \, \mathrm{s}$. Calculate the value of $a$ and its percentage uncertainty.
parts.markscheme:
- Rearrange the equation $s=\frac{1}{2} a t^2$ to get $a=\frac{2 s}{t^2}$ <marks>1</marks>

- Substitute values: $a=\frac{2(1.20)}{(1.50)^2}=1.07 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$ <marks>1</marks>

- Calculate percentage uncertainty: Percentage uncertainty in $a=2(0.4 \%)+0.3 \%=1.1 \%$ <marks>1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
First, let's list the values and uncertainties provided in the question. We know the distance $s$ and the time $t$, along with their respective percentage uncertainties.

- Distance $s = 1.20 \, \mathrm{m}$ with a percentage uncertainty of $0.3 \%$
- Time $t = 1.50 \, \mathrm{s}$ with a percentage uncertainty of $0.4 \%$

steps.title: Isolate the acceleration
steps.content:
The question provides the relationship between displacement and acceleration when an object starts from rest ($u=0$), which is a simplified version of the kinematic equation found in **Topic A.1.2** of your data booklet.

We need to rearrange this equation to solve for $a$:

$$\begin{aligned} s &= \frac{1}{2} a t^2 \\ 2s &= at^2 \\ a &= \frac{2s}{t^2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate acceleration magnitude
steps.content:
Now, substitute the known values into our rearranged equation to find the value of $a$:

$$a = \frac{2(1.20)}{(1.50)^2}$$

$$a = 1.07 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$$

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to find the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the numerical value of acceleration.

steps.title: Propagate percentage uncertainties
steps.content:
To find the total percentage uncertainty in $a$, we use the propagation rules for multiplication, division, and powers. 

When quantities are multiplied or divided, their percentage uncertainties are added. For a quantity raised to a power $n$, we multiply its percentage uncertainty by $|n|$.

For $a = \frac{2s}{t^2}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \% \text{ uncertainty in } a &= (\% \text{ uncertainty in } s) + 2 \times (\% \text{ uncertainty in } t) \\ &= 0.3\% + 2(0.4\%) \\ &= 0.3\% + 0.8\% \\ &= 1.1\% \end{aligned}$$

Thus, the acceleration is $1.07 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$ with a percentage uncertainty of $1.1 \%$.

calculator.description: Sum the percentage uncertainties.

steps.title: Rearrange and calculate acceleration
steps.content:
To find the acceleration $a$, we first rearrange the given kinematic equation. Since the car is released from rest, the equation is simplified as follows:

$$a = \frac{2s}{t^2}$$

Substituting the measured values:

$$a = \frac{2(1.20)}{(1.50)^2} = 1.0666... \approx 1.07 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the nominal value of acceleration.

steps.title: Calculate absolute uncertainties
steps.content:
Using **Extreme Value Analysis**, we first determine the absolute uncertainties for distance $s$ and time $t$ using their given percentage uncertainties:

$$\Delta s = \frac{0.3}{100} \times 1.20 \, \mathrm{m} = 0.0036 \, \mathrm{m}$$
$$\Delta t = \frac{0.4}{100} \times 1.50 \, \mathrm{s} = 0.0060 \, \mathrm{s}$$

highlights.text: percentage uncertainty in the measurement of t is 0.4 %
percentage uncertainty in the measurement of s is 0.3 %

steps.title: Determine extreme acceleration values
steps.content:
To find the possible range of acceleration, we calculate the maximum ($a_{max}$) and minimum ($a_{min}$) values. 

To get $a_{max}$, we use the largest possible distance and the smallest possible time:
$$a_{max} = \frac{2(s + \Delta s)}{(t - \Delta t)^2} = \frac{2(1.2036)}{(1.494)^2} \approx 1.0785 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$$

To get $a_{min}$, we use the smallest distance and the largest time:
$$a_{min} = \frac{2(s - \Delta s)}{(t + \Delta t)^2} = \frac{2(1.1964)}{(1.506)^2} \approx 1.0550 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$$

desmos.instructions: Create expressions for a_max and a_min.

calculator.description: Calculate the extreme values of acceleration.

steps.title: Calculate final percentage uncertainty
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the absolute uncertainty $\Delta a$ from the range, and then convert it to a percentage of the nominal value $a = 1.0667$:

$$\Delta a = \frac{a_{max} - a_{min}}{2} = \frac{1.0785 - 1.0550}{2} = 0.01175 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$$

$$\text{Percentage uncertainty} = \left( \frac{0.01175}{1.0667} \right) \times 100 \approx 1.1 \%$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final percentage uncertainty from the range.

steps.title: Identify known variables
steps.content:
First, let's list the information we have from the question and identify our initial state:
- Distance $s = 1.20 \, \mathrm{m}$
- Time $t = 1.50 \, \mathrm{s}$
- Initial velocity $u = 0 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}$ (since it is "released from rest")
- Percentage uncertainty in $s$ ($\Delta s\%$) = $0.3 \%$
- Percentage uncertainty in $t$ ($\Delta t\%$) = $0.4 \%$

Our goal is to find the acceleration $a$ and its percentage uncertainty by first finding the final velocity $v$ as an intermediate step.

steps.title: Calculate intermediate final velocity
steps.content:
Using the kinematic equation for average velocity, we can find the final velocity $v$. 

$$\begin{aligned} s &= \frac{u+v}{2}t \\ 1.20 &= \frac{0+v}{2}(1.50) \\ v &= \frac{2 \times 1.20}{1.50} \\ v &= 1.60 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-1}} \end{aligned}$$

This gives us the velocity of the car at the bottom of the ramp.

calculator.description: Calculate the final velocity v.

steps.title: Uncertainty in final velocity
steps.content:
When variables are multiplied or divided, their percentage uncertainties are added. Since $v = \frac{2s}{t}$ (and the number 2 has no uncertainty), we calculate the percentage uncertainty in $v$ as:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta v\% &= \Delta s\% + \Delta t\% \\ \Delta v\% &= 0.3\% + 0.4\% \\ \Delta v\% &= 0.7\% \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: percentage uncertainty in the measurement of t is 0.4 %
percentage uncertainty in the measurement of s is 0.3 %

steps.title: Calculate acceleration
steps.content:
Now we use the final velocity to find the acceleration $a$. From the definition of acceleration (or the first kinematic equation):

$$\begin{aligned} v &= u + at \\ 1.60 &= 0 + a(1.50) \\ a &= \frac{1.60}{1.50} \\ a &\approx 1.0667 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, we get $a = 1.07 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$.

calculator.description: Divide final velocity by time to find acceleration.

steps.title: Final percentage uncertainty
steps.content:
To find the final percentage uncertainty in $a$, we again apply the rule for division ($a = v/t$):

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta a\% &= \Delta v\% + \Delta t\% \\ \Delta a\% &= 0.7\% + 0.4\% \\ \Delta a\% &= 1.1\% \end{aligned}$$

This matches the result we would get if we had used the direct formula $a = \frac{2s}{t^2}$, where $\Delta a\% = \Delta s\% + 2\Delta t\%$.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculated acceleration is **$1.07 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$** with a percentage uncertainty of **$1.1\%$**.

**Marking Breakdown:**
- **1 mark** for calculating the numerical value of $a$ correctly ($1.07$).
- **1 mark** for correctly identifying the uncertainty rule (adding percentage uncertainties).
- **1 mark** for the final percentage uncertainty value ($1.1\%$).

parts.content: Suggest two ways to reduce the percentage uncertainty in the value of $a$, and explain your reasoning.
parts.markscheme:
Award **[1]** for each appropriate improvement stated (max **[2]**) and **[1]** for appropriate explanation. Examples include:

- Use a longer ramp to increase $s$. <marks>1</marks>
- Explanation: Larger distances reduce the percentage uncertainty in $s$ and $t$. <marks>1</marks>

- Use a high-speed camera or precise light gates for measuring $t$. <marks>1</marks>
- Explanation: Allows for more accurate timing than a stopwatch. <marks>1</marks>

- Repeat the experiment multiple times. <marks>1</marks>
- Explanation: Reduces the effect of random errors in timing. <marks>1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the uncertainty relationship
steps.content:
To understand how to reduce uncertainty in $a$, we first look at the equation for acceleration derived from the question:

$$a = \frac{2s}{t^2}$$

In IB Physics, when quantities are multiplied or divided, their percentage uncertainties are added. For $a$, the total percentage uncertainty $(\frac{\Delta a}{a})$ is given by:

$$\frac{\Delta a}{a} = \frac{\Delta s}{s} + 2\left(\frac{\Delta t}{t}\right)$$

where $\Delta s$ and $\Delta t$ are the absolute uncertainties (the precision of your tools).

steps.title: Increase distance (s)
steps.content:
The first way to reduce uncertainty using the 'scale' approach is to **increase the distance $s$** by using a longer ramp.

If the tool used to measure distance (like a meter ruler) has a fixed absolute uncertainty $\Delta s$, then increasing the measured value $s$ makes the fraction $\frac{\Delta s}{s}$ smaller. This directly reduces the percentage uncertainty in $s$.

steps.title: Increase travel time (t)
steps.content:
The second way is to **decrease the angle of the ramp**. 

A shallower ramp results in a smaller acceleration, meaning the car will take a longer time $t$ to cover the same distance. Just like with distance, if the absolute uncertainty in timing $\Delta t$ (such as human reaction time or digital resolution) remains constant, a larger value of $t$ results in a smaller percentage uncertainty $\frac{\Delta t}{t}$.

steps.title: Explain the reasoning
steps.content:
The reasoning for both methods is based on the **Increasing Scale of Measurements**. 

By increasing the 'scale' (magnitude) of the values we record ($s$ and $t$), the fixed errors of our measuring instruments become a smaller proportion of the total measurement. Since the percentage uncertainty in $a$ is the sum of these individual percentage uncertainties, increasing the scale of $s$ and $t$ leads to a more precise value for $a$.

steps.title: Analyze the uncertainty in $a$
steps.content:
To reduce the percentage uncertainty in the acceleration $a$, we first look at the relationship provided in the question. Rearranging the formula for $a$ gives:

$$a = \frac{2s}{t^2}$$

According to the rules for propagation of uncertainties, the percentage uncertainty in $a$ is the sum of the percentage uncertainty in $s$ and twice the percentage uncertainty in $t$:

$$\frac{\Delta a}{a} = \frac{\Delta s}{s} + 2\left(\frac{\Delta t}{t}\right)$$

Since $t$ is squared, reducing the uncertainty in the time measurement is the most effective way to improve the precision of $a$.

steps.title: Suggestion 1: Light Gates
steps.content:
The first technological upgrade is to use **light gates** connected to an electronic timer to measure $t$. 

When the car passes through the first gate, it starts the timer, and the second gate stops it. This replaces a manual stopwatch, which is subject to human reaction time (typically $\approx 0.2\text{ s}$).

steps.title: Suggestion 2: Video Analysis
steps.content:
The second upgrade is to use a **high-speed camera** or video analysis software. 

By recording the motion at a high frame rate (e.g., 240 frames per second), we can pinpoint the exact frame the car begins to move and the exact frame it passes the $1.20\text{ m}$ mark. This allows for a much more precise determination of both the time $t$ and the distance $s$.

steps.title: Explain the reasoning
steps.content:
The reasoning for both methods is that they significantly reduce the **absolute uncertainty** in the measurements. 

Electronic sensors have a precision of milliseconds, compared to the tenths of a second associated with human reaction time. Since percentage uncertainty is calculated as:

$$\text{Percentage Uncertainty} = \frac{\text{Absolute Uncertainty}}{\text{Measured Value}} \times 100\%$$

a smaller absolute uncertainty directly results in a smaller percentage uncertainty for $t$, and consequently, for the calculated acceleration $a$.

steps.title: Identify the Goal
steps.content: To reduce the percentage uncertainty in the calculated acceleration $a$, we need to address the sources of uncertainty in our measurements of distance $s$ and time $t$. We will use the **statistical repetition** approach, which focuses on gathering more data to minimize the influence of individual measurement variations.

steps.title: Suggestion 1: Repeat and Average
steps.content:
The first way to improve the result is to repeat the timing measurement multiple times for the same distance ($s = 1.20 \text{ m}$) and then calculate the mean (average) time. 

In an IB experiment, it is standard practice to perform at least 3 to 5 trials for each independent variable setting to ensure the data is reliable.

steps.title: Suggestion 2: Graphical Analysis
steps.content:
A second way is to repeat the experiment using several different distances $s$ (for example, $0.80 \text{ m}$, $1.00 \text{ m}$, $1.20 \text{ m}$, etc.) and recording the time for each. 

By plotting a graph of $s$ against $t^2$, we can determine the acceleration from the gradient. This is a form of statistical repetition because the line of best fit effectively "averages" the data from all measurements across the entire range.

steps.title: Explain the Reasoning
steps.content:
The reasoning behind these suggestions is that they reduce the impact of **random errors**. 

Random errors, such as the human reaction time when starting or stopping a stopwatch, cause measurements to fluctuate above and below the true value. By repeating the trials and averaging the results (either through a mean or a line of best fit), these fluctuations tend to cancel each other out, leading to a more precise and accurate value for the acceleration.

highlights.text: Reduces the effect of random errors

parts.content: The student performs multiple trials and records the following values of $a$ from different runs: $1.05 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$, $1.08 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$, and $1.02 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$. Calculate the mean value of $a$ and its uncertainty using half the range of the data.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the mean value: Mean $a=\frac{1.05+1.08+1.02}{3}=1.05 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$ <marks>1</marks>

- Calculate the uncertainty: Uncertainty $=\frac{\text { range }}{2}=\frac{1.08-1.02}{2}=0.03 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$ <marks>1</marks>

- Final answer: $a=1.05 \pm 0.03 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$ <marks>1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the trial values
steps.content: First, let's identify the three experimental values recorded for acceleration $a$ from the question. These values are $1.05 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$, $1.08 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$, and $1.02 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$. We will use these to find both the average performance and the spread (uncertainty) of the data.

steps.title: Calculate the arithmetic mean
steps.content:
The mean (average) value is found by summing the three trials and dividing by the total number of trials ($n = 3$).

$$\text{Mean } a = \frac{1.05 + 1.08 + 1.02}{3}$$

$$\text{Mean } a = 1.05 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the fraction template or parentheses to sum the values, then divide by 3.

ti84.instructions: Enter (1.05 + 1.08 + 1.02) / 3 on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the mean formula or use the mean function: mean(1.05, 1.08, 1.02)

calculator.description: Calculate the mean of the three acceleration values.

steps.title: Determine the uncertainty range
steps.content:
In IB Physics, when multiple trials are available, the absolute uncertainty is often calculated using **half the range**. 

First, find the maximum and minimum values in the data set:
- Max value $= 1.08 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$
- Min value $= 1.02 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$

Now, apply the formula:
$$\text{Uncertainty} = \frac{\text{Range}}{2} = \frac{\text{Max} - \text{Min}}{2}$$

$$\Delta a = \frac{1.08 - 1.02}{2} = 0.03 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the difference between the max and min, then divide by 2.

calculator.description: Calculate half the range to find the uncertainty.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Finally, we combine the mean value and the calculated uncertainty. Ensure that both values are expressed to the same number of decimal places (hundredths place in this case) and include the units.

$$a = 1.05 \pm 0.03 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$$

**Marking Breakdown:**
- 1 mark for the correct mean value ($1.05$).
- 1 mark for the correct uncertainty calculation ($0.03$).
- 1 mark for the final formatted answer with units.

questions.specification:
A research vehicle is undergoing a performance test on a straight horizontal track. The velocity $v$ of the vehicle is recorded at regular intervals as it accelerates from rest. The following table shows the data collected during the first $6.0$ seconds of the motion:

| Time $t / \mathrm{s}$ | Velocity $v / \mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| $0.0$ | $0.0$ |
| $2.0$ | $12.0$ |
| $4.0$ | $24.0$ |
| $6.0$ | $36.0$ |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses velocity–time data to find displacement via area under a v–t graph (distance traveled in first 6 s).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uniform acceleration/deceleration segment requires applying constant-acceleration kinematics (e.g., v-u-at, v^2=u^2+2as) to find stopping time and total elapsed time.

parts.content: Calculate the distance travelled by the vehicle during the first $6.0$ seconds of the test.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognition that distance is the area under the velocity–time graph OR use of $s = \frac{u+v}{2}t$  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $s = \frac{0 + 36.0}{2} \times 6.0 = 108 \, \mathrm{m}$  <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> max for $216 \, \mathrm{m}$ (omission of the $1/2$ factor).</note>
<note>Alternative method: use average velocity $\bar v = \frac{0+36.0}{2} = 18.0\,\mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}$ then $s=\bar v t=18.0\times 6.0=108\,\mathrm{m}$.</note>

parts.content: At $t = 6.0 \, \mathrm{s}$, the vehicle begins to decelerate uniformly. It travels a further $54 \, \mathrm{m}$ before coming to a complete stop. Determine the total time elapsed from $t = 0$ until the vehicle is stationary.
parts.markscheme:
- Use of $s = \frac{u+v}{2}t$ to find braking time: $54 = \frac{36.0 + 0}{2} \times t_{\mathrm{brake}}$  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $t_{\mathrm{brake}} = 3.0 \, \mathrm{s}$  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Total time $= 6.0 + 3.0 = 9.0 \, \mathrm{s}$  <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>2 marks</marks> max for a final answer of $3.0 \, \mathrm{s}$ (braking phase only).</note>
<note>Alternative method: $v^{2}=u^{2}+2as$ gives $0=36.0^{2}+2a(54)$ so $a=-12\,\mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$, then $t_{\mathrm{brake}}=\frac{v-u}{a}=\frac{0-36.0}{-12}=3.0\,\mathrm{s}$, then add $6.0\,\mathrm{s}$.</note>

It experiences a constant frictional force of $2500\ \text{N}$ and maintains a constant speed of $15\ \text{m s}^{-1}$. What is the total power output of the truck's engine?

questions.specification:
A student investigates the relationship between force $(F)$ and acceleration $(a)$ by applying varying forces to a cart of fixed mass $(m)$. The following data were recorded:

| Force $(F)(\mathrm{N})$ | Acceleration $(a)\left(\mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}\right)$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1.0 | 0.5 |
| 2.0 | 1.1 |
| 3.0 | 1.6 |
| 4.0 | 2.1 |
| 5.0 | 2.5 |
| 6.0 | 3.0 |
| 7.0 | 3.6 |

The relationship between $F, m$, and $a$ is governed by Newton's second law:

$$
F=m a
$$

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/49b049b2-7be9-4d83-84d8-448210d370a7.png)

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The investigation directly tests Newton’s second law (F=ma) via a force–acceleration graph and uses the slope to determine mass; discussion of frictional effects fits Newton’s laws context.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Interpreting the force–acceleration graph and describing the trend involves qualitative/quantitative description of motion from graphical data.

parts.content: Identify two potential sources of systematic uncertainty in the experiment and for each suggest a way to minimize it.
parts.markscheme:
- Frictional forces acting on the cart. <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- Solution: Use a low-friction track or air track to minimize friction. <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- Zero error in the force sensor or Newton meter. <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- Solution: Zero or calibrate the sensor before taking measurements. <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Define Systematic Uncertainty
steps.content: A systematic uncertainty is a consistent error that shifts all measurements in the same direction. In this experiment, we are investigating the mechanical relationship between force and acceleration, so we look for factors in the physical setup that might create a constant offset in the data.

steps.title: Analyze Friction Effects
steps.content:
One primary mechanical factor is **friction** between the cart and the track. Newton's Second Law assumes $F$ is the net force:

$$\text{data booklet: } F = ma$$

However, friction opposes motion. This means the recorded force ($F_{applied}$) is actually slightly larger than the net force ($F_{net}$) causing the acceleration. This shift in values is a source of systematic uncertainty.

highlights.text: frictional forces acting on the cart

steps.title: Minimize Frictional Forces
steps.content: To minimize the impact of friction, you can use a **low-friction track or an air track**. An air track uses a cushion of air to significantly reduce the mechanical resistance between the cart and the surface, making the applied force closer to the net force.

steps.title: Identify Equipment Calibration Errors
steps.content: A second source of systematic uncertainty is **zero error** in the measuring equipment (like a force sensor or Newton meter). If the sensor is not set to zero before adding weights or applying force, every recorded value will be too high or too low by a fixed amount.

highlights.text: Zero error in the force sensor or Newton meter

steps.title: Zero and Calibrate Sensors
steps.content: To minimize this, you should **zero or calibrate the sensor** immediately before taking measurements. By ensuring the reading is $0.0\text{ N}$ when no force is applied, you remove the constant offset that causes systematic error.

steps.title: Verify Data Trends
steps.content: While not required for the marks, we can use a calculator to verify that the relationship $\frac{F}{a}$ (mass $m$) is consistent. If we see a trend (like $m$ slightly changing), it supports the idea that systematic factors like friction are influencing the results.

gdc.instructions: Divide the force by the acceleration for the first and last rows of the table.

ti84.instructions: Calculate 1.0 / 0.5 for the first point and 7.0 / 3.6 for the last point.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the mass for each point to observe if it remains constant.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass (F/a) for the first and last data points to check for consistency.

steps.title: Identify Instrumental Zero Errors
steps.content:
A common systematic uncertainty in this experiment is a **zero error** (or zero offset) in the force sensor. This occurs when the sensor provides a non-zero reading even when no force is being applied to the cart. 

This shift affects every data point equally, which is a hallmark of systematic error. In your graph, this would cause the line of best fit to not pass through the origin $(0,0)$ as predicted by $$F = ma$$.

steps.title: Electronic Zeroing and Calibration
steps.content:
To minimize the zero error in the force sensor, you should perform an **electronic zeroing** or **recalibration** before starting the data collection. 

Most digital force sensors have a 'Tare' or 'Zero' button in the software. By ensuring the sensor reads exactly $0.0\text{ N}$ when the cart is stationary and disconnected, you ensure the instrumental offset is removed from your measurements.

steps.title: Analyze Friction as Offset
steps.content:
A second source of systematic uncertainty is **friction** acting between the cart and the track. While friction is an external force, it acts as a consistent 'negative' offset in the net force calculation. 

Because friction always opposes motion, the actual net force causing acceleration is slightly less than the force recorded by your sensor ($$F_{net} = F_{applied} - F_{friction}$$). This shifts the data consistently downward.

steps.title: Minimize Friction Effects
steps.content:
To minimize this systematic error, you can 'calibrate' the physical environment to be as close to frictionless as possible. 

Suggested solutions include:
1. Using an **air track**, which creates a cushion of air to eliminate contact friction.
2. Using a **low-friction track** with high-quality wheels.
3. Compensating for friction by slightly tilting the track so that the component of gravity down the slope exactly cancels out the frictional force.

steps.title: Analyze the theoretical relationship
steps.content:
According to Newton's Second Law, the relationship between force ($F$) and acceleration ($a$) is given by:

$$F = ma$$

In a graph of $F$ (y-axis) against $a$ (x-axis), this equation takes the form $y = mx + c$, where the gradient is the mass $m$ and the y-intercept $c$ should be zero. This signifies a **direct proportion**. If the line of best fit does not pass through the origin $(0,0)$, it indicates the presence of **systematic uncertainty**.

steps.title: Deduce intercept using GDC
steps.content:
To determine if a systematic error exists, we calculate the line of best fit for the provided data points. 

Using a calculator to perform a linear regression on the coordinates $(a, F)$, we find that the equation is approximately:

$$F \approx 2.00a - 0.10$$

Because the y-intercept is not zero (it is approximately $-0.10\text{ N}$), we can deduce that there is a constant offset in our measurements, which is characteristic of a systematic error.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Statistics or Spreadsheet menu.
2. Input the 'a' data in the first column and 'F' data in the second.
3. Perform a Linear Regression (y = ax + b).
4. Check the 'b' value for the y-intercept.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [STAT] [1:Edit].
2. Enter acceleration values in L1 and force values in L2.
3. Press [STAT], arrow right to [CALC], then select [4:LinReg(ax+b)].
4. Select L1 for Xlist and L2 for Ylist, then [Calculate].

desmos.instructions:
1. Create a table with the given 'a' and 'F' values.
2. In a new cell, type: y1 ~ m*x1 + c.
3. Observe the value of 'c'.

calculator.description: Calculate the linear regression to find the y-intercept (systematic offset).

steps.title: Identify Uncertainty 1: Friction
steps.content:
The first source of systematic uncertainty identified via the intercept is **frictional forces** acting on the cart. 

If friction is present, the net force is $F_{net} = F_{applied} - F_{friction}$. Substituting this into Newton's second law ($F_{net} = ma$):

$$F_{applied} = ma + F_{friction}$$

This would shift the graph upwards, resulting in a non-zero intercept. To minimize this effect, you should use a **low-friction track** or an **air track**.

steps.title: Identify Uncertainty 2: Calibration
steps.content:
The second source is a **zero error** in the equipment (such as the force sensor or Newton meter). 

If the sensor is not correctly calibrated, it might record a force even when no force is applied, or vice versa. This adds a constant value to every measurement, shifting the entire line of best fit away from the origin. To minimize this, you must **zero or calibrate the sensor** before starting the experiment.

parts.content: Explain why it is important to use a low-friction track for this investigation.
parts.markscheme: Low-friction track: Friction opposes motion, reducing the measured acceleration for a given force and introducing errors in the calculated mass $m$. <marks> 2 marks </marks>

steps.title: Define the Resultant Force
steps.content:
Newton’s Second Law, which you can find in **Topic A.2 Forces and momentum**, states that the acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the **resultant** (or net) force acting on it. 

In the data booklet, this is written as:

$$F = ma$$

In this experiment, the force $F$ recorded by the student is the *applied force*. However, the acceleration $a$ is determined by the *net force*.

steps.title: Analyze the Effect of Friction
steps.content:
Friction is a resistive force that opposes motion. When friction is present, the net force ($F_{\text{net}}$) is the difference between the applied force ($F_{\text{app}}$) and the frictional force ($f$):

$$\begin{aligned} F_{\text{net}} &= F_{\text{app}} - f \\ ma &= F_{\text{app}} - f \end{aligned}$$

If the track is not low-friction, a portion of the applied force is used just to overcome friction rather than to accelerate the cart.

steps.title: Impact on Measured Acceleration
steps.content:
Because friction reduces the net force acting on the cart, the **measured acceleration will be lower** than it would be on a perfectly frictionless track for the same applied force.

From the equation $a = \frac{F_{\text{net}}}{m}$, if $F_{\text{net}}$ decreases due to friction, the resulting acceleration $a$ also decreases.

steps.title: Identify Errors in Calculation
steps.content:
Using a high-friction track introduces a **systematic error**. Since the student assumes the applied force $F$ is the only force acting ($F = ma$), the reduction in acceleration would lead to an incorrect calculation of the mass ($m = \frac{F}{a}$).

A low-friction track is essential to ensure that the applied force is as close as possible to the resultant force, making the experimental results valid.

steps.title: Define direct proportionality
steps.content:
According to Newton's Second Law, the relationship between force and acceleration is given by:

$$F = ma$$

In an ideal, frictionless experiment, the acceleration ($a$) should be **directly proportional** to the applied force ($F$). This means that if we were to graph $F$ against $a$, the line of best fit should pass exactly through the origin $(0,0)$. This is a core concept from **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**.

steps.title: Identify friction as error
steps.content:
Friction acts as a **systematic error**. It is a consistent force that always opposes the motion of the cart. Instead of the net force being simply the applied force ($F$), the actual net force is:

$$F_{net} = F - f_{friction}$$

This means that some of the force you apply is "wasted" just to overcome friction before the cart even begins to accelerate.

steps.title: Analyze the graph's intercept
steps.content: Because of this friction, the graph of $F$ vs $a$ will no longer show direct proportionality. Instead, it will have a **non-zero intercept** on the force axis. This intercept represents the frictional force that must be exceeded for acceleration to occur ($F > 0$ even when $a = 0$).

steps.title: Impact on mass calculation
steps.content: The goal of the experiment is usually to find the mass ($m$) from the gradient of the graph. If friction is present, it introduces a systematic shift in the data points. While the gradient might still give a value for mass, the lack of direct proportionality reduces the **experimental validity** and accuracy of the results, as the relationship $F = ma$ only holds true for the *resultant* (net) force.

highlights.text: Friction opposes motion, reducing the measured acceleration for a given force and introducing errors in the calculated mass $m$.

parts.content: Using the graph provided, describe the trend observed between force and acceleration.
parts.markscheme: Trend: The graph shows a linear relationship between $F$ and $a$, indicating that $F \propto a$ as expected from $F=m a$. <marks> 2 marks </marks>

steps.title: Analyze the graph shape
steps.content:
To describe the trend, we first look at the overall shape of the line of best fit drawn through the data points. We can see that the data points follow a clear path that can be represented by a **straight line**.

In physics, a straight-line graph indicates a **linear relationship** between the variables on the axes—in this case, force ($F$) and acceleration ($a$).

highlights.text: describe the trend observed between force and acceleration

steps.title: Identify the origin
steps.content:
Next, we observe where the line of best fit intersects the axes. The line passes directly through (or very close to) the **origin** $(0, 0)$. 

This means that if there is no force acting on the cart ($F = 0$), the cart does not accelerate ($a = 0$), which aligns with our physical understanding of motion.

steps.title: Conclude direct proportionality
steps.content:
When a graph is a straight line and passes through the origin, it identifies a specific relationship called **direct proportionality**. 

Mathematically, we express this as:
$$F \propto a$$

This confirms the relationship defined in Newton's second law from your data booklet, where the mass ($m$) acts as the constant of proportionality (the gradient of the line):
$$F = ma$$

steps.title: Final Description
steps.content:
To earn the 2 marks for this question, your description should include both key observations:
1. The relationship is **linear** (or a straight line).
2. Force is **directly proportional** to acceleration (or the line passes through the origin).

**Final Answer:** The graph shows a linear relationship where force is directly proportional to acceleration ($F \propto a$).

steps.title: Identify the visual trend
steps.content: By looking at the provided graph, we can see that the data points for Force ($F$) and Acceleration ($a$) form a clear straight line. This suggests a linear relationship between the two variables.

steps.title: Apply proportionality reasoning
steps.content:
In proportionality reasoning, we observe that as the force increases, the acceleration also increases at a constant rate. Since the ratio of $\frac{F}{a}$ remains roughly constant (the slope of the line), we can conclude that force is directly proportional to acceleration.

Mathematically, this is expressed as:
$$F \propto a$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the ratio F/a for several points in the table.

ti84.instructions: Divide several Force values by their corresponding Acceleration values to check for a constant ratio.

desmos.instructions: Plot the points and calculate the slope (F/a) to see if it remains consistent.

calculator.description: Verify that the ratio of Force to Acceleration is approximately constant for different data points.

steps.title: Link to physical law
steps.content:
This proportional relationship is exactly what we expect from Newton's second law, which is found in the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$F = ma$$

Since the mass ($m$) of the cart is fixed, it acts as the constant of proportionality. Therefore, a straight-line graph through the origin confirms the theoretical relationship.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content: To earn both marks, you should state that the graph shows a **linear relationship**, which indicates that **$F$ is directly proportional to $a$** ($F \propto a$).

steps.title: Analyze the visual data
steps.content: To describe the trend, we first look at the visual representation of the data. On the provided graph, the data points for Force ($F$) and Acceleration ($a$) follow a clear straight-line path that passes through (or very close to) the origin. This suggests a **linear relationship**.

steps.title: Apply Newton's Second Law
steps.content:
According to **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**, the theoretical relationship between these variables is defined by Newton's second law, which you can find in the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$F = ma$$

In this experiment, the student is using a **fixed mass** ($m$). Mathematically, this equation takes the form $y = kx$, where:
- $y$ is the Force ($F$)
- $x$ is the Acceleration ($a$)
- $k$ is the constant Mass ($m$)

steps.title: Verify proportionality
steps.content:
Since $m$ is a constant value, the equation $F = ma$ shows that $F$ is **directly proportional** to $a$ ($F \propto a$). 

We can verify this by checking if the ratio $\frac{F}{a}$ (which represents the mass $m$) is approximately constant for the recorded data points.

gdc.instructions: Perform division for various data pairs to see if the result (mass) is constant.

ti84.instructions: Divide the Force values by their corresponding Acceleration values from the table.

desmos.instructions: Divide F by a for selected points.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio F/a for a few points to check for a constant mass.

steps.title: Describe the final trend
steps.content:
Based on the theoretical law and the plotted graph, the trend is a **linear relationship**. This indicates that force is directly proportional to acceleration ($F \propto a$), exactly as predicted by Newton's second law for a constant mass.

To earn both marks, you should mention:
1. That the relationship is **linear**.
2. That Force is **proportional** to Acceleration ($F \propto a$).

parts.content: Based on the graph's slope, determine the effective mass $m$ of the cart. Explain your reasoning.
parts.markscheme:
Determining $m$: The slope of the graph represents the effective mass $m$ of the cart. <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Using the graph:
- Slope $= \Delta F / \Delta a = (7.0 - 1.0) / (3.6 - 0.5) = 6.0 / 3.1 \approx 1.94 \mathrm{~kg}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Link physics to the graph
steps.content:
Newton's second law is given by the equation $$F = ma$$. 

On the provided graph, Force ($F$) is plotted on the $y$-axis and acceleration ($a$) is plotted on the $x$-axis. This matches the mathematical form of a straight line through the origin, $y = mx$, where the gradient (slope) corresponds to the mass ($m$). 

This concept is covered in **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**.

steps.title: Calculate the gradient
steps.content:
To find the effective mass, we calculate the gradient ($m$) using two points from the line of best fit (or the data table). Let's use the first and last data points for maximum precision:
- Point 1: $(0.5, 1.0)$
- Point 2: $(3.6, 7.0)$

Using the gradient formula:
$$\text{gradient} = \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta a} = \frac{F_2 - F_1}{a_2 - a_1}$$

$$\text{gradient} = \frac{7.0 - 1.0}{3.6 - 0.5} = \frac{6.0}{3.1}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the change in F divided by the change in a.

desmos.instructions: Divide the difference in y-values by the difference in x-values.

calculator.description: Calculate the slope from the selected coordinates.

steps.title: Determine effective mass
steps.content:
Performing the division:
$$\frac{6.0}{3.1} \approx 1.9355$$

Rounding to an appropriate number of significant figures, the effective mass is:
$$m \approx 1.94 \text{ kg}$$

In an IB exam, you earn one mark for identifying that the slope represents the mass and another mark for correctly calculating the value from the graph's data.

steps.title: Relate physics to graph geometry
steps.content:
To find the mass $m$ from a graph of Force ($F$) versus Acceleration ($a$), we look at Newton's second law from the **Mechanics** section of the data booklet:

$$F = ma$$

This equation follows the form of a straight line, $y = mx + c$. Since Force is plotted on the $y$-axis and Acceleration on the $x$-axis, the gradient (slope) of the line of best fit directly represents the effective mass $m$.

steps.title: Perform linear regression
steps.content: Using a Graphic Display Calculator (GDC) to perform a linear regression on all the data points provides the most accurate gradient for the line of best fit. We enter the acceleration ($a$) values into the $x$-list ($L_1$) and the force ($F$) values into the $y$-list ($L_2$).

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Statistics or Spreadsheet app.
2. Enter acceleration in Column A and force in Column B.
3. Perform a Linear Regression (y = mx + c or y = a + bx) analysis.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [STAT][1:Edit] and enter acceleration values in L1 and force values in L2.
2. Press [STAT][CALC][4:LinReg(ax+b)].
3. Set Xlist to L1 and Ylist to L2, then select [Calculate].

desmos.instructions:
1. Create a table with the data (x = acceleration, y = force).
2. Type the regression equation: y1 ~ m*x1 + c.

calculator.description: Calculate the linear regression (slope) for the provided data points.

steps.title: Determine the effective mass
steps.content:
From the linear regression analysis, the slope ($m$) is found to be approximately $2.00$. 

$$\text{Slope} \approx 2.00 \text{ kg}$$

If we follow a simpler manual calculation using the endpoints of the data set (as shown in the markscheme):

$$\text{Slope} = \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta a} = \frac{7.0 - 1.0}{3.6 - 0.5} = \frac{6.0}{3.1} \approx 1.94 \text{ kg}$$

Both methods confirm that the mass is approximately $2.0 \text{ kg}$, with the regression providing a value of $2.00 \text{ kg}$ based on the entire data set.

steps.title: Final answer and reasoning
steps.content:
The effective mass of the cart is approximately **$1.94 \text{ kg}$** to **$2.00 \text{ kg}$**. 

**Reasoning:** In the linear relationship $F = ma$, mass $m$ is the proportionality constant. On a graph of $F$ (y-axis) vs $a$ (x-axis), this constant is the gradient. By using a GDC for linear regression, we determine the slope of the best-fit line that minimizes the distance to all data points.

parts.content: Using the data for $F=5.0 \mathrm{~N}$ and $a=2.5 \mathrm{~m\,s^{-2}}$, calculate the mass $m$ of the cart.
parts.markscheme: $m = \frac{F}{a} = \frac{5.0}{2.5} = 2.00 \mathrm{~kg}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content: To calculate the mass $m$, we first identify the specific force and acceleration values provided in the question. This topic is covered in **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**.

steps.title: Select the equation
steps.content: We use Newton's second law, which relates force, mass, and acceleration. You can find this in the **Mechanics** section of your IB data booklet.

steps.title: Rearrange for mass
steps.content:
Since we need to find the mass ($m$), we rearrange the formula by dividing both sides by acceleration ($a$) to isolate $m$:

$$m = \frac{F}{a}$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values into the rearranged equation and solve:

$$\begin{aligned} m &= \frac{5.0}{2.5} \\ m &= 2.0 \text{ kg} \end{aligned}$$

According to the markscheme, the answer is given to three significant figures as $2.00 \text{ kg}$.

ti84.instructions: Enter 5.0 / 2.5 on the home screen and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass by dividing force by acceleration.

steps.title: Link Physics to Geometry
steps.content:
To find the mass from the graph, we look at the relationship between the variables. According to Newton's Second Law, the force $F$ and acceleration $a$ are related by:

$$F = m a$$

In a graph of Force $(F)$ on the vertical axis and Acceleration $(a)$ on the horizontal axis, the mass $m$ represents the **gradient** (slope) of the line, because the equation follows the linear form $y = mx$.

steps.title: Identify coordinates
steps.content:
The question asks us to use a specific data point. From the information provided, we locate the point on the graph where:

- Force ($F$) = $5.0\text{ N}$
- Acceleration ($a$) = $2.5\text{ m s}^{-2}$

Since the relationship is directly proportional, the line of best fit passes through the origin $(0,0)$.

steps.title: Calculate the mass
steps.content:
To calculate the mass $m$, we find the gradient using the ratio of the vertical change to the horizontal change from the origin:

$$\begin{aligned} m &= \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta a} \\ m &= \frac{5.0}{2.5} \end{aligned}$$

$$m = 2.00\text{ kg}$$

gdc.instructions: Perform the division 5.0 / 2.5 on the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter 5.0 divided by 2.5 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass by dividing force by acceleration.

parts.content: If the uncertainty in $F$ is $\pm 0.05 \mathrm{~N}$ and the uncertainty in $a$ is $\pm 0.05 \mathrm{~m\,s^{-2}}$, calculate the percentage uncertainty in the mass $m$ determined in Part 5.
parts.markscheme:
1. Percentage uncertainty in $F$:

$$
\frac{\pm 0.05}{5.0} \times 100 = 1.0\%
$$

<marks> 1 mark </marks>

2. Percentage uncertainty in $a$:

$$
\frac{\pm 0.05}{2.5} \times 100 = 2.0\%
$$

<marks> 1 mark </marks>

3. Total percentage uncertainty in $m$:

$$
\text{Total} = 1.0\% + 2.0\% = 3.0\%
$$

<marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.content:
To find the uncertainty in mass ($m$), we first rearrange Newton's second law, which you can find in the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$F = ma \implies m = \frac{F}{a}$$

When quantities are multiplied or divided, the IB curriculum requires us to **add the percentage uncertainties** of each individual value to find the total percentage uncertainty. We will use the values from the data set where $F = 5.0\text{ N}$ and $a = 2.5\text{ m s}^{-2}$ as our reference point.

steps.title: Percentage uncertainty in Force
steps.content:
First, calculate the percentage uncertainty for the force ($F$). We use the formula:

$$\text{Percentage uncertainty} = \frac{\text{absolute uncertainty}}{\text{measured value}} \times 100$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\frac{0.05\text{ N}}{5.0\text{ N}} \times 100 = 1.0\%$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 0.05 divided by 5.0, then multiply by 100.

calculator.description: Calculate the percentage uncertainty for a force of 5.0 N with an error of 0.05 N.

steps.title: Percentage uncertainty in Acceleration
steps.content:
Next, we do the same for the acceleration ($a$):

$$\frac{0.05\text{ m s}^{-2}}{2.5\text{ m s}^{-2}} \times 100 = 2.0\%$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 0.05 divided by 2.5, then multiply by 100.

calculator.description: Calculate the percentage uncertainty for an acceleration of 2.5 m/s² with an error of 0.05 m/s².

steps.title: Total percentage uncertainty
steps.content:
Finally, to find the total percentage uncertainty in the mass ($m$), we add the percentage uncertainties of $F$ and $a$ together:

$$\text{Total } \% \text{ uncertainty} = 1.0\% + 2.0\% = 3.0\%$$

calculator.description: Sum the individual percentage uncertainties.

steps.title: Identify Relationship and Variables
steps.content:
To find the mass $m$, we rearrange Newton's second law. From the data table and markscheme, we'll use the specific data point where $F = 5.0\text{ N}$ and $a = 2.5\text{ m s}^{-2}$.

$$\begin{aligned} F &= ma \\ m &= \frac{F}{a} \end{aligned}$$

This question uses **Topic A.2 Forces and momentum**. Understanding how to manipulate $F=ma$ is key here.

steps.title: Determine Maximum Mass Value
steps.content:
In a fraction $m = \frac{F}{a}$, the maximum possible value for $m$ occurs when the numerator ($F$) is as large as possible and the denominator ($a$) is as small as possible.

Using the given uncertainties:
- Max force: $F_{\text{max}} = 5.0 + 0.05 = 5.05\text{ N}$
- Min acceleration: $a_{\text{min}} = 2.5 - 0.05 = 2.45\text{ m s}^{-2}$

$$m_{\text{max}} = \frac{5.05}{2.45} \approx 2.0612\text{ kg}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the maximum possible mass.

steps.title: Determine Minimum Mass Value
steps.content:
Similarly, the minimum possible value for $m$ occurs when the numerator is as small as possible and the denominator is as large as possible.

- Min force: $F_{\text{min}} = 5.0 - 0.05 = 4.95\text{ N}$
- Max acceleration: $a_{\text{max}} = 2.5 + 0.05 = 2.55\text{ m s}^{-2}$

$$m_{\text{min}} = \frac{4.95}{2.55} \approx 1.9412\text{ kg}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the minimum possible mass.

steps.title: Calculate Percentage Uncertainty
steps.content:
Using the Max/Min method, the absolute uncertainty $\Delta m$ is half the range of the calculated values. The percentage uncertainty is then calculated relative to the nominal mass ($2.0\text{ kg}$).

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta m &= \frac{m_{\text{max}} - m_{\text{min}}}{2} \\ \Delta m &= \frac{2.0612 - 1.9412}{2} = 0.06 \\ \% \text{ Uncertainty} &= \frac{0.06}{2.0} \times 100 = 3\% \end{aligned}$$

This matches the markscheme requirement by summing the individual percentage uncertainties: $1\% (F) + 2\% (a) = 3\%$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the uncertainty using the max/min range and convert to percentage.

ti84.instructions: Enter (2.0612 - 1.9412) / 2 to get the absolute uncertainty, then divide by 2 and multiply by 100.

calculator.description: Calculate the final percentage uncertainty.

steps.title: Relate mass to gradient
steps.content:
In this investigation, the relationship is given by Newton's second law, which shows that mass $m$ is the ratio of force to acceleration. On a graph of Force ($F$) against acceleration ($a$), the mass is represented by the gradient (slope) of the line.

$$\begin{aligned} F &= ma \\ m &= \frac{F}{a} = \text{gradient} \end{aligned}$$

In a **Graphical Gradient Analysis**, the uncertainty in the mass is determined by how much the slope can vary while still passing through the error bars. This variation is calculated by finding the percentage difference between the best-fit line and the lines of maximum or minimum slope.

steps.title: Percentage uncertainty in Force
steps.content:
To estimate the total uncertainty in the gradient, we first find the percentage uncertainty for a representative data point. Let's use the point where $F = 5.0\text{ N}$.

$$\text{Percentage uncertainty in } F = \frac{\text{absolute uncertainty}}{\text{measured value}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Percentage uncertainty in } F = \frac{0.05\text{ N}}{5.0\text{ N}} \times 100 = 1.0\%$$

calculator.description: Calculate the percentage uncertainty for Force.

steps.title: Percentage uncertainty in acceleration
steps.content:
Next, we find the percentage uncertainty for the corresponding acceleration value, $a = 2.5\text{ m s}^{-2}$. This vertical and horizontal uncertainty determines the size of the error bars that the lines of maximum and minimum slope must pass through.

$$\text{Percentage uncertainty in } a = \frac{0.05\text{ m s}^{-2}}{2.5\text{ m s}^{-2}} \times 100 = 2.0\%$$

calculator.description: Calculate the percentage uncertainty for acceleration.

steps.title: Calculate total percentage uncertainty
steps.content:
For quantities related by division ($m = F/a$), the total percentage uncertainty is the sum of the individual percentage uncertainties. 

In **Graphical Gradient Analysis**, this total sum represents the percentage difference between the gradient of the best-fit line and the gradients of the lines of maximum and minimum slope.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Total \% uncertainty in } m &= 1.0\% + 2.0\% \\ &= 3.0\% \end{aligned}$$

This accounts for the full markscheme requirements: 1 mark for each individual percentage calculation and 1 mark for the final sum.

calculator.description: Sum the percentage uncertainties.

questions.specification:
A rescue airplane flies at a constant horizontal velocity $u$ at a height $H$ above a level plain. A relief package is released from the airplane at point $P$ and follows a parabolic trajectory, as shown in the diagram below.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-9fd70bbb-8824-44a1-bd5a-bea8b4184f02-1772167695732/63523e81-1cf2-47b4-82b4-faa6e493866f.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.3 Projectile motion

parts.content: On the diagram, draw two arrows at point $Q$ representing the horizontal and vertical components of the package's velocity. Label these components $v_x$ and $v_y$ respectively.
parts.markscheme:
- Horizontal arrow ($v_x$) pointing to the right from point $Q$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Vertical arrow ($v_y$) pointing vertically downward from point $Q$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Arrows must start at point Q. The length of the horizontal arrow should be approximately equal to the initial velocity vector if shown, and the vertical arrow should be pointing down.</note>

parts.content: The airplane is flying at a height $H = 125\text{ m}$. Calculate the time $T$ taken for the package to reach the ground. (Assume $g = 10\text{ m s}^{-2}$ and air resistance is negligible).
parts.markscheme:
- Use kinematic equation for vertical motion: $H = \dfrac{1}{2}gT^2$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate $T$: $T = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 125}{10}} = 5.0\text{ s}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State the horizontal component of the package's velocity at the moment of impact in terms of $u$.
parts.markscheme: - Horizontal velocity remains $u$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Show that the horizontal distance $R$ (range) travelled by the package from the point of release to the impact point is given by $R = u \sqrt{\dfrac{2H}{g}}$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the range equation: $R = uT$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Substitute expression for time $T = \sqrt{\dfrac{2H}{g}}$ to get $R = u \sqrt{\dfrac{2H}{g}}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain why the horizontal velocity of the package remains constant while its vertical velocity increases during the flight.
parts.markscheme:
- In the absence of air resistance, there is no horizontal force acting on the package, so by Newton's first law, the horizontal velocity remains constant <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The force of gravity (weight) acts vertically on the package, causing a constant vertical acceleration $g$, which increases the vertical velocity over time <marks>1 mark</marks>
