questions.specification:
A student investigates the power output of a small photovoltaic (PV) panel under changing light conditions on an overcast day. At five different times during the day, the student connects a range of resistive loads and records the corresponding current and voltage from the panel. The graph below shows the I-V data for midday (12:00) when irradiance was measured at $720 \pm 20 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$. The panel has an area of $0.030 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_07_14_11e170205bbe661f58b1g-09.jpg?height=683&width=1212&top_left_y=535&top_left_x=425)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.3 Power and efficiency
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3 Power and efficiency
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Multiple parts focus on calculating electrical power from I–V data, identifying maximum power point, and computing efficiency from input irradiance and panel area (power and efficiency concepts).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic B - The particulate nature of matter > B.5 Current and circuits > B.5.4 Power and Resistor Configurations
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.5.4 Power and Resistor Configurations
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses I–V measurements with different resistive loads to determine output power (P=IV) and how power varies with load/voltage, aligning with circuit power relationships for resistive configurations.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic B - The particulate nature of matter > B.5 Current and circuits > B.5.2 Electrical Current and Voltage
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.5.2 Electrical Current and Voltage
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Interpreting an I–V characteristic curve and extracting corresponding current and voltage at the maximum power point requires understanding of current–voltage behavior in electrical measurements.

parts.content: Use the I-V data to calculate power at each point. Then describe how the power output changes with voltage and explain why it reaches a maximum.
parts.markscheme:
- Power values correctly calculated for each data point using $P=I V$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Power increases with voltage up to a maximum, then decreases; maximum occurs due to the trade-off between current drop and voltage gain. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the power formula
steps.content:
To determine the power output from the $I-V$ data, we use the fundamental definition of electrical power. Power is the product of the potential difference (voltage) across the component and the current flowing through it. 

This concept is covered in **Topic B.5: Current and circuits** of the IB Physics syllabus.

$$P = VI$$

steps.title: Point-by-point power calculation
steps.content:
We extract the $(V, I)$ coordinates from the six data points shown on the graph and calculate the power $P$ for each:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Point 1: } P &= 0.0 \text{ V} \times 1.25 \text{ A} = 0.00 \text{ W} \\ \text{Point 2: } P &= 1.0 \text{ V} \times 1.20 \text{ A} = 1.20 \text{ W} \\ \text{Point 3: } P &= 2.0 \text{ V} \times 1.15 \text{ A} = 2.30 \text{ W} \\ \text{Point 4: } P &= 3.0 \text{ V} \times 1.05 \text{ A} = 3.15 \text{ W} \\ \text{Point 5: } P &= 3.5 \text{ V} \times 0.90 \text{ A} = 3.15 \text{ W} \\ \text{Point 6: } P &= 4.0 \text{ V} \times 0.45 \text{ A} = 1.80 \text{ W} \end{aligned}$$

Correctly calculating these values earns the first mark.

gdc.instructions: Enter the voltage values in List 1 (L1) and current values in List 2 (L2). Create List 3 (L3) defined as L1 * L2 to see all power values at once.

ti84.instructions: Simply multiply the V and I values for each pair: 1.0*1.20, 2.0*1.15, 3.0*1.05, 3.5*0.90, 4.0*0.45.

desmos.instructions: Create a table with x-values as Voltage and y-values as Current. In a new cell, type x1 * y1 to generate the power list.

calculator.description: Calculate power for each point on the GDC.

highlights.text: Power values correctly calculated for each data point using $P=I V$.

steps.title: Describe the power trend
steps.content:
Looking at our calculated values, we can describe how power changes with voltage:
- As voltage increases from $0.0 \text{ V}$ to approximately $3.0 \text{ V}$, the power output **increases**.
- The power reaches a **maximum** value of approximately $3.15 \text{ W}$ between $3.0 \text{ V}$ and $3.5 \text{ V}$.
- Beyond $3.5 \text{ V}$, the power output **decreases** sharply as the voltage approaches the open-circuit voltage.

steps.title: Explain the trade-off
steps.content:
The existence of a maximum power point is due to the **trade-off** between voltage gain and current loss:

1. **Initial Phase:** As $V$ increases, $I$ decreases only slightly. The gain in $V$ outweighs the small drop in $I$, so the product $P = VI$ increases.
2. **The Peak:** At the maximum power point, the rate of increase in voltage is perfectly balanced by the rate of decrease in current.
3. **Final Phase:** At high voltages, the current drops off very sharply toward zero. Here, the large loss in current is much greater than the gain in voltage, causing the total power to fall.

highlights.text: Power increases with voltage up to a maximum, then decreases; maximum occurs due to the trade-off between current drop and voltage gain.

parts.content: Determine the value of the maximum power output and state the corresponding current and voltage.
parts.markscheme:
- Power $=V I=6.0 \times 0.30=1.80 \mathrm{~W}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Stated clearly with units; point corresponds to max value on graph. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Locate the 'knee' point
steps.content:
To find the maximum power output from an $I-V$ characteristic curve of a photovoltaic cell, we look for the **'knee'** of the graph. This is the point where the curve transitions from being relatively flat (where current is high) to dropping sharply towards zero current as the voltage increases.

Looking at the provided graph, this transition occurs right before the vertical drop, which is at the point where the voltage is $6.0\text{ V}$ and the current is $0.30\text{ A}$.

steps.title: Read current and voltage
steps.content:
By visually identifying the knee, we can read the specific values for the maximum power point ($P_{max}$):

- **Voltage ($V$):** $6.0\text{ V}$
- **Current ($I$):** $0.30\text{ A}$

Note that the grid marks are every $0.2\text{ V}$ on the x-axis and every $0.02\text{ A}$ on the y-axis, making these readings precise.

steps.title: Calculate maximum power
steps.content:
Using the definition of electrical power from **Topic B.5: Current and circuits**, we calculate the power output at this point:

$$\text{Power } (P) = V \times I$$

Substituting our values:

$$P = 6.0\text{ V} \times 0.30\text{ A} = 1.80\text{ W}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply 6.0 by 0.30 in the calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter 6.0 * 0.30 on the home screen and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the product of voltage and current to find power.

steps.title: State the final values
steps.content:
The maximum power output is found at the knee of the curve. 

- **Maximum Power:** $1.80\text{ W}$
- **Current:** $0.30\text{ A}$
- **Voltage:** $6.0\text{ V}$

According to the markscheme, you earn one mark for the correct power calculation and one mark for clearly stating the corresponding current and voltage with units.

steps.title: Define Power in Circuits
steps.content:
To find the power output from an $I-V$ graph, we use the fundamental relation between current, voltage, and power. This concept is covered in **Topic B.5: Current and Circuits**.

The power $P$ delivered by the panel is the product of the potential difference $V$ and the current $I$:

$$P = V I$$

steps.title: Sample Representative Data Points
steps.content:
To find the maximum power, we need to sample several points $(V, I)$ from the curve, specifically around the 'knee' of the graph where both $V$ and $I$ are relatively high. 

Let's pick three points to compare:
1.  **Point A:** $V = 5.0\text{ V}$, $I = 0.35\text{ A}$
2.  **Point B:** $V = 6.0\text{ V}$, $I = 0.30\text{ A}$
3.  **Point C:** $V = 7.0\text{ V}$, $I = 0.15\text{ A}$

steps.title: Calculate and Compare Power
steps.content:
Now we calculate the power for each of our sampled points to see which is the highest:

$$\begin{aligned} P_A &= 5.0 \times 0.35 = 1.75\text{ W} \\ P_B &= 6.0 \times 0.30 = 1.80\text{ W} \\ P_C &= 7.0 \times 0.15 = 1.05\text{ W} \end{aligned}$$

Comparing these values, the power is clearly maximized at **Point B**.

gdc.instructions: Perform multiplication for each pair of coordinates.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the voltage and current values for each point.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the products to find power values.

calculator.description: Calculate power for the sampled points.

steps.title: Final Result and Units
steps.content:
The maximum power occurs at the 'knee' of the curve. 

- **Maximum Power:** $1.80\text{ W}$
- **Current at max power:** $0.30\text{ A}$
- **Voltage at max power:** $6.0\text{ V}$

Make sure to include the units for all three values as required by the markscheme.

parts.content: Calculate the efficiency of the panel at midday using the maximum power output, irradiance, and area. Include appropriate units.
parts.markscheme:
- Use: $\eta=\frac{P_{\max }}{E \cdot A}=\frac{1.80}{720 \cdot 0.030} \approx 0.0833$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Final answer: $\eta=8.3 \%$. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify maximum power point
steps.content:
To find the maximum power output ($P_{\text{max}}$), we look for the "knee" of the I-V curve. This is the point where the product of voltage ($V$) and current ($I$) is at its highest value.

From the graph at midday:
- The voltage at the knee is $V = 6.0\text{ V}$
- The current at the knee is $I = 0.30\text{ A}$

steps.title: Calculate maximum power output
steps.content:
Using the relationship for electrical power, we calculate the output power at this point:

$$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{max}} &= V \times I \\ P_{\text{max}} &= 6.0\text{ V} \times 0.30\text{ A} \\ P_{\text{max}} &= 1.80\text{ W} \end{aligned}$$

This represents the maximum useful power the panel produces under these specific light conditions.

gdc.instructions: Input 6.0 * 0.30 into the main calculation screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the product of voltage and current to find power.

steps.title: Determine total input power
steps.content:
The total power incident on the panel ($P_{\text{in}}$) depends on the solar irradiance and the surface area of the panel:

$$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{in}} &= \text{Irradiance} \times \text{Area} \\ P_{\text{in}} &= 720\text{ W/m}^{2} \times 0.030\text{ m}^{2} \\ P_{\text{in}} &= 21.6\text{ W} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Multiply the irradiance by the panel area to find input power.

steps.title: Calculate solar panel efficiency
steps.content:
Efficiency ($\eta$) is the ratio of useful power output to total power input. This concept is covered in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

$$\begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{P_{\text{max}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \\ \eta &= \frac{1.80\text{ W}}{21.6\text{ W}} \\ \eta &\approx 0.0833 \end{aligned}$$

To express this as a percentage, we multiply by $100$:
$$\eta = 8.3\%$$

calculator.description: Divide the output power by the input power.

parts.content: Estimate the uncertainty in the efficiency based on the uncertainty in the irradiance reading.
parts.markscheme:
- Recalculate using $E=700 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}: \eta^{\prime}=\frac{1.80}{700 \cdot 0.030} \approx 0.0857$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Difference in efficiency: $\Delta \eta \approx 0.0024 \Rightarrow 0.24 \%$. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To find the uncertainty in efficiency using extreme values, we first identify the nominal values and the uncertainty range for irradiance:

- **Maximum Power Output ($P_{out}$):** $1.80\text{ W}$ (determined from the peak of the $I-V$ curve).
- **Nominal Irradiance ($I$):** $720\text{ W/m}^2$
- **Irradiance Uncertainty:** $\pm 20\text{ W/m}^2$
- **Area ($A$):** $0.030\text{ m}^2$

The uncertainty in irradiance gives us a range from $700\text{ W/m}^2$ to $740\text{ W/m}^2$.

steps.title: Calculate nominal efficiency
steps.content:
Efficiency ($\eta$) is defined as the ratio of useful power output to total power input. This concept is covered in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

$$P_{in} = \text{Irradiance} \times \text{Area}$$
$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{P_{out}}{I \times A}$$

Using the nominal irradiance ($720\text{ W/m}^2$):
$$\eta_{nom} = \frac{1.80}{720 \times 0.030} \approx 0.0833$$

gdc.instructions: Perform the division and multiplication in one step.

ti84.instructions: Enter the fraction in the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the nominal efficiency value.

steps.title: Calculate extreme efficiency
steps.content:
The **Extreme Values Calculation** approach involves recalculating the final result using a boundary value of the uncertain variable. We will use the lower bound of irradiance, $700\text{ W/m}^2$, to find a new efficiency ($\eta'$):

$$\eta' = \frac{1.80}{700 \times 0.030} \approx 0.0857$$

gdc.instructions: Recalculate using the lower irradiance value.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation into the main screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the efficiency at the lower boundary of irradiance.

steps.title: Determine the uncertainty
steps.content:
The uncertainty in efficiency ($\Delta\eta$) is the absolute difference between our nominal value and the extreme value:

$$\Delta\eta = |\eta' - \eta_{nom}|$$
$$\Delta\eta = |0.0857 - 0.0833| = 0.0024$$

To express this as a percentage, we multiply by $100$:
$$\text{Uncertainty} = 0.24\%$$

gdc.instructions: Subtract the two previously calculated efficiencies.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the nominal result from the extreme result.

calculator.description: Calculate the difference between the two efficiency results.

steps.title: Identify the efficiency formula
steps.content:
To find the uncertainty in efficiency ($\eta$), we first recall the definition of efficiency from **Topic A.3.3 Power and efficiency**. 

Efficiency is the ratio of useful power output to the total power input:
$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$$

The power input from the sun is calculated as the product of irradiance ($I$) and the panel's area ($A$):
$$P_{in} = I \times A$$

Substituting this into our efficiency formula gives:
$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{I \times A}$$

This shows that the efficiency is inversely proportional to the irradiance ($I$).

steps.title: Calculate nominal efficiency
steps.content:
From the previous parts of this investigation, the maximum power output ($P_{out}$) was determined to be $1.80 \text{ W}$. We use the given values for irradiance and area to find the nominal efficiency at midday.

$$\begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{1.80 \text{ W}}{720 \text{ W/m}^2 \times 0.030 \text{ m}^2} \\ \eta &\approx 0.0833 \text{ (or } 8.33\% \text{)} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate irradiance fractional uncertainty
steps.content:
The **Fractional Uncertainty Rule** states that if a quantity is multiplied or divided by a variable, the fractional uncertainty of that quantity is equal to the fractional uncertainty of the variable (assuming other terms are constant).

Since we are only considering the uncertainty in irradiance ($I$):
$$\frac{\Delta \eta}{\eta} = \frac{\Delta I}{I}$$

Given $I = 720 \text{ W/m}^2$ and $\Delta I = 20 \text{ W/m}^2$:
$$\text{Fractional uncertainty} = \frac{20}{720} \approx 0.0278 \text{ (or } 2.78\% \text{)}$$

steps.title: Find absolute uncertainty
steps.content:
Finally, we multiply the nominal efficiency by the fractional uncertainty to find the absolute uncertainty in efficiency ($\Delta \eta$):

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta \eta &= \eta \times \left( \frac{\Delta I}{I} \right) \\ \Delta \eta &= 0.0833 \times \frac{20}{720} \\ \Delta \eta &\approx 0.0023 \end{aligned}$$

As a percentage, this is approximately $0.23\%$. The markscheme accepts values around $0.002$ or $0.2\%$ based on these calculations.

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, type the expression (1.80 / (720 * 0.030)) * (20 / 720) and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter (1.80 / (720 * 0.030)) * (20 / 720) into the main screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type (1.80 / (720 * 0.030)) * (20 / 720) into the calculator to see the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the absolute uncertainty in efficiency by multiplying the nominal value by the fractional uncertainty of irradiance.

questions.specification: A mountain hiker of mass $65 \text{ kg}$ climbs $500 \text{ m}$ vertically over the course of 2 hours. Assuming $25\%$ of the chemical energy from food is converted into useful work, how much energy must the hiker consume?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.3 Power and efficiency
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3 Power and efficiency
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses an efficiency (25%) to relate useful mechanical work (gain in gravitational potential energy) to chemical energy consumed; efficiency is a core part of this calculation.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Requires calculating the useful work done against gravity via energy transfer as gravitational potential energy change (mgh).

options.markscheme:
Useful work done against gravity:
$$W = mgh = 65 \times 9.81 \times 500 \approx 3.19 \times 10^5 \text{ J}$$

Efficiency $\eta = 0.25$, so input energy required:
$$E_{\text{in}} = \frac{W}{\eta} = \frac{3.19 \times 10^5}{0.25} \approx 1.28 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$$

Rounding to 2 significant figures gives $1.3 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$.

options.markscheme:
Useful energy output: $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 = \frac{1}{2}(12.5)(6.0)^2 = 225\text{ J}$

Energy lost: $320 - 225 = 95\text{ J}$

Percentage loss: $\frac{95}{320} \times 100\% = 29.68\dots\% \approx 30\%$.

What is the total work done by the force to stretch the spring from an extension of $$0 \text{ m}$$ to $$0.60 \text{ m}$$?

parts.content: State the principle of conservation of energy.
parts.markscheme:
- Statement that energy cannot be created or destroyed <marks>B1</marks>
- Statement that energy can only be transformed from one form to another **OR** the total energy in a closed system remains constant <marks>B1</marks>
2 marks total

steps.title: Recall the fundamental law
steps.content: The principle of conservation of energy is a fundamental law in physics covered in **Topic A.3.1**. It describes the behavior of energy in any physical process. To earn full marks in an IB exam, you need to provide a two-part definition that covers both what energy *cannot* do and what it *actually* does.

steps.title: Define energy's permanence
steps.content: The first part of the formal definition states that the total quantity of energy is fixed. Specifically, you must state that energy cannot be created or destroyed. This is the first requirement for the markscheme.

highlights.text: Statement that energy cannot be created or destroyed

steps.title: Describe energy transformation
steps.content:
The second part of the definition explains what happens to energy instead of being created or destroyed. It can only be transformed from one form to another (for example, from kinetic energy to thermal energy). 

Alternatively, you can phrase this by saying the total energy in a closed (isolated) system remains constant. Both versions are acceptable for the second mark.

highlights.text: Statement that energy can only be transformed from one form to another OR the total energy in a closed system remains constant

steps.title: State the final principle
steps.content:
Combining these two parts gives the complete principle:

**Energy cannot be created or destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another, or the total energy of a closed system remains constant.**

parts.content: Explain how the principle of conservation of energy applies to a pendulum swinging in the absence of air resistance.
parts.markscheme:
- Explanation that gravitational potential energy at the highest points is transformed into kinetic energy at the lowest point (and vice versa) <marks>B1</marks>
- Explanation that in the absence of air resistance, the total mechanical energy (sum of gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy) remains constant throughout the motion <marks>B1</marks>
2 marks total

steps.title: Identify energy types
steps.content:
In a swinging pendulum, we are primarily dealing with two types of energy from **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**: 

1. **Gravitational Potential Energy ($\Delta E_p$)**: Energy stored due to the height of the pendulum bob.
2. **Kinetic Energy ($E_k$)**: Energy the bob has due to its motion.

According to the data booklet, these are calculated as:

$$\Delta E_p = mg\Delta h$$
$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

steps.title: Explain the energy transformation
steps.content:
The principle of conservation of energy states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed. 

As the pendulum swings:
- At the **highest points** of the swing, the bob momentarily stops ($v=0$), meaning its energy is entirely **gravitational potential energy**.
- As it falls toward the **equilibrium (lowest) point**, this potential energy is transformed into **kinetic energy**, reaching maximum speed at the bottom.
- As it rises again, kinetic energy is transformed back into gravitational potential energy.

steps.title: State the conservation law
steps.content:
Because the system is in the **absence of air resistance**, there are no non-conservative forces doing work to remove energy from the system (like friction or drag).

Therefore, the **total mechanical energy** (the sum of $E_p + E_k$) remains **constant** throughout the entire motion. Any loss in $E_p$ is exactly balanced by a gain in $E_k$.

steps.title: Identify energy types
steps.content:
To explain the conservation of energy in a pendulum, we first identify the two types of mechanical energy involved: **gravitational potential energy** ($E_p$) and **kinetic energy** ($E_k$). These concepts are fundamental parts of **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

From the IB data booklet, we define these as:

$$\Delta E_p = mg\Delta h$$
$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

steps.title: Describe the energy transformation
steps.content:
As the pendulum swings, energy is continuously transformed between these two forms:

*   **At the highest points:** The pendulum momentarily stops ($v=0$), meaning all its mechanical energy is stored as **gravitational potential energy** ($E_p$).
*   **At the lowest point:** The pendulum reaches its maximum speed and minimum height. Here, the $E_p$ has been transformed into **kinetic energy** ($E_k$).

This continuous exchange allows the pendulum to swing back and forth.

steps.title: Apply conservation principle
steps.content:
The principle of conservation of energy states that in an isolated system, energy cannot be created or destroyed. In this scenario, we are told there is an **absence of air resistance**.

Because there are no dissipative forces (like friction or air resistance) doing work on the system, no energy is lost as heat. Therefore, the **total mechanical energy** ($E_{total}$), which is the sum of $E_k$ and $E_p$, remains **constant** throughout the entire motion:

$$E_{total} = E_k + E_p = \text{constant}$$

questions.specification: A winch is used to raise a $150.0\ \text{kg}$ crate vertically at a constant acceleration of $2.00\ \text{m s}^{-2}$ over a distance of $10.0\ \text{m}$.

parts.content: Calculate the net force acting on the crate.
parts.markscheme:
$F_{\text{net}} = ma = 150.0 \times 2.00$ <marks>M1</marks>

$= 300\ \text{N}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify known variables
steps.content:
To find the net force, we first need to identify the mass and the acceleration of the crate from the problem description. Note that while a distance of $10.0\ \text{m}$ is mentioned, it isn't required to calculate the net force.

*   Mass ($m$) = $150.0\ \text{kg}$
*   Acceleration ($a$) = $2.00\ \text{m s}^{-2}$

steps.title: Apply Newton's Second Law
steps.content:
Newton's Second Law states that the net force acting on an object is proportional to its acceleration. We can find this using the formula from the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$F = ma$$

steps.title: Calculate the net force
steps.content:
Now, we substitute our values into the equation to find the net force ($F_{\text{net}}$):

$$\begin{aligned} F_{\text{net}} &= 150.0\ \text{kg} \times 2.00\ \text{m s}^{-2} \\ F_{\text{net}} &= 300\ \text{N} \end{aligned}$$

In an IB exam, showing this substitution clearly is worth the first mark (**M1**).

gdc.instructions: Multiply the values in the calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the product into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the product of mass and acceleration.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The net force acting on the crate is $300\ \text{N}$. 

Remember that the net force is the vector sum of all forces (like tension and weight), but Newton's Second Law allows us to find its value directly using mass and acceleration.

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Work-Energy Principle**, we first list the known values from the question. We assume the crate starts from rest ($u = 0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$).

- Mass of the crate, $m = 150.0\text{ kg}$
- Constant acceleration, $a = 2.00\text{ m s}^{-2}$
- Distance moved, $s = 10.0\text{ m}$
- Initial velocity, $u = 0\text{ m s}^{-1}$

steps.title: Determine final velocity squared
steps.content:
We can find the final velocity squared ($v^2$) using the kinematic equations from **Topic A.1**. Since we know $u$, $a$, and $s$, we use:

$$v^2 = u^2 + 2as$$

Substituting our values:
$$\begin{aligned} v^2 &= 0^2 + 2(2.00)(10.0) \\ v^2 &= 40.0 \text{ m}^2 \text{ s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the value of v squared.

steps.title: Calculate change in kinetic energy
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the change in kinetic energy ($\Delta E_k$). Since the crate starts from rest, the change in kinetic energy is equal to the final kinetic energy:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

Using our previous result for $v^2$:
$$\begin{aligned} \Delta E_k &= \frac{1}{2}(150.0)(40.0) \\ \Delta E_k &= 3000 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the kinetic energy.

steps.title: Solve for net force
steps.content:
The **Work-Energy Theorem** from **Topic A.3** states that the net work done ($W_{\text{net}}$) on an object is equal to its change in kinetic energy ($\Delta E_k$). 

We know that work is force times distance in the direction of motion:
$$W_{\text{net}} = F_{\text{net}} \times s$$

Setting $W_{\text{net}} = \Delta E_k$:
$$\begin{aligned} F_{\text{net}} \times 10.0 &= 3000 \\ F_{\text{net}} &= \frac{3000}{10.0} \\ F_{\text{net}} &= 300 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$

This confirms the net force acting on the crate is $300 \text{ N}$.

calculator.description: Calculate the net force by dividing work by distance.

steps.title: Identify mass and acceleration
steps.content:
First, we need to extract the known values for the crate from the problem description. We are looking for its mass and its vertical acceleration.

- Mass ($m$) = $150.0\ \text{kg}$
- Acceleration ($a$) = $2.00\ \text{m s}^{-2}$

steps.title: Calculate the weight force
steps.content:
The weight ($W$) is the downward gravitational force acting on the crate. To find this, we use the mass and the gravitational acceleration constant $g$ found in the **Physical Constants** section of your data booklet.

$$\begin{aligned} W &= m \times g \\ W &= 150.0 \times 9.8 \\ W &= 1470\ \text{N} \end{aligned}$$

This force acts vertically downwards.

calculator.description: Calculate the weight force by multiplying mass by gravity.

steps.title: Determine the upward tension
steps.content:
For the crate to accelerate upwards, the tension ($T$) from the winch must overcome the weight and provide the extra force for acceleration. Using Newton's Second Law, the required upward tension is:

$$\begin{aligned} T &= m(g + a) \\ T &= 150.0 \times (9.8 + 2.00) \\ T &= 150.0 \times 11.8 \\ T &= 1770\ \text{N} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate total upward tension.

steps.title: Calculate the net force
steps.content:
In a **Resultant Force Analysis**, the net force ($F_{\text{net}}$) is the difference between the individual upward and downward forces:

$$\begin{aligned} F_{\text{net}} &= T - W \\ F_{\text{net}} &= 1770\ \text{N} - 1470\ \text{N} \\ F_{\text{net}} &= 300\ \text{N} \end{aligned}$$

According to the markscheme, we can also find this directly using the formula from **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**:

$$F = ma$$

$$150.0 \times 2.00 = 300\ \text{N}$$

calculator.description: Calculate net force as the difference between tension and weight.

parts.content: Determine the total upward force exerted by the winch.
parts.markscheme:
$F_{\text{total}} = F_{\text{net}} + mg = 300 + 150.0 \times 9.81$ <marks>M1</marks>

$= 1770\ \text{N}$ ($1.77 \times 10^3\ \text{N}$) <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the forces
steps.content:
To find the total upward force, we first need to identify the forces acting on the crate. There are two main forces:
1. The **upward force** ($F$) exerted by the winch.
2. The **weight** ($W = mg$) acting downwards due to gravity.

Since the crate is accelerating upwards, the net force ($F_{\text{net}}$) must also be directed upwards.

steps.title: Apply Newton's Second Law
steps.content:
From **Topic A.2 Forces and momentum**, we use Newton's Second Law to relate the net force to acceleration:

$$\text{Net Force } (F_{\text{net}}) = ma$$

In this vertical motion, the net force is the difference between the upward force and the weight:

$$F - mg = ma$$

steps.title: Rearrange for upward force
steps.content:
We need to solve for the total upward force $F$. Rearranging the equation gives:

$$F = ma + mg$$

This shows that the winch must provide enough force to both accelerate the crate ($ma$) and overcome its weight ($mg$).

steps.title: Calculate the total force
steps.content:
Now, substitute the known values into the equation. Using $m = 150.0\text{ kg}$, $a = 2.00\text{ m s}^{-2}$, and $g = 9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$:

$$\begin{aligned} F &= (150.0 \times 2.00) + (150.0 \times 9.81) \\ F &= 300 + 1471.5 \\ F &= 1771.5 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures (matching the precision of the acceleration $2.00$), we get $1770\text{ N}$.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression (150 * 2) + (150 * 9.81) on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Input 150(2 + 9.81) into the calculator to find the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the total upward force by adding the net force and the weight.

steps.title: Analyze the Work-Energy approach
steps.content:
To find the force exerted by the winch using the Work-Energy Theorem, we recognize that the work done by the winch ($W$) must equal the total change in mechanical energy of the crate. This consists of the change in gravitational potential energy ($\Delta E_p$) and the change in kinetic energy ($\Delta E_k$).

$$W = \Delta E_p + \Delta E_k$$

This method is covered in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

steps.title: Calculate potential energy change
steps.content:
First, we calculate the gain in gravitational potential energy as the crate is raised. Using the formula from the data booklet:

$$\Delta E_p = mg\Delta h$$

Substituting the known values ($m = 150.0\text{ kg}$, $g = 9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$, and $\Delta h = 10.0\text{ m}$):

$$\Delta E_p = 150.0 \times 9.81 \times 10.0 = 14715\text{ J}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication 150 * 9.81 * 10 into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression 150 * 9.81 * 10 into a new row.

calculator.description: Calculate the change in gravitational potential energy.

steps.title: Calculate kinetic energy change
steps.content:
Next, we find the change in kinetic energy. Since the crate starts from rest ($u = 0$), we first find the final velocity $v$ using the kinematic equation:

$$v^2 = u^2 + 2as$$
$$v^2 = 0 + 2(2.00)(10.0) = 40.0\text{ m}^2\text{ s}^{-2}$$

Now, calculate $\Delta E_k$:

$$\Delta E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 = 0.5 \times 150.0 \times 40.0 = 3000\text{ J}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the change in kinetic energy.

steps.title: Calculate total upward force
steps.content:
The total work done by the winch is the sum of these energy changes:

$$W = 14715 + 3000 = 17715\text{ J}$$

Work is also defined as $W = F \times s$. We can rearrange this to solve for the total force ($F$):

$$\begin{aligned} F &= \frac{W}{s} \\ F &= \frac{17715}{10.0} \\ F &= 1771.5\text{ N} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get **$1770\text{ N}$** (or $1.77 \times 10^3\text{ N}$).

calculator.description: Calculate the final force by dividing total work by distance.

parts.content: Calculate the total work done by the winch.
parts.markscheme:
$W = F_{\text{total}} \times d = 1771.5 \times 10.0$ <marks>M1</marks>

$= 1.77 \times 10^4\ \text{J}$ ($17700\ \text{J}$) <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the forces
steps.content:
To find the work done by the winch, we first need to find the force it exerts, which is the tension ($T$) in the cable. 

Two forces act on the crate as it moves upward:
1. The tension ($T$) acting upwards.
2. The weight ($mg$) acting downwards.

Since the crate is accelerating upwards, the net force ($F_{net}$) is the difference between these two forces.

steps.title: Calculate the tension force
steps.content:
We use Newton's Second Law to set up our equation. This concept is covered in **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**.

$$F = ma$$

Substituting our forces into the equation for the upward direction:
$$T - mg = ma$$
$$T = m(g + a)$$

Using the values provided for a mass of $150.0\ \text{kg}$, an acceleration of $2.00\ \text{m s}^{-2}$, and the standard gravity $g = 9.81\ \text{m s}^{-2}$:

$$T = 150.0 \times (9.81 + 2.00)$$
$$T = 1771.5\ \text{N}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the mass times the sum of g and acceleration.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the tension force exerted by the winch.

steps.title: Apply the work formula
steps.content:
Now that we have the force exerted by the winch, we can calculate the work done over the distance of $10.0\ \text{m}$ using the formula from **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

$$W = Fs \cos\theta$$

Since the force (tension) and the displacement are both upwards, the angle $\theta$ is $0^\circ$ (and $\cos 0^\circ = 1$).

$$\begin{aligned} W &= 1771.5 \times 10.0 \times 1 \\ W &= 17715\ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation earns you the first mark (**M1**) for showing the correct method of multiplying the total force by the distance.

calculator.description: Multiply the tension force by the distance to find work.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Finally, we express the answer to an appropriate number of significant figures (3 sig figs based on the given acceleration and distance).

$$W = 1.77 \times 10^4\ \text{J}$$

Rounding $17715$ to three significant figures gives $17700$, which is correctly written in scientific notation as $1.77 \times 10^4\ \text{J}$. This earns the final mark (**A1**).

steps.title: Identify the Energy Changes
steps.content:
To find the total work done by the winch using the **Work-Energy Theorem**, we need to account for all the energy transferred to the crate. Since the crate is being lifted and accelerated upwards, the work done goes into two places:
1. Increasing the **gravitational potential energy** ($Δ E_p$).
2. Increasing the **kinetic energy** ($Δ E_k$).

Mathematically, this is represented as:
$$W_{\text{total}} = \Delta E_p + \Delta E_k$$

This approach is a direct application of concepts found in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

steps.title: Calculate Potential Energy Change
steps.content:
The change in gravitational potential energy is calculated using the formula from the data booklet:
$$\Delta E_p = mg\Delta h$$

Given:
- $m = 150.0\ \text{kg}$
- $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$ (from the Physical Constants section)
- $\Delta h = 10.0\ \text{m}$

$$\Delta E_p = 150.0 \times 9.8 \times 10.0 = 14700\ \text{J}$$

steps.title: Calculate Kinetic Energy Change
steps.content:
The change in kinetic energy is equal to the work done by the *net* force ($F_{\text{net}} = ma$). Over a distance $s$, this is:
$$\Delta E_k = F_{\text{net}} \times s = (ma) \times s$$

Given:
- $m = 150.0\ \text{kg}$
- $a = 2.00\ \text{m s}^{-2}$
- $s = 10.0\ \text{m}$

$$\Delta E_k = 150.0 \times 2.00 \times 10.0 = 3000\ \text{J}$$

steps.title: Find Total Work Done
steps.content:
Finally, we sum the two energy changes to find the total work done by the winch:
$$\begin{aligned} W_{\text{total}} &= \Delta E_p + \Delta E_k \\ W_{\text{total}} &= 14700\ \text{J} + 3000\ \text{J} \\ W_{\text{total}} &= 17700\ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

In standard scientific notation to 3 significant figures, this is:
$$W_{\text{total}} = 1.77 \times 10^4\ \text{J}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 14700 + 3000 on the main calculation screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of potential and kinetic energy changes.

parts.content: Calculate the final speed of the crate at the end of the $10.0\ \text{m}$ ascent, assuming it started from rest.
parts.markscheme:
$v^2 = u^2 + 2as \implies v = \sqrt{2 \times 2.00 \times 10.0}$ 

$= 6.32\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify known variables
steps.content:
First, let's identify the information given in the problem. This is a kinematics problem where the crate moves with constant acceleration. We know:

*   Initial velocity ($u$): $0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ (since it started from rest)
*   Acceleration ($a$): $2.00\ \text{m s}^{-2}$
*   Displacement ($s$): $10.0\ \text{m}$

steps.title: Select the kinematic equation
steps.content:
To find the final speed ($v$), we look for a kinematic (SUVAT) equation from the data booklet that links $u$, $v$, $a$, and $s$ without needing time ($t$). 

The appropriate formula from the **Mechanics** section is:

$$v^{2}=u^{2}+2as$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, we substitute our known values into the equation and solve for $v$:

$$\begin{aligned} v^2 &= 0^2 + 2(2.00)(10.0) \\ v^2 &= 40.0 \\ v &= \sqrt{40.0} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation gives us the final velocity directly.

gdc.instructions: Enter the square root function, input 40, and press execute/enter.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [x²] (the square root key), type 40, and press [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate the square root of 40 to find the final speed.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to three significant figures (as given in the question), we get:

$$v = 6.32\ \text{m s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Identify known variables
steps.content:
To solve this problem using kinematics (found in **Topic A.1**), we first need to list the information given in the question:

*   Initial velocity ($u$): $0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ (since it "started from rest")
*   Acceleration ($a$): $2.00\ \text{m s}^{-2}$
*   Displacement ($s$): $10.0\ \text{m}$

steps.title: Calculate time of ascent
steps.content:
First, we find the time ($t$) it takes to travel $10.0\ \text{m}$ using the displacement formula from the data booklet:

$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

Substituting our known values:

$$\begin{aligned} 10.0 &= (0)t + \frac{1}{2}(2.00)t^2 \\ 10.0 &= 1.00t^2 \\ t &= \sqrt{10.0} \end{aligned}$$

Using a calculator, we find $t \approx 3.162\ \text{s}$.

gdc.instructions: Enter sqrt(10) in the main calculation screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the square root of 10 to find the time.

steps.title: Find final speed
steps.content:
Now that we have the time, we can use the velocity-time equation to find the final speed ($v$):

$$v = u + at$$

Substituting our values ($u=0$, $a=2.00$, and $t = \sqrt{10.0}$):

$$\begin{aligned} v &= 0 + (2.00)(\sqrt{10.0}) \\ v &= 6.3245... \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures (as per the given data), we get $v = 6.32\ \text{m s}^{-1}$.

ti84.instructions: Multiply 2 by the previous answer (ANS) or type 2 * √(10) and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type 2 * sqrt(10) in the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the final velocity by multiplying acceleration by time.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The final speed of the crate is **$6.32\ \text{m s}^{-1}$**. 

Even though the markscheme uses the shortcut formula ($v^2 = u^2 + 2as$), calculating the time first is a valid multi-step approach that leads to the same accurate result.

steps.title: Identify the Work-Energy Theorem
steps.content:
To find the final speed, we use the Work-Energy Theorem, which states that the net work ($W_{net}$) done on an object is equal to its change in kinetic energy ($\Delta E_k$).

From the question, we know:
- Mass ($m$) = $150.0\text{ kg}$
- Acceleration ($a$) = $2.00\text{ m s}^{-2}$
- Distance ($s$) = $10.0\text{ m}$
- Initial speed ($u$) = $0$ (since it **started from rest**)

steps.title: Calculate Net Work Done
steps.content:
First, we find the net work done on the crate. Since the crate is moving vertically with a constant acceleration, the net force is $F_{net} = ma$. The net work is the product of this net force and the displacement ($s$):

$$\begin{aligned} W_{net} &= F_{net} \times s \\ W_{net} &= (m \times a) \times s \end{aligned}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\begin{aligned} W_{net} &= (150.0 \times 2.00) \times 10.0 \\ W_{net} &= 3000\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the net work done on the crate.

steps.title: Relate Work to Kinetic Energy
steps.content:
The net work done equals the change in kinetic energy ($W_{net} = \Delta E_k$). Since the crate starts from rest, the initial kinetic energy is zero, so the net work equals the final kinetic energy:

$$W_{net} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

Substituting our values:

$$3000 = \frac{1}{2}(150.0)v^2$$

steps.title: Solve for Final Speed
steps.content:
Now we rearrange the equation to solve for the final speed ($v$):

$$\begin{aligned} v^2 &= \frac{3000}{0.5 \times 150.0} \\ v^2 &= \frac{3000}{75} \\ v^2 &= 40 \\ v &= \sqrt{40} \end{aligned}$$

$$v \approx 6.32\text{ m s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Press the square root key, then type 40 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [x^2] (for sqrt), then type 40 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt(40) into a new line.

calculator.description: Calculate the square root of 40 to find the final speed.

parts.content: Discuss how the work done by the winch is distributed in terms of energy transformations.
parts.markscheme: The work done by the winch increases both the gravitational potential energy and the kinetic energy of the crate. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the energy input
steps.content: In this scenario, the winch is the external agent performing work on the crate. According to the principle of conservation of energy (covered in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**), the work done by the winch must result in a change in the total mechanical energy of the crate system.

steps.title: Analyze the vertical displacement
steps.content:
The crate is being **raised vertically** over a distance of $10.0\text{ m}$. Since the height of the crate is increasing, its gravitational potential energy is also increasing.

From the data booklet, the change in potential energy is given by:
$$\Delta E_{p}=mg\Delta h$$

steps.title: Analyze the motion
steps.content:
The crate is moving with a **constant acceleration of 2.00 m s⁻²**. Because the crate is accelerating from an initial state (implied starting from rest or simply increasing its velocity), its speed is increasing. An increase in speed results in a gain of kinetic energy.

From the data booklet, kinetic energy is given by:
$$E_{k}=\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$$

steps.title: Summarize the energy distribution
steps.content: Combining these observations, the total work done by the winch is distributed into two parts: one part increases the crate's **gravitational potential energy** (due to height) and the other part increases its **kinetic energy** (due to acceleration).

steps.title: Calculate potential energy change
steps.content:
To understand where the energy goes, let's first calculate the gain in gravitational potential energy ($\Delta E_p$). Since the crate is being raised vertically, we use the formula from the data booklet:

$$\Delta E_p = mg\Delta h$$

Given:
- $m = 150.0\text{ kg}$
- $g = 9.8\text{ m s}^{-2}$ 
- $\Delta h = 10.0\text{ m}$

$$\Delta E_p = 150.0 \times 9.8 \times 10.0 = 14,700\text{ J}$$

steps.title: Find final velocity squared
steps.content:
Next, we look at the kinetic energy change. Because the crate is accelerating, its velocity increases. Assuming it starts from rest ($u = 0$), we use the kinematic equation to find the final velocity squared ($v^2$):

$$v^2 = u^2 + 2as$$

Substituting the given values:
- $u = 0\text{ m s}^{-1}$
- $a = 2.00\text{ m s}^{-2}$
- $s = 10.0\text{ m}$

$$v^2 = 0^2 + 2(2.00)(10.0) = 40.0\text{ m}^2\text{s}^{-2}$$

calculator.description: Calculate final velocity squared ($v^2$)

steps.title: Calculate kinetic energy change
steps.content:
Now we can calculate the gain in kinetic energy ($E_k$) using the formula:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

$$\Delta E_k = \frac{1}{2}(150.0)(40.0) = 3,000\text{ J}$$

calculator.description: Calculate kinetic energy gain

steps.title: Analyze energy distribution
steps.content:
By comparing our results, we can see that the work done by the winch ($W = \Delta E_p + \Delta E_k = 17,700\text{ J}$) is distributed between two types of energy:

1.  **Gravitational Potential Energy ($14,700\text{ J}$):** Required to move the crate higher against gravity.
2.  **Kinetic Energy ($3,000\text{ J}$):** Required to accelerate the crate to a higher speed.

Therefore, the work done by the winch increases both the **gravitational potential energy** and the **kinetic energy** of the crate.

steps.title: Identify the energy transfer
steps.content: In this scenario, the winch is doing work $W$ on the crate. According to **Topic A.3 Work, energy and power**, work is a way of transferring energy. We need to determine where this energy goes as the crate moves upward and accelerates.

steps.title: Work against gravity
steps.content:
As the crate is raised vertically over a distance of $10.0\text{ m}$, the winch must exert a force to overcome gravity. This component of the work done by the winch is stored as **gravitational potential energy** ($E_p$). 

From the data booklet, the change in potential energy is:
$$\Delta E_p = mg\Delta h$$

steps.title: Work resulting in acceleration
steps.content:
The crate is also moving with a "constant acceleration of $2.00\text{ m s}^{-2}$". This means there is a net force acting on the crate, and therefore **net work** is being done. 

According to the **Work-Energy Theorem**, the net work done on an object results in a change in its **kinetic energy** ($E_k$):
$$W_{net} = \Delta E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 - \frac{1}{2}mu^2$$

steps.title: Conclusion on energy distribution
steps.content: Since the winch provides the total energy for both the lifting and the acceleration, we can conclude that the work done by the winch is distributed into two parts: increasing the crate's **gravitational potential energy** and increasing its **kinetic energy**.

options.markscheme: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Work done} &= \Delta E_{\text{k}} = \frac{1}{2}m(v^2 - u^2) \\ &= \frac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times (30^2 - 10^2) \\ &= 400\,000 \text{ J} \\ \\ \text{Time taken:} \quad s &= \left(\frac{u+v}{2}\right)t \\ 200 &= \left(\frac{10+30}{2}\right)t \\ t &= 10 \text{ s} \\ \\ \text{Average Power} &= \frac{W}{t} = \frac{400\,000}{10} \\ &= 40\,000 \text{ W} \end{aligned}$$

questions.specification: A skier of mass $70 \, \mathrm{kg}$ is skiing down a slope inclined at $25^{\circ}$ to the horizontal. The slope is $50 \, \mathrm{m}$ long. Assume no air resistance, and the coefficient of kinetic friction between the skis and the slope is $\mu_k=0.15$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a) and (b) involve resolving weight along the slope and calculating work done against friction, directly using work/energy transfer concepts (work = force × distance).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.1 Conservation of energy
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.1 Conservation of energy
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) explicitly asks for an energy-considerations approach: gravitational potential energy converted to kinetic energy minus energy dissipated by friction (non-conservative work).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Resolving forces on an incline (weight components, normal force) and using friction (F=μN) relies on Newtonian force analysis, even though the final speed is found via energy.

parts.content: Calculate the component of the skier's weight acting parallel to the slope.
parts.markscheme:
- State the expression for the parallel component of weight: $F_{\text{parallel}} = mg \sin(\theta)$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute the values: $F_{\text{parallel}} = 70 \times 9.81 \times \sin(25^{\circ})$

- Calculate the final value: $F_{\text{parallel}} \approx 290 \, \mathrm{N}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the given values
steps.content: To calculate the parallel component of the weight, we first need to identify the mass of the skier and the angle of the slope provided in the question. We also need the gravitational field strength ($g$) from the physical constants section of your data booklet.

steps.title: Recall the resolution formula
steps.content:
When an object is on an inclined plane, its weight acts vertically downwards. We can resolve this weight into two components using trigonometry. The component acting parallel to the slope (the one trying to pull the skier down) is given by:

$$F_{\text{parallel}} = mg \sin(\theta)$$ 

This is a standard application of resolving forces in **Topic A.2 Forces and momentum**.

steps.title: Substitute and calculate
steps.content:
Now, substitute the known values into our formula:

$$\begin{aligned} F_{\text{parallel}} &= 70 \times 9.8 \times \sin(25^{\circ}) \\ F_{\text{parallel}} &\approx 289.92 \, \mathrm{N} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Set angle mode to Degrees.
2. Enter the calculation: 70 * 9.8 * sin(25).

ti84.instructions:
1. Ensure the calculator is in Degree mode (Press [MODE], highlight 'DEGREE', press [ENTER]).
2. Multiply the mass, gravity, and the sine of the angle: 70 * 9.8 * sin(25).

desmos.instructions:
1. Click the wrench icon (Settings) and select 'Degrees'.
2. Type the expression: 70 * 9.8 * sin(25).

calculator.description: Calculate the parallel component of weight.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to two significant figures (as given in the question for the angle and coefficient of friction), we get:

$$F_{\text{parallel}} = 290 \, \mathrm{N}$$

In an exam, you earn one mark for the correct expression or substitution and one mark for the final value with units.

steps.title: Calculate the total weight
steps.content:
First, we determine the skier's weight ($W$), which is the force due to gravity acting vertically downwards. We use the formula for weight:

$$W = mg$$

From the data booklet, the acceleration due to gravity is $g = 9.8 \, \mathrm{m \, s^{-2}}$. Substituting the given mass:

$$W = 70 \times 9.8 = 686 \, \mathrm{N}$$

steps.title: Construct the force triangle
steps.content:
To find the component of weight parallel to the slope, we construct a right-angled force triangle. In this construction:
- The total weight ($W = 686 \, \mathrm{N}$) is the **hypotenuse**, acting vertically down.
- One leg of the triangle is the component **perpendicular** to the slope ($F_{\perp}$).
- The other leg is the component **parallel** to the slope ($F_{\parallel}$).

Geometry shows that the angle between the weight vector and the perpendicular component is equal to the incline of the slope, which is $25^{\circ}$.

steps.title: Apply the sine ratio
steps.content:
In our right-angled force triangle, the parallel component ($F_{\parallel}$) is the side **opposite** to the $25^{\circ}$ angle. We use the definition of sine:

$$\sin(\theta) = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\sin(25^{\circ}) = \frac{F_{\parallel}}{mg}$$

Rearranging to solve for the parallel component:

$$F_{\parallel} = mg \sin(25^{\circ})$$

steps.title: Calculate the parallel component
steps.content:
Finally, we substitute the numerical values into the expression to find the magnitude of the force parallel to the slope:

$$F_{\parallel} = 686 \times \sin(25^{\circ})$$

$$F_{\parallel} \approx 290 \, \mathrm{N}$$

According to the markscheme, stating the expression $mg \sin(\theta)$ earns the first mark, and providing the final calculated value earns the second mark.

gdc.instructions: Set the angle mode to Degrees. Enter the calculation: 70 * 9.8 * sin(25).

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in Degree mode. Press [MODE], select 'DEGREE', then [ENTER]. Then, type 70 * 9.8 * sin(25) and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Click the wrench icon to ensure the 'Degrees' setting is selected. Type: 70 * 9.8 * sin(25).

calculator.description: Calculate the parallel component of weight using the sine function.

parts.content: Determine the work done against friction as the skier moves down the slope.
parts.markscheme:
The frictional force is given by $F_{\text{friction}} = \mu_k N$.

- Calculate the normal force $N$: $N = mg \cos(\theta) = 70 \times 9.81 \times \cos(25^{\circ}) \approx 622.4 \, \mathrm{N}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the frictional force: $F_{\text{friction}} = 0.15 \times 622.4 \approx 93.4 \, \mathrm{N}$

- Calculate the work done against friction: $W_{\text{friction}} = F_{\text{friction}} \times d = 93.4 \times 50 \approx 4670 \, \mathrm{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the forces
steps.content:
To find the work done against friction, we first need to determine the magnitude of the friction force acting on the skier. Because the skier is on an inclined slope, the normal force ($N$)—which determines the friction—is not simply equal to the weight, but rather the component of the weight perpendicular to the slope.

From the question, we have:
- Mass ($m$) = $70 \, \mathrm{kg}$
- Slope angle ($\theta$) = $25^{\circ}$
- Distance ($d$) = $50 \, \mathrm{m}$
- Coefficient of kinetic friction ($\mu_k$) = $0.15$

steps.title: Calculate the normal force
steps.content:
The normal force $N$ is the component of the weight ($mg$) acting perpendicular to the inclined plane. Using the geometry of the slope:

$$N = mg \cos(\theta)$$

Using the gravitational acceleration $g = 9.8 \, \mathrm{m \, s^{-2}}$ from the data booklet:

$$N = 70 \times 9.8 \times \cos(25^{\circ})$$

$$N \approx 621.7 \, \mathrm{N}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 70 * 9.8 * cos(25) into the main calculation screen.

desmos.instructions: Type '70 * 9.8 * cos(25)' and ensure the angle mode is set to Degrees.

calculator.description: Calculate the normal force $N = 70 \times 9.8 \times \cos(25^\circ)$. Ensure your calculator is in Degree mode.

steps.title: Calculate the friction force
steps.content:
Now we calculate the kinetic friction force ($F_f$) using the coefficient of friction $\mu_k$. The formula is:

$$F_f = \mu_k N$$

Substituting our values:

$$F_f = 0.15 \times 621.7$$

$$F_f \approx 93.26 \, \mathrm{N}$$

calculator.description: Calculate friction force by multiplying $\mu_k$ and $N$.

steps.title: Determine the work done
steps.content:
The work done against friction ($W_f$) is the product of the friction force and the distance traveled along the slope. We use the formula for work from the data booklet:

$$W = Fs \cos(\theta)$$

Since we are calculating the work done *against* friction, and friction acts parallel to the path of motion (displacement), we simply multiply the force magnitude by the distance:

$$W_f = F_f \times d$$

$$W_f = 93.26 \times 50$$

$$W_f \approx 4663 \, \mathrm{J}$$

Rounding to two significant figures (following IB convention for the given $\mu_k = 0.15$), we get **$4700 \, \mathrm{J}$**. (Note: If using $g = 9.81$, the result is $\approx 4670 \, \mathrm{J}$).

calculator.description: Calculate total work by multiplying force and distance.

parts.content: Using energy considerations, calculate the skier's speed at the bottom of the slope if they start from rest.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the vertical height $h$ or initial potential energy: $h = 50 \sin(25^{\circ}) \approx 21.13 \, \mathrm{m}$ OR $E_{\text{initial}} = mgd \sin(\theta)$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the initial potential energy: $E_{\text{initial}} = 70 \times 9.81 \times 21.13 \approx 14510 \, \mathrm{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Apply the work-energy principle: $E_{\text{kinetic}} = E_{\text{initial}} - W_{\text{friction}} = 14510 - 4670 = 9840 \, \mathrm{J}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the speed: $v = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{\text{kinetic}}}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9840}{70}} \approx 16.8 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

methods.label: Potential Energy and Work against Friction

steps.title: Identify the energy approach
steps.content:
To find the skier's speed, we will use the principle of conservation of energy. Since there is friction, the total mechanical energy is not conserved; instead, the initial potential energy is converted into kinetic energy and work done against friction.

From the data booklet, we'll need the formulas for potential energy, work, and kinetic energy:

$$\Delta E_{p}=mg\Delta h$$
$$W=Fs\cos\theta$$
$$E_{k}=\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$$

steps.title: Calculate vertical height
steps.content:
The skier travels $50 \, \mathrm{m}$ down a $25^{\circ}$ slope. We need the vertical height change ($h$) to calculate the potential energy.

Using trigonometry:
$$h = 50 \cdot \sin(25^{\circ})$$
$$h \approx 21.13 \, \mathrm{m}$$

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode. Press [5][0][SIN][2][5][ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Set the calculator to Degrees and type 50 * sin(25).

calculator.description: Calculate the vertical height using the sine function.

steps.title: Determine initial potential energy
steps.content:
Now we calculate the gravitational potential energy ($E_p$) at the top of the slope. We use $g = 9.8 \, \mathrm{m \, s^{-2}}$ as provided in the physical constants.

$$\begin{aligned} E_p &= mgh \\ E_p &= 70 \cdot 9.8 \cdot 21.13 \\ E_p &\approx 14495 \, \mathrm{J} \end{aligned}$$

*(Note: Using $g=9.81$ would yield $\approx 14510 \, \mathrm{J}$ as seen in the markscheme, but we follow the data booklet constant $9.8$)*.

calculator.description: Calculate the initial potential energy.

steps.title: Calculate work against friction
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the energy lost to friction. 

1. Find the normal force ($R$): $R = mg \cos(25^{\circ})$
2. Find the friction force ($F_f$): $F_f = \mu_k R = 0.15 \cdot 70 \cdot 9.8 \cdot \cos(25^{\circ})$
3. Calculate work ($W_f$): $W_f = F_f \cdot d$

$$\begin{aligned} W_f &= 0.15 \cdot 70 \cdot 9.8 \cdot \cos(25^{\circ}) \cdot 50 \\ W_f &\approx 4663 \, \mathrm{J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the work done by friction.

steps.title: Find final speed
steps.content:
The final kinetic energy ($E_k$) is the initial potential energy minus the work lost to friction:

$$E_k = E_p - W_f = 14495 - 4663 = 9832 \, \mathrm{J}$$

Now, solve for $v$ using $E_{k}=\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= \sqrt{\frac{2E_k}{m}} \\ v &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 9832}{70}} \\ v &\approx 16.8 \, \mathrm{m \, s^{-1}} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Solve for the final speed v.

steps.title: Identify the Net Work-Energy Theorem
steps.content:
To find the skier's speed, we can use the **Net Work-Energy Theorem**. This theorem states that the net work done on an object is equal to its change in kinetic energy:

$$W_{\text{net}} = \Delta E_k = E_{k, \text{final}} - E_{k, \text{initial}}$$

Since the skier **starts from rest**, the initial kinetic energy is $0$. Therefore, the work done by the net force acting along the slope will equal the final kinetic energy at the bottom. This concept is covered in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

steps.title: Calculate gravity's parallel component
steps.content:
First, we find the component of the skier's weight acting parallel to the slope (downwards). Using trigonometry:

$$F_{\text{parallel}} = mg \sin(\theta)$$

Using the values from the question and the data booklet ($g = 9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$):

$$\begin{aligned} F_{\text{parallel}} &= 70 \times 9.8 \times \sin(25^{\circ}) \\ F_{\text{parallel}} &\approx 289.9 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Set angle mode to degrees and compute the product.

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is in DEGREES. Enter the expression.

desmos.instructions: Set calculator to degrees and type the expression.

calculator.description: Calculate the force component parallel to the slope.

steps.title: Calculate the kinetic friction force
steps.content:
Friction acts against the motion. The force of kinetic friction depends on the normal force ($R$), which is the component of weight perpendicular to the slope ($mg \cos(\theta)$):

$$\begin{aligned} F_f &= \mu_k R \\ F_f &= \mu_k mg \cos(\theta) \end{aligned}$$

Substituting the known values:

$$\begin{aligned} F_f &= 0.15 \times 70 \times 9.8 \times \cos(25^{\circ}) \\ F_f &\approx 93.3 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Compute the friction force magnitude in degree mode.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the coefficient by mass, gravity, and the cosine of the angle.

desmos.instructions: Enter the formula for kinetic friction.

calculator.description: Calculate the magnitude of the friction force.

steps.title: Determine Net Force and Work
steps.content:
The net force ($F_{\text{net}}$) is the difference between the driving force (gravity) and the resistive force (friction). Then, we multiply by the displacement ($s = 50 \text{ m}$) to find the net work:

$$\begin{aligned} F_{\text{net}} &= F_{\text{parallel}} - F_f \\ F_{\text{net}} &\approx 289.9 - 93.3 = 196.6 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$

Now, apply the work formula from the data booklet:

$$W = Fs \cos \theta$$

Since the net force is parallel to the displacement, $\cos(0) = 1$:

$$\begin{aligned} W_{\text{net}} &= F_{\text{net}} \times s \\ W_{\text{net}} &= 196.6 \times 50 \approx 9830 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the net force by the distance.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the difference between the previous results and multiply by 50.

calculator.description: Calculate total net work done.

steps.title: Solve for final speed
steps.content:
Finally, we set the net work equal to the kinetic energy formula from the data booklet to solve for $v$:

$$\begin{aligned} W_{\text{net}} &= \frac{1}{2}mv^2 \\ 9830 &= \frac{1}{2} \times 70 \times v^2 \\ v^2 &= \frac{2 \times 9830}{70} \\ v &= \sqrt{280.86} \approx 16.8 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

**Marking Breakdown:**
- **M1**: Correct method for parallel component of weight or height.
- **A1**: Correct calculation of work or energy values.
- **M1**: Applying the Work-Energy principle ($E_k = W_{\text{net}}$).
- **A1**: Final speed correctly rounded to 3 significant figures.

ti84.instructions: Take the square root of (2 * Net Work / mass).

calculator.description: Solve for the final velocity.

parts.content: Calculate the gravitational potential energy lost by the skier.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct substitution: $E_p = mgh = 60.0 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 30.0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Correct final answer with unit: $1.77 \times 10^4 \, \text{J}$ (or $17.7 \, \text{kJ}$) <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To find the gravitational potential energy ($E_p$) lost, we use the formula for the change in potential energy from the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$\Delta E_{p}=mg\Delta h$$

This relates the energy change to the mass of the object ($m$), the acceleration due to gravity ($g$), and the vertical change in height ($\Delta h$). This concept is part of **Topic A.3 Work, energy and power**.

steps.title: Substitute the known values
steps.content:
From the question, we can extract the following information:
- Mass ($m$) = $60.0\ \text{kg}$
- Vertical height ($\Delta h$) = $30.0\ \text{m}$
- Acceleration due to gravity ($g$) = $9.81\ \text{m s}^{-2}$ (standard IB value used in markschemes)

Now, substitute these into our equation:

$$\Delta E_p = 60.0 \times 9.81 \times 30.0$$

steps.title: Calculate the final answer
steps.content:
Using a calculator to find the product:

$$\Delta E_p = 17658\ \text{J}$$

Rounding to **three significant figures** (matching the precision of the given values $60.0$ and $30.0$), we get:

$$\Delta E_p = 1.77 \times 10^4\ \text{J} \text{ or } 17.7\ \text{kJ}$$

parts.markscheme:
- Correct energy equation and rearrangement: $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 \implies v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 17658}{60.0}}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Correct final answer with unit: $24.3 \, \text{m\,s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the energy conservation
steps.content: Since we are told to assume there is **no friction**, we can apply the Law of Conservation of Energy. All the gravitational potential energy ($E_p$) at the top of the slope will be converted into kinetic energy ($E_k$) at the bottom. This concept is covered in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

steps.title: Calculate initial potential energy
steps.content:
First, we calculate the gravitational potential energy the skier has at the top using the formula from the data booklet:

$$\Delta E_{p}=mg\Delta h$$

Given:
- $m = 60.0\text{ kg}$
- $g \approx 9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$ (standard acceleration due to gravity)
- $\Delta h = 30.0\text{ m}$

$$\Delta E_p = 60.0 \times 9.81 \times 30.0 = 17658\text{ J}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the initial Gravitational Potential Energy.

steps.title: Equate energies
steps.content:
The skier starts **from rest**, meaning their initial kinetic energy is zero. At the bottom, their potential energy is zero. Therefore:

$$E_{k, \text{bottom}} = E_{p, \text{top}}$$

Using the kinetic energy formula from the data booklet:

$$\frac{1}{2}mv^2 = 17658$$

steps.title: Solve for speed
steps.content:
Now we rearrange the equation to solve for the final speed $v$:

$$\begin{aligned} v^2 &= \frac{2 \times 17658}{60.0} \\ v &= \sqrt{\frac{35316}{60.0}} \\ v &= \sqrt{588.6} \end{aligned}$$

Using a calculator, we find:
$$v \approx 24.3\text{ m s}^{-1}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter square root (2 * 17658 / 60) and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt(2 * 17658 / 60) into a calculation cell.

calculator.description: Solve for the final velocity v.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The speed of the skier at the bottom of the slope is **$24.3\text{ m s}^{-1}$**. 

In an IB exam, you earn **1 mark** for correctly equating the energy terms and rearranging the formula, and **1 mark** for the correct numerical answer with the appropriate unit.

steps.title: Identify kinematic variables
steps.content:
To find the final speed using the **Kinematic Equations** approach, we first identify the known values from the problem. This problem falls under **Topic A.1: Kinematics**.

*   Initial velocity ($u$): $0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ (since the skier starts "from rest")
*   Vertical height ($h$): $30.0\text{ m}$
*   Acceleration due to gravity ($g$): $9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$ (commonly used for standard gravity in IB calculations to match markschemes)

steps.title: Relate slope to height
steps.content:
On a frictionless slope with angle $\theta$, the acceleration along the slope ($a$) and the distance traveled ($s$) are related to vertical height $h$:

1.  The acceleration along the slope is $a = g \sin\theta$.
2.  The distance along the slope is $s = \frac{h}{\sin\theta}$.

When we multiply $a \times s$, the $\sin\theta$ terms cancel out:

$$as = (g \sin\theta) \cdot \left(\frac{h}{\sin\theta}\right) = gh$$

This shows that the final speed depends only on the vertical height, regardless of the slope angle.

steps.title: Apply SUVAT equation
steps.content:
Now we use the kinematic equation for motion with constant acceleration from the **Mechanics** section of the data booklet:

$$\text{v}^{2}=\text{u}^{2}+2\text{as}$$

Substituting our knowns ($u=0$ and $as = gh$):

$$v^2 = 0 + 2gh$$
$$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final speed
steps.content:
Substitute the values into the derived formula:

$$v = \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 30.0}$$
$$v = \sqrt{588.6}$$

Calculating this gives approximately $24.26\text{ m s}^{-1}$. To follow IB significant figure rules (using the 3 sig figs from $30.0\text{ m}$), we round this to $24.3\text{ m s}^{-1}$.

gdc.instructions: Enter the square root function, then type 2 * 9.81 * 30.0 and execute.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [x²] to get the square root symbol. Type 2 * 9.81 * 30.0 and press [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate the final velocity using the square root of 2gh.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The speed of the skier at the bottom of the slope is:

$$v = 24.3\text{ m s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Identify the Work-Energy Principle
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Work-Energy Theorem**, we need to recognize that the net work done on the skier is equal to their change in kinetic energy ($\Delta E_k$). Since there is no friction, the only force doing work is gravity.

This concept is found in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

steps.title: Calculate work done by gravity
steps.content:
The work done by gravity is the product of the gravitational force ($F = mg$) and the vertical displacement ($s = h$). Using the formula for work:

$$W = Fs \cos \theta$$

Since the force of gravity and the vertical displacement are in the same direction for our energy calculation, we use:

$$W = mgh$$

Substituting the values (using $g = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ to match the markscheme's specific value of $17658$ J):

$$W = 60.0 \times 9.81 \times 30.0 = 17658 \text{ J}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication of mass, gravity, and height.

desmos.instructions: Enter 60 * 9.81 * 30 to find the work in Joules.

calculator.description: Calculate the work done by gravity.

steps.title: Relate work to kinetic energy
steps.content:
According to the Work-Energy Theorem, $W = \Delta E_k$. Since the skier starts **from rest**, the initial kinetic energy is zero. Therefore, the work done equals the final kinetic energy:

$$W = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

Using the kinetic energy formula from the data booklet:

$$17658 = \frac{1}{2} \times 60.0 \times v^2$$

steps.title: Solve for final speed
steps.content:
Now, we rearrange the equation to solve for $v$:

$$\begin{aligned} v^2 &= \frac{2 \times 17658}{60.0} \\ v &= \sqrt{\frac{35316}{60.0}} \\ v &= 24.3 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This matches the markscheme requirements for the correct equation arrangement and the final answer with units.

gdc.instructions: Use the square root function on the result of 35316 divided by 60.

desmos.instructions: Enter sqrt(2 * 17658 / 60) to find the speed.

Correct final answer with unit: 24.3 m s^{-1}

parts.content: Explain how energy conservation is applied in this situation.

steps.title: Identify the conservation principle
steps.content:
In physics, we look at the forces acting on a system to see if energy is lost. This question involves **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**. Since the question states there is **no friction**, there are no non-conservative forces doing work to remove energy from the system (like heat). 

Therefore, we can state that the **total mechanical energy** of the skier (the sum of kinetic and potential energy) remains constant throughout the slide.

steps.title: Describe the energy transformation
steps.content:
As the skier moves down the slope, their vertical height $h$ decreases, which means their **gravitational potential energy** ($E_p$) decreases. 

According to the data booklet:
$$\Delta E_p = mg\Delta h$$

Simultaneously, the skier's speed increases, meaning their **kinetic energy** ($E_k$) increases. 

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

steps.title: Explain the complete conversion
steps.content:
Because we are assuming a frictionless environment, no energy is transformed into thermal energy (heat). This means that **all** of the initial gravitational potential energy at the top is **completely converted** into kinetic energy at the bottom of the $30.0\text{ m}$ slope.

To summarize for the exam marks:
1. Total mechanical energy is conserved during the motion.
2. Gravitational potential energy is completely converted into kinetic energy because there is no friction.

steps.title: State mechanical energy conservation
steps.content: In IB Physics, specifically in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**, we learn that the total mechanical energy of a system is the sum of its kinetic and potential energies. Because the question specifies there is no friction, no work is done by non-conservative forces. Therefore, the total mechanical energy remains constant throughout the skier's motion.

steps.title: Identify initial and final energy
steps.content:
At the start, the skier is at a vertical height of $30.0\ \text{m}$ and is at rest. This means they have maximum gravitational potential energy and zero kinetic energy. The formulas for these from your data booklet are:

$$\Delta E_{p}=mg\Delta h$$
$$E_{k}=\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$$

Since the skier starts from rest, their initial velocity $u = 0$, so their initial $E_k = 0$.

options.markscheme: This value incorrectly omits the acceleration due to gravity $g$, calculating only $m \times h$ per unit time.

The mass of water moved per second is calculated as:
$\frac{m}{t} = \rho \times \frac{V}{t} = 1000 \text{ kg m}^{-3} \times 1.2 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3 \text{ s}^{-1} = 12 \text{ kg s}^{-1}$.
The useful power output is the rate of increase in gravitational potential energy:
$P = \frac{\Delta E_p}{\Delta t} = \frac{mgh}{t} = \left(\frac{m}{t}\right) g h$.
$P = 12 \times 9.81 \times 5.0 = 588.6 \text{ W} \approx 5.9 \times 10^2 \text{ W}$.
This value is the product of density and volume flow rate, representing only the mass flow rate without accounting for energy conversion factors.
This value results from a decimal place error in the calculation of mass or height.
