parts.content: Calculate the elastic potential energy stored in the spring.
parts.markscheme:
- State formula for elastic potential energy: $E_{\text{P}}=\frac{1}{2} k(\Delta x)^2$
- Substitute values: $E_{\text{P}}=\frac{1}{2}(200)(0.10)^2$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate result: $E_{\text{P}}=1.0 \text{ J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the given values
steps.content:
First, let's identify the information provided in the question. We are given the spring constant and the distance the spring has been compressed. 

*   Spring constant ($k$): $200 \text{ N m}^{-1}$
*   Compression distance ($\Delta x$): $0.10 \text{ m}$

steps.title: State the energy formula
steps.content:
To find the energy stored, we use the formula for **elastic potential energy** ($E_{p}$) from **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**. This formula calculates the work done to compress or stretch a spring by a certain distance.

$$
E_{p} = \frac{1}{2}k\Delta x^{2}
$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, we simply substitute our values into the formula. Remember to square the compression distance before multiplying:

$$\begin{aligned} E_{p} &= \frac{1}{2}(200)(0.10)^{2} \\ E_{p} &= 100 \times 0.01 \\ E_{p} &= 1.0 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation earns you the first mark (**M1**) for the substitution and the second mark (**A1**) for the correct final answer with units.

gdc.instructions: Enter the calculation into the run-matrix or calculate menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression directly into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the elastic potential energy using the formula.

steps.title: Identify given variables
steps.content:
First, let's identify the values provided in the question. We are given the spring constant ($k$) and the compression distance ($\Delta x$).

$$\begin{aligned} k &= 200 \text{ N m}^{-1} \\ \Delta x &= 0.10 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate average force
steps.content:
Since the force in a spring increases linearly as it is compressed (starting from $0 \text{ N}$), we use the average force ($F_{avg}$) to calculate the work done. The formula for the average force during compression is:

$$F_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} k \Delta x$$

Substituting our values:

$$F_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \times 200 \times 0.10 = 10 \text{ N}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the average force.

steps.title: Determine energy stored
steps.content:
The elastic potential energy ($E_P$) stored in the spring is equal to the work done to compress it. From the data booklet, work is defined as:

$$W = Fs \cos\theta$$

In this case, the force and displacement are in the same direction ($\cos(0) = 1$), so we multiply our average force by the compression distance ($d = \Delta x$):

$$\begin{aligned} E_P = W &= F_{avg} \times \Delta x \\ E_P &= 10 \times 0.10 \\ E_P &= 1.0 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

This confirms the result from the standard formula $$E_P = \frac{1}{2} k(\Delta x)^2$$ used in the markscheme.

calculator.description: Calculate final energy.

steps.title: Understand the graphical relationship
steps.content:
In physics, the work done to compress a spring—which is stored as elastic potential energy ($E_{\text{P}}$)—is equivalent to the area under a **Force-extension ($F-x$) graph**. 

For an elastic spring, the force is proportional to the extension ($F = kx$), which creates a linear relationship starting from the origin. This means the area under the graph forms a triangle.

steps.title: Calculate the maximum force
steps.content:
First, we need to find the force required to reach the maximum compression. This will represent the **height** of our triangle on the graph.

Using Hooke's Law:
$$F = k \Delta x$$

Substituting our values:
$$\begin{aligned} F &= 200 \text{ N m}^{-1} \times 0.10 \text{ m} \\ F &= 20 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation earned the first mark (**M1**) in the markscheme by setting up the relationship for energy.

calculator.description: Calculate the maximum force (F = k * x)

steps.title: Calculate the area
steps.content:
The stored energy is the area of the triangle formed by the base (compression, $\Delta x$) and the height (force, $F$).

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$$
$$E_{\text{P}} = \frac{1}{2} \times \Delta x \times F$$

Substituting our values:
$$\begin{aligned} E_{\text{P}} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 0.10 \text{ m} \times 20 \text{ N} \\ E_{\text{P}} &= 1.0 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

This matches the formula $E_{\text{P}} = \frac{1}{2}k(\Delta x)^2$ because $F = k\Delta x$.

calculator.description: Calculate the area of the triangle (0.5 * base * height)

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The elastic potential energy stored in the spring is:
$$E_{\text{P}} = 1.0 \text{ J}$$

This final result earns the second mark (**A1**). Remember to always include units in your final answer!

parts.content: If the spring launches a $0.50 \text{ kg}$ object horizontally, determine the speed of the object after leaving the spring, assuming no energy is lost.
parts.markscheme:
- Apply conservation of energy: $E_{\text{P}}=E_{\text{K}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} k(\Delta x)^2=\frac{1}{2} m v^2$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Rearrange for velocity: $v=\sqrt{\frac{k(\Delta x)^2}{m}}$
- Substitute values: $v=\sqrt{\frac{(200)(0.10)^2}{0.50}}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate speed: $v=2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify energy transformation
steps.content: This problem is based on the principle of **Conservation of Energy** (found in **Topic A.3.1**). Since we are told to assume no energy is lost, all the elastic potential energy ($E_{p}$) stored in the compressed spring is converted into the kinetic energy ($E_{k}$) of the object as it is launched.

steps.title: Set up energy equation
steps.content:
Using the formulas for elastic potential energy and kinetic energy, we can equate the two states:

$$\text{Initial } E_{p} = \text{Final } E_{k}$$
$$\frac{1}{2}k(\Delta x)^2 = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

Where:
- $k$ is the spring constant ($200 \text{ N m}^{-1}$)
- $\Delta x$ is the compression ($0.10 \text{ m}$)
- $m$ is the mass ($0.50 \text{ kg}$)
- $v$ is the final speed

steps.title: Rearrange for velocity
steps.content:
To find the speed, we rearrange the equation to solve for $v$. First, notice that the $\frac{1}{2}$ cancels out from both sides:

$$\begin{aligned} k(\Delta x)^2 &= mv^2 \\ v^2 &= \frac{k(\Delta x)^2}{m} \\ v &= \sqrt{\frac{k(\Delta x)^2}{m}} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate and verify result
steps.content:
Now, substitute the given values into our equation:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{200 \times (0.10)^2}{0.50}}$$

Calculating the term inside the square root first:
$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2}{0.50}} = \sqrt{4}$$

This gives us a final speed of $2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.

gdc.instructions: In the main calculation menu, type sqrt((200 * 0.10^2) / 0.50) and press execute.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [x²] (square root), then type (200 * 0.10^2) / 0.50 and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt((200 * 0.10^2) / 0.50) into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the final speed by evaluating the square root of the energy ratio.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The speed of the object after leaving the spring is **$2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$**.

In an IB exam, you earn:
- **1 mark** for correctly equating the energy formulas ($M1$).
- **1 mark** for substituting the correct values ($M1$).
- **1 mark** for the final answer with units ($A1$).

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To solve this problem, we first list the known quantities from the question:
- Spring constant: $k = 200 \text{ N m}^{-1}$
- Compression distance: $\Delta x = 0.10 \text{ m}$
- Mass of the object: $m = 0.50 \text{ kg}$

We will use the **Work-Energy Theorem**, which states that the work done on an object is equal to its change in kinetic energy: $$W = \Delta E_k$$. Since the object starts from rest, its initial kinetic energy is zero.

steps.title: Calculate average force
steps.content:
The force exerted by a spring is not constant; it follows Hooke's Law ($F = kx$). To calculate the work done using the general work formula, we can find the **average force** exerted as the spring decompresses from $0.10 \text{ m}$ to $0 \text{ m}$.

$$\begin{aligned} F_{max} &= k \Delta x = 200 \times 0.10 = 20 \text{ N} \\ F_{min} &= 0 \text{ N} \\ F_{avg} &= \frac{F_{max} + F_{min}}{2} = \frac{20 + 0}{2} = 10 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the average force exerted by the spring.

steps.title: Calculate work done
steps.content:
Now we calculate the work done ($W$) by the spring using the average force over the displacement $s = \Delta x$. Using the formula from the data booklet:

$$W = Fs \cos \theta$$

Since the force and displacement are in the same direction, $\cos(0^\circ) = 1$.

$$W = F_{avg} \times \Delta x = 10 \times 0.10 = 1.0 \text{ J}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the average force by the distance: 10 * 0.10

ti84.instructions: Multiply the average force by the distance: 10 * 0.10

calculator.description: Calculate total work done by the spring.

steps.title: Set work equal to kinetic energy
steps.content:
According to the Work-Energy Theorem, this work transfer is entirely converted into the kinetic energy ($E_k$) of the object. Using the kinetic energy formula from the data booklet:

$$W = E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

Substitute the known values ($W = 1.0 \text{ J}$ and $m = 0.50 \text{ kg}$):

$$1.0 = \frac{1}{2}(0.50)v^2$$

steps.title: Solve for speed
steps.content:
Finally, we rearrange the equation to solve for the speed ($v$):

$$\begin{aligned} 1.0 &= 0.25 v^2 \\ v^2 &= \frac{1.0}{0.25} = 4.0 \\ v &= \sqrt{4.0} = 2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This matches the conservation of energy approach where elastic potential energy ($E_p = \frac{1}{2}k(\Delta x)^2$) is converted to kinetic energy.

calculator.description: Solve for v by taking the square root of (Work / (0.5 * mass)).

steps.title: Model the system as SHM
steps.content: When a spring-mass system is compressed and released, it undergoes **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)**. The object is launched horizontally and leaves the spring when the spring returns to its natural length (the equilibrium position). In SHM, the speed of the object is at its maximum when it passes through this equilibrium position. Therefore, the launch speed is equal to the maximum velocity, $v_{max}$, of the oscillation.

steps.title: Calculate angular frequency
steps.content:
To find the maximum velocity, we first need the angular frequency $\omega$ of the system. For a mass on a spring, the relationship between the spring constant $k$, the mass $m$, and the angular frequency is:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

Given $k = 200 \text{ N m}^{-1}$ and $m = 0.50 \text{ kg}$:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{200}{0.50}} = 20 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter sqrt(200/0.50) in the main calculation window.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [x²] (square root), then type 200 / 0.50 and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt(200/0.50) into an expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the angular frequency ω.

steps.title: Find the maximum velocity
steps.content:
In SHM, the maximum velocity is related to the amplitude $A$ (the maximum displacement from equilibrium) by the formula:

$$v_{max} = \omega A$$

The amplitude in this case is the initial compression of the spring, which is $0.10 \text{ m}$. Using the $\omega$ we calculated:

$$\begin{aligned} v_{max} &= 20 \times 0.10 \\ v_{max} &= 2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Multiply angular frequency by amplitude to find speed.

steps.title: State the final speed
steps.content:
The speed of the object after leaving the spring is $2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. 

In an exam, you can earn marks by:
1. Showing the link between potential and kinetic energy or using the SHM parameters (1 mark).
2. Correctly substituting the values into your chosen formula (1 mark).
3. Providing the final answer with correct units and significant figures (1 mark).

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.1 Conservation of energy
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Directly asks about applying conservation of energy to a pendulum and how air resistance causes mechanical energy to decrease over time (energy converted to thermal/sound).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.4 Standing waves and resonance > C.4.4 Damping effects
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.4.4 Damping effects
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Air resistance introduces damping in an oscillatory system; the question describes the effect on the motion/energy over time consistent with damping effects.

parts.content: Explain how the principle of conservation of energy applies to a pendulum swinging in the presence of air resistance, and describe how this affects the total mechanical energy of the system over time.
parts.markscheme:
- In the presence of air resistance, some of the mechanical energy (kinetic and potential) of the pendulum is converted into thermal energy and sound <marks>B1</marks>
- As a result, the total mechanical energy of the system decreases over time <marks>B1</marks>
- The pendulum gradually loses amplitude with each swing because of this energy dissipation <marks>B1</marks>
- The principle of conservation of energy still holds because the total energy (mechanical + thermal + sound) in the system remains constant <marks>B1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Identify energy types
steps.content:
In an ideal pendulum, we consider only mechanical energy, which is the sum of kinetic energy ($E_k$) and gravitational potential energy ($E_p$). This is covered in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**. 

However, in a real-world scenario with air resistance, we must account for the interaction between the pendulum bob and the air molecules.

steps.title: Explain energy conversion
steps.content:
As the pendulum swings, it does work against the air resistance (friction). This process involves an **energy transformation** where some of the pendulum's mechanical energy is converted into non-mechanical forms, specifically **thermal energy** in the air and the bob, and a small amount of **sound energy**.

$$\text{Work done against air resistance} \rightarrow \text{Thermal Energy} + \text{Sound Energy}$$

highlights.text: mechanical energy (kinetic and potential) of the pendulum is converted into thermal energy and sound

steps.title: Analyze mechanical energy loss
steps.content:
Because energy is being diverted away from the mechanical system, the **total mechanical energy** ($E_k + E_p$) of the pendulum decreases over time. 

This loss of mechanical energy is visible as **damping**: the pendulum loses amplitude with each successive swing, meaning it no longer reaches its original maximum height.

highlights.text: total mechanical energy of the system decreases over time

steps.title: Apply conservation principle
steps.content:
According to the **principle of conservation of energy**, energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed. While mechanical energy is lost from the pendulum, the **total energy of the closed system** (pendulum + surrounding air) remains constant.

$$E_{\text{total}} = (E_k + E_p) + E_{\text{thermal}} + E_{\text{sound}} = \text{constant}$$

highlights.text: total energy (mechanical + thermal + sound) in the system remains constant

steps.title: Define the Conservation Principle
steps.content:
To start, let's look at the big picture. The **principle of conservation of energy** states that energy cannot be created or destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another. 

In our pendulum system, even with air resistance, the **total energy** (the sum of mechanical energy and all other forms like heat) remains constant. If you're studying **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**, you'll remember that energy transformations are the key to understanding motion.

steps.title: Identify Non-Conservative Work
steps.content:
Using the **Work-Energy Approach**, we identify air resistance as a non-conservative force. As the pendulum bob moves, air resistance always acts in the opposite direction to the displacement. 

From the data booklet, the work done $W$ by a force $F$ over a displacement $s$ is:

$$W = Fs \cos \theta$$

Because the force of air resistance is opposite to the motion, the angle $\theta$ is $180^\circ$. Since $\cos(180^\circ) = -1$, the work done by air resistance is **negative**. This means energy is being removed from the pendulum's mechanical system.

steps.title: Analyze Mechanical Energy Loss
steps.content:
The **work-energy theorem** tells us that the work done by non-conservative forces equals the change in the system's total mechanical energy ($E_k + E_p$). 

$$\text{Work}_{nc} = \Delta E_{mechanical}$$

Since the work done by air resistance is negative, the **total mechanical energy decreases** over time. This mechanical energy doesn't just vanish; it is transformed into **thermal energy** (heating up the bob and the surrounding air) and a small amount of **sound energy**.

steps.title: Observe the Physical Effect
steps.content:
As the mechanical energy decreases, the pendulum can no longer reach its original height or speed. 

*   Because potential energy is given by $$\Delta E_p = mg\Delta h$$, a loss in energy means a lower maximum height ($h$).
*   This results in the pendulum gradually losing **amplitude** with each swing.

In summary, while the total energy of the universe remains constant, the pendulum's mechanical energy dissipates into the environment as heat and sound due to the negative work done by air resistance.

steps.title: Define the damped system
steps.content: In this scenario, the pendulum acts as a **damped harmonic oscillator**. Air resistance provides a resistive force that always opposes the direction of motion. This resistance performs work against the pendulum's motion, leading to a continuous transfer of energy away from the oscillating system.

steps.title: Explain energy dissipation
steps.content: As the pendulum swings through the air, its **mechanical energy** (the sum of kinetic energy, $E_k$, and gravitational potential energy, $E_p$) is not conserved on its own. Instead, it is converted into **thermal energy** in the surrounding air and the pendulum itself, as well as some sound energy.

highlights.text: mechanical energy (kinetic and potential) of the pendulum is converted into thermal energy and sound

steps.title: Relate amplitude and energy
steps.content:
A fundamental principle of oscillatory systems is that the total mechanical energy $E$ is proportional to the square of the amplitude $A$:

$$E \propto A^2$$

Because the system is losing mechanical energy to its surroundings, the total mechanical energy decreases over time. Consequently, the **amplitude** of the pendulum's swing decreases with each successive cycle. This effect is known as damping, as explored in **Topic C.4 (Damping effects)**.

highlights.text: total mechanical energy of the system decreases over time
pendulum gradually loses amplitude with each swing because of this energy dissipation

steps.title: Apply conservation of energy
steps.content: The principle of conservation of energy (from **Topic A.3**) still applies to the system as a whole. While the mechanical energy decreases, the **total energy** of the system and its surroundings (mechanical + thermal + sound) remains **constant**. Energy is transformed from one form to another, but it is never destroyed.

highlights.text: principle of conservation of energy still holds because the total energy (mechanical + thermal + sound) in the system remains constant
